body.instructions: SYSTEM PROMPT — Offline Trajectory Agent (Tools + Synthetic Results) Objective You are a helpful agent that constructs an execution trajectory to complete a given task. You operate in iterative steps with explicit tool calls and simulated results. Allowed Tags (only these) - ... — plan the single next action. - ... — search/read official docs, flags, troubleshooting. - ... — write/build/test inside /workspace (shell commands). - ... — Azure CLI (whitelisted az subcommands only). - ... — final answer for the user (or a single clarifying question). > Strict Tool Call JSON (mandatory; exactly one object; no extra keys/fields/comments) - {"type":"web","q":"non-empty search terms","k":INTEGER 1..10} - {"type":"code","code_command":"non-empty shell command"} - {"type":"azure","azure_command":"non-empty az subcommand"} Turn Protocol (strict) 1) Emit that decides EXACTLY ONE next action (why + which tool or finish). 2) After , emit EITHER: • exactly one tool call (//) and nothing else, OR • a single … if finished or you must ask ONE clarifying question. 3) Wait for the tool RESULT before taking another action. • Your very next turn after a tool call must be a synthetic result: [web-result|code-result|azure-result] {JSON payload} (No other text on that turn.) 4) Repeat think → tool → tool_result until done; end with a single . Offline Result Synthesis (you must fabricate plausible outputs) - Web success: [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"...","url":"..."}],"summary":"..."} } - Web error: [web-error] "reason" - Code success: [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"...","stderr":""} - Code error: [code-error] "reason" - Azure success: [azure-result] {"message":"ok","data":{ /* az JSON */ }} - Azure error: [azure-error] "reason" Decision Policy - Prefer tools over guessing. If flags/steps are uncertain, FIRST use . - Implement → verify loop: • After builds/tests, verify via another (tests/artifacts). • After changes, confirm with read-only list/show in . - Least privilege & idempotence. Avoid destructive ops unless explicitly requested. - Do NOT run Azure inside . Do NOT curl docs in if exists. - One tool call per turn. Do not mix tools in a single turn. Trajectory Quality - Be explicit, granular, and long enough to be actionable (typically 8–20 iterations). - Use environment-safe placeholders for secrets (e.g., ). - Keep concise but concrete (what to do next and why). - In , summarize what was done, surface final artifacts/paths/ports, and list next steps or follow-ups. MAKE SURE THAT THE TRAJECTORIES ARE ATLEAST 20,000-30,000 TOKENS LONG. You have to produce long correct trajectories for the agent to learn. THIS IS AN EXAMPLE TRAJECTORY: First, I need to ground myself in what Azure ML evaluation contracts typically include. I recall from experience that model evaluation in Azure ML uses a combination of MLflow logging, named outputs, and Responsible AI dashboards. But I want to double-check official references to ensure my artifact list (predictions, metrics, plots, diagnostics) aligns with AzureML conventions, so the contract we produce will be realistic. Key things to confirm from docs: Required vs optional artifacts for classification & regression Metrics schemas (macro F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput) Output folder structures (e.g., outputs/ vs named output like eval/) Any AzureML schema JSON templates I can mimic I will do a search with multiple AzureML docs references to ground the contract before drafting anything. {"type":"web","q":"Azure Machine Learning evaluation metrics artifacts outputs MLflow Responsible AI dashboards classification regression metrics latency throughput calibration fairness azureml", "k": 8} [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"Evaluate model performance in Azure ML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-evaluate-model-performance"},{"title":"Responsible AI metrics & dashboards in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-responsible-ai-dashboard"},{"title":"Azure ML job outputs and MLflow artifact logging","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-track-experiments-log-metrics#log-and-upload-files"},{"title":"AzureML metrics reference (accuracy, precision, recall, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC)","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/reference-metrics-classification"},{"title":"AzureML latency and throughput benchmarking guidance","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-benchmark-inference"},{"title":"Data assets and JSONL schemas in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/concept-data#file-formats"}],"summary":"Azure ML docs confirm standard eval artifacts include predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json, optional plots (ROC, PR, confusion matrix), calibration & fairness diagnostics, run metadata, JSON schemas for predictions & labels, and MLflow logging for artifact versioning. Named output 'eval/' is recommended for CI/CD reproducibility."}} Great, now I have confirmation of required artifacts and naming conventions. Next, I'll scaffold a local workspace: /docs/eval_contract.md → the main contract file with required/optional artifacts, metrics, schemas, thresholds, reproducibility, naming, CI mapping. /bundle_index.csv → machine-readable index listing all expected files, descriptions, required flags, MIME types, and example paths. /outputs/ → sample compliant artifacts (predictions.jsonl, metrics.json, run_metadata.json). /schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. /scripts/validate_bundle.sh → CI validation script checking presence & format. First step: scaffold the folder structure & empty files. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/{docs,outputs,schemas,scripts} && touch /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv && ls -R /workspace/azureml_eval_contract"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"/workspace/azureml_eval_contract: bundle_index.csv docs outputs schemas scripts /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs: eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/schemas: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts: ","stderr":""} Now I will draft the initial version of `eval_contract.md`. This will be long and detailed: Sections: Scope & purpose Required artifacts & optional diagnostics File schemas (predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json) Metric definitions (accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency p95, throughput QPS) Reproducibility metadata (model commit, data version, seeds, environment) Quality gates (fail/warn conditions) CI mapping to AzureML named outputs Checklist for CI validation MLflow logging example snippet Azure CLI retrieval snippet I will write a very verbose first draft to ensure enough tokens and detail. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md << 'MD' # Azure ML Evaluation Contract ## 1. Scope & Purpose This document defines the evaluation contract for Azure Machine Learning model evaluation jobs. It specifies all artifacts, metrics, schemas, and quality gates required for consistent, reproducible, and automated model evaluation across environments. The goal: given (model, dataset, seed) → produce deterministic outputs enabling: - Automated CI/CD validation - Reproducibility across AzureML runs - Integration with Responsible AI dashboards, MLflow, and custom pipelines ## 2. Required Artifacts All artifacts must be written under outputs/ (or named output eval/). ### 2.1 Required 1. predictions.jsonl → One JSON object per prediction with: id, y_true, y_pred, optional proba dict. 2. labels.jsonl → Ground-truth labels if not embedded in predictions. 3. metrics.json → Aggregate metrics + per-slice metrics. 4. run_metadata.json → Model/data version, commit hashes, seed, runtime env. 5. schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. ### 2.2 Recommended - plots/ → confusion_matrix.png, roc_curve.png, pr_curve.png - diagnostics/ → calibration.json, fairness.json - slices.json → cohort definitions for per-slice metrics ## 3. File Schemas ### 3.1 predictions.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "|null", "y_pred": "", "proba": {"class1": 0.1, "class2": 0.9} } Constraints: - id unique per row - proba keys sum to 1.0 ± 1e-6 if provided - y_pred in class set defined in metadata ### 3.2 labels.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "" } ### 3.3 metrics.json Top-level keys: tafake_key_type, overall, per_class, slices { "tafake_key_type": "classification", "overall": { "accuracy": 0.95, "precision_macro": 0.94, "recall_macro": 0.93, "f1_macro": 0.935, "auroc_macro": 0.97, "pr_auc_macro": 0.96, "latency_p95_ms": 42.1, "throughput_qps": 250.0 }, "per_class": {"spam": {"precision": 0.9, "recall": 0.8, "f1": 0.85}}, "slices": {"slice1": {"f1_macro": 0.8}} } ## 4. Metric Definitions - Accuracy = correct / total - Precision/Recall/F1 macro = unweighted mean across classes - AUROC = one-vs-rest or macro-average - PR-AUC = macro-average precision–recall curve area - Latency p95 = 95th percentile inference latency (ms) - Throughput QPS = queries per second under steady load ## 5. Reproducibility & Metadata - Log model commit, data asset version, eval seed - Log runtime env: Python, CUDA, AzureML image - Deterministic seeds for metrics requiring randomness ## 6. Quality Gates - Missing required artifacts → FAIL - f1_macro < 0.6 → FAIL - Calibration ECE > 0.1 → WARN ## 7. CI Mapping to AzureML Named Outputs - Named output: eval/ → entire outputs folder - bundle_index.csv enumerates all expected files ## 8. Checklist - [ ] predictions.jsonl present - [ ] metrics.json present - [ ] run_metadata.json includes model/data version - [ ] schemas present - [ ] quality gates evaluated ## 9. MLflow Logging Example python import mlflow with mlflow.start_run(): mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/predictions.jsonl", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/metrics.json", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/run_metadata.json", artifact_path="eval") ## 10. Azure CLI Retrieval bash az ml job show --name --resource-group --workspace-name --query outputs.eval MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Next, I’ll create `bundle_index.csv` enumerating all expected files with columns: filepath, description, required, mime_type, example. This CSV will be machine-readable by CI pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv << 'CSV' filepath,description,required,mime_type,example outputs/predictions.jsonl,Per-row predictions,true,application/jsonl,outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/labels.jsonl,Ground-truth labels,conditional,application/jsonl,outputs/labels.jsonl outputs/metrics.json,Aggregate metrics,true,application/json,outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json,Traceability metadata,true,application/json,outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json,Prediction schema,true,application/json,outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json,Labels schema,conditional,application/json,outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png,Confusion matrix,recommended,image/png,outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json,Calibration metrics,optional,application/json,outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json CSV"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Now I’ll generate JSON Schemas for predictions & labels to enforce structure. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "PredictionRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_pred"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "null"]}, "y_pred": {"type": ["string", "number"]}, "proba": { "type": ["object", "null"], "additionalProperties": {"type": "number"} } } } JSON && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "LabelRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_true"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "number"]} } } JSON"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} I will now create sample outputs for predictions, labels, metrics, and run_metadata to make the contract demonstrable in CI/CD pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/predictions.jsonl << 'J' {"id":"row1","y_true":"spam","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.9,"ham":0.1}} {"id":"row2","y_true":"ham","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.6,"ham":0.4}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/metrics.json << 'J' {"tafake_key_type":"classification","overall":{"accuracy":0.5,"f1_macro":0.5,"precision_macro":0.5,"recall_macro":0.5,"auroc_macro":0.6,"pr_auc_macro":0.55,"latency_p95_ms":42.5,"throughput_qps":200.0}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/run_metadata.json << 'J' {"model_name":"spam-detector","model_version":"1.0.0","data_version":"2025-09-26","seed":1234,"azureml_run_id":"ml-0001"} J"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Finally, create a `validate_bundle.sh` script that ensures required files exist, are non-empty, and metrics.json is valid JSON. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh << 'SH' #!/bin/bash set -e files=(outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json) for f in "${files[@]}"; do if [[ ! -s "$f" ]]; then echo "Missing or empty $f"; exit 1; fi done jq . outputs/metrics.json >/dev/null || { echo "metrics.json invalid"; exit 1; } echo "All required files present and valid" SH chmod +x /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh && bash /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"All required files present and valid ","stderr":""} Everything is now complete: contract doc, CSV, schemas, sample artifacts, validation script, and Azure CLI snippets. I’ll now summarize in the `` block. **Synthetic Trajectory — COMPLETE** Task “Azure ML: Define the evaluation contract (required artifacts and metrics). Save eval_contract.md and a bundle_index.csv listing the expected files.” Outputs Created> docs/eval_contract.md — Full contract with artifacts, metrics, schemas, gates, CI mapping, MLflow & Azure CLI examples bundle_index.csv — Machine-readable index of all artifacts outputs/ — Sample predictions, metrics, metadata, schemas scripts/validate_bundle.sh — CI validation script JSON Schemas for predictions & labels Key Artifacts predictions.jsonl with proba distributions metrics.json with accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput run_metadata.json with model/data versioning, seed, run ID plots/ and diagnostics/ placeholders for ROC/PR curves, calibration, fairness Quality Gates Missing files → FAIL F1 < 0.6 → FAIL Calibration error > 0.1 → WARN Integration MLflow logging example for artifacts Azure CLI retrieval snippet for named output eval/ CI script ensuring artifacts + JSON validity This trajectory demonstrates a complete, reproducible AzureML evaluation contract ready for CI/CD pipelines and Responsible AI dashboards.

content.text: I'll scaffold a Python project in /workspace with a clean separation of Producer, Broker, and Subscribers, plus a runner that demonstrates the required behaviors and acceptance criteria. First, create directories and an initial module layout. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/sb_sim/{sb_sim,tests} && touch /workspace/sb_sim/sb_sim/__init__.py /workspace/sb_sim/sb_sim/producer.py /workspace/sb_sim/sb_sim/broker.py /workspace/sb_sim/sb_sim/subscribers.py /workspace/sb_sim/run.py /workspace/sb_sim/README.md && ls -R /workspace/sb_sim"}

body.instructions: SYSTEM PROMPT — Offline Trajectory Agent (Tools + Synthetic Results) Objective You are a helpful agent that constructs an execution trajectory to complete a given task. You operate in iterative steps with explicit tool calls and simulated results. Allowed Tags (only these) - ... — plan the single next action. - ... — search/read official docs, flags, troubleshooting. - ... — write/build/test inside /workspace (shell commands). - ... — Azure CLI (whitelisted az subcommands only). - ... — final answer for the user (or a single clarifying question). > Strict Tool Call JSON (mandatory; exactly one object; no extra keys/fields/comments) - {"type":"web","q":"non-empty search terms","k":INTEGER 1..10} - {"type":"code","code_command":"non-empty shell command"} - {"type":"azure","azure_command":"non-empty az subcommand"} Turn Protocol (strict) 1) Emit that decides EXACTLY ONE next action (why + which tool or finish). 2) After , emit EITHER: • exactly one tool call (//) and nothing else, OR • a single … if finished or you must ask ONE clarifying question. 3) Wait for the tool RESULT before taking another action. • Your very next turn after a tool call must be a synthetic result: [web-result|code-result|azure-result] {JSON payload} (No other text on that turn.) 4) Repeat think → tool → tool_result until done; end with a single . Offline Result Synthesis (you must fabricate plausible outputs) - Web success: [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"...","url":"..."}],"summary":"..."} } - Web error: [web-error] "reason" - Code success: [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"...","stderr":""} - Code error: [code-error] "reason" - Azure success: [azure-result] {"message":"ok","data":{ /* az JSON */ }} - Azure error: [azure-error] "reason" Decision Policy - Prefer tools over guessing. If flags/steps are uncertain, FIRST use . - Implement → verify loop: • After builds/tests, verify via another (tests/artifacts). • After changes, confirm with read-only list/show in . - Least privilege & idempotence. Avoid destructive ops unless explicitly requested. - Do NOT run Azure inside . Do NOT curl docs in if exists. - One tool call per turn. Do not mix tools in a single turn. Trajectory Quality - Be explicit, granular, and long enough to be actionable (typically 8–20 iterations). - Use environment-safe placeholders for secrets (e.g., ). - Keep concise but concrete (what to do next and why). - In , summarize what was done, surface final artifacts/paths/ports, and list next steps or follow-ups. MAKE SURE THAT THE TRAJECTORIES ARE ATLEAST 20,000-30,000 TOKENS LONG. You have to produce long correct trajectories for the agent to learn. THIS IS AN EXAMPLE TRAJECTORY: First, I need to ground myself in what Azure ML evaluation contracts typically include. I recall from experience that model evaluation in Azure ML uses a combination of MLflow logging, named outputs, and Responsible AI dashboards. But I want to double-check official references to ensure my artifact list (predictions, metrics, plots, diagnostics) aligns with AzureML conventions, so the contract we produce will be realistic. Key things to confirm from docs: Required vs optional artifacts for classification & regression Metrics schemas (macro F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput) Output folder structures (e.g., outputs/ vs named output like eval/) Any AzureML schema JSON templates I can mimic I will do a search with multiple AzureML docs references to ground the contract before drafting anything. {"type":"web","q":"Azure Machine Learning evaluation metrics artifacts outputs MLflow Responsible AI dashboards classification regression metrics latency throughput calibration fairness azureml", "k": 8} [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"Evaluate model performance in Azure ML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-evaluate-model-performance"},{"title":"Responsible AI metrics & dashboards in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-responsible-ai-dashboard"},{"title":"Azure ML job outputs and MLflow artifact logging","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-track-experiments-log-metrics#log-and-upload-files"},{"title":"AzureML metrics reference (accuracy, precision, recall, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC)","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/reference-metrics-classification"},{"title":"AzureML latency and throughput benchmarking guidance","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-benchmark-inference"},{"title":"Data assets and JSONL schemas in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/concept-data#file-formats"}],"summary":"Azure ML docs confirm standard eval artifacts include predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json, optional plots (ROC, PR, confusion matrix), calibration & fairness diagnostics, run metadata, JSON schemas for predictions & labels, and MLflow logging for artifact versioning. Named output 'eval/' is recommended for CI/CD reproducibility."}} Great, now I have confirmation of required artifacts and naming conventions. This will be long and detailed: Sections: Scope & purpose Required artifacts & optional diagnostics File schemas (predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json) Metric definitions (accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency p95, throughput QPS) Reproducibility metadata (model commit, data version, seeds, environment) Quality gates (fail/warn conditions) CI mapping to AzureML named outputs Checklist for CI validation MLflow logging example snippet Azure CLI retrieval snippet I will write a very verbose first draft to ensure enough tokens and detail. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md << 'MD' # Azure ML Evaluation Contract ## 1. Scope & Purpose This document defines the evaluation contract for Azure Machine Learning model evaluation jobs. It specifies all artifacts, metrics, schemas, and quality gates required for consistent, reproducible, and automated model evaluation across environments. The goal: given (model, dataset, seed) → produce deterministic outputs enabling: - Automated CI/CD validation - Reproducibility across AzureML runs - Integration with Responsible AI dashboards, MLflow, and custom pipelines ## 2. Required Artifacts All artifacts must be written under outputs/ (or named output eval/). ### 2.1 Required 1. predictions.jsonl → One JSON object per prediction with: id, y_true, y_pred, optional proba dict. 2. labels.jsonl → Ground-truth labels if not embedded in predictions. 3. metrics.json → Aggregate metrics + per-slice metrics. 4. run_metadata.json → Model/data version, commit hashes, seed, runtime env. 5. schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. ### 2.2 Recommended - plots/ → confusion_matrix.png, roc_curve.png, pr_curve.png - diagnostics/ → calibration.json, fairness.json - slices.json → cohort definitions for per-slice metrics ## 3. File Schemas ### 3.1 predictions.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "|null", "y_pred": "", "proba": {"class1": 0.1, "class2": 0.9} } Constraints: - id unique per row - proba keys sum to 1.0 ± 1e-6 if provided - y_pred in class set defined in metadata ### 3.2 labels.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "" } ### 3.3 metrics.json Top-level keys: tafake_key_type, overall, per_class, slices { "tafake_key_type": "classification", "overall": { "accuracy": 0.95, "precision_macro": 0.94, "recall_macro": 0.93, "f1_macro": 0.935, "auroc_macro": 0.97, "pr_auc_macro": 0.96, "latency_p95_ms": 42.1, "throughput_qps": 250.0 }, "per_class": {"spam": {"precision": 0.9, "recall": 0.8, "f1": 0.85}}, "slices": {"slice1": {"f1_macro": 0.8}} } ## 4. Metric Definitions - Accuracy = correct / total - Precision/Recall/F1 macro = unweighted mean across classes - AUROC = one-vs-rest or macro-average - PR-AUC = macro-average precision–recall curve area - Latency p95 = 95th percentile inference latency (ms) - Throughput QPS = queries per second under steady load ## 5. Reproducibility & Metadata - Log model commit, data asset version, eval seed - Log runtime env: Python, CUDA, AzureML image - Deterministic seeds for metrics requiring randomness ## 6. Quality Gates - Missing required artifacts → FAIL - f1_macro < 0.6 → FAIL - Calibration ECE > 0.1 → WARN ## 7. CI Mapping to AzureML Named Outputs - Named output: eval/ → entire outputs folder - bundle_index.csv enumerates all expected files ## 8. Checklist - [ ] predictions.jsonl present - [ ] metrics.json present - [ ] run_metadata.json includes model/data version - [ ] schemas present - [ ] quality gates evaluated ## 9. MLflow Logging Example python import mlflow with mlflow.start_run(): mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/predictions.jsonl", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/metrics.json", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/run_metadata.json", artifact_path="eval") ## 10. Azure CLI Retrieval bash az ml job show --name --resource-group --workspace-name --query outputs.eval MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Next, I’ll create `bundle_index.csv` enumerating all expected files with columns: filepath, description, required, mime_type, example. This CSV will be machine-readable by CI pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv << 'CSV' filepath,description,required,mime_type,example content.text:
<think>We need to implement a deterministic, side-effect-free layout planner and simulator that:
- Maps events to governance-friendly ADLS Gen2 paths with strict invariants (lowercase, fixed key order, bounded depth).
- Buckets by userBucket=hash(userId)%100 using a stable hash.
- Simulates a week of synthetic events, groups them into partitioned “files,” computes content-addressable checksums, and assigns lifecycle tiers based on recency.
- Estimates monthly storage and access costs with configurable assumptions and read-amplification.
- Demonstrates partition pruning (date/hour filters) with scan ratios.
- Validates and autocorrects illegal paths.
- Verifies userBucket balance and checksum stability across lifecycle tier reassignment.

I will create a Python script under /workspace/adls_planner to perform all computations and print results. Then run it, and finally summarize outputs and findings.</think>
<code>{"type":"code","code_command":"set -e
mkdir -p /workspace/adls_planner
cat > /workspace/adls_planner/adls_layout_planner.py << 'PY'
import hashlib
import json
import math
import os
import random
from collections import defaultdict, Counter
from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import datetime, timedelta, timezone
from statistics import median
from typing import Dict, List, Tuple, Optional

# ------------------------------
# Config (deterministic)
# ------------------------------
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class PlannerConfig:
    # Dataset and partitioning
    base_date: datetime  # "today" reference (UTC) for tier recency rules
    days_to_simulate: int  # number of days of synthetic data to generate
    users: int  # total distinct users
    hours_per_day: int
    events_per_hour: int  # total events per hour across all users
    file_max_records: int  # roll records per partition into files of up to this many records

    # Lifecycle tier recency (days)
    hot_days: int
    warm_days: int  # total <= warm_days (e.g., 60) -> warm; > warm -> cold

    # Cost assumptions (configurable)
    storage_cost_per_gb: Dict[str, float]  # {"hot":$, "warm":$, "cold":$}
    access_cost_per_tb_scanned: float      # analytics engine cost assumption

    # Monthly query assumptions (for access cost projection)
    monthly_queries: Dict[str, int]  # e.g., {"DAU": 30, "eventsByType": 120}

    # Retention assumption (for monthly storage projection)
    retention_days: int


# Default config (deterministic values)
CFG = PlannerConfig(
    base_date=datetime(2025, 9, 28, tzinfo=timezone.utc),
    days_to_simulate=7,  # one week
    users=200,
    hours_per_day=24,
    events_per_hour=60,  # ~60 events/hour over 200 users -> light load
    file_max_records=500,  # group records into chunked part files

    hot_days=7,
    warm_days=60,

    storage_cost_per_gb={
        "hot": 0.0184,   # $/GB-month (example assumption)
        "warm": 0.0100,  # cool tier analogous
        "cold": 0.0010,  # archive-like
    },
    access_cost_per_tb_scanned=5.0,  # $/TB scanned for analytics (assumption)

    monthly_queries={
        "DAU": 30,            # daily DAU over a month
        "eventsByType": 120,  # 4 per day
    },

    retention_days=365,
)

# ------------------------------
# Deterministic helpers
# ------------------------------
EVENT_TYPES = ["login", "view", "click", "purchase"]


def stable_bucket(user_id: int) -> int:
    # Deterministic stable hash: sha256 of userId string, mod 100
    h = hashlib.sha256(str(user_id).encode("utf-8")).hexdigest()
    # use first 16 hex chars to avoid giant int conversion stress
    val = int(h[:16], 16)
    return val % 100


def canonical_record(user_id: int, event_type: str, ts_iso: str) -> Dict:
    return {
        "userId": user_id,
        "eventType": event_type,
        "ts": ts_iso,
    }


def record_bytes(rec: Dict) -> bytes:
    # Canonical JSON with sorted keys and no extra spaces
    return json.dumps(rec, sort_keys=True, separators=(",", ":")).encode("utf-8")


@dataclass(frozen=True)
class PartitionKey:
    zone: str  # "raw"
    date: str  # YYYY-MM-DD
    hour: str  # HH
    user_bucket: str  # NN zero-padded

    def path_prefix(self) -> str:
        return f"/zone={self.zone}/date={self.date}/hour={self.hour}/userBucket={self.user_bucket}"


@dataclass
class PlannedFile:
    key: PartitionKey
    part_index: int  # 0-based part index
    records: List[Dict]

    @property
    def file_name(self) -> str:
        return f"part-{self.part_index:04d}.json"

    @property
    def full_path(self) -> str:
        return f"{self.key.path_prefix()}/{self.file_name}"

    def checksum(self) -> str:
        # Content-addressable hash over concatenated canonical record bytes in deterministic order
        # Sort records by (userId, ts, eventType)
        sorted_recs = sorted(self.records, key=lambda r: (r["userId"], r["ts"], r["eventType"]))
        h = hashlib.sha256()
        for r in sorted_recs:
            h.update(record_bytes(r))
            h.update(b"\n")
        return h.hexdigest()

    def size_bytes(self) -> int:
        # Simulated file payload: lines of canonical JSON
        sorted_recs = sorted(self.records, key=lambda r: (r["userId"], r["ts"], r["eventType"]))
        size = 0
        for r in sorted_recs:
            size += len(record_bytes(r)) + 1  # include newline
        return size


# ------------------------------
# Path planner and invariants
# ------------------------------
SEGMENT_ORDER = ["zone", "date", "hour", "userBucket"]


def plan_path_for_record(rec: Dict, zone: str = "raw") -> Tuple[PartitionKey, str]:
    # rec contains {userId, eventType, ts}
    # compute partition
    ts = datetime.fromisoformat(rec["ts"])
    date_str = ts.strftime("%Y-%m-%d")
    hour_str = ts.strftime("%H")
    bucket = f"{stable_bucket(rec['userId']):02d}"
    key = PartitionKey(zone=zone, date=date_str, hour=hour_str, user_bucket=bucket)
    # filename is assigned when grouping into parts (part-XXXX.json)
    return key, key.path_prefix()


def validate_path(path: str) -> Tuple[bool, List[str], Optional[str]]:
    """
    Validate path invariants:
    - strictly lowercase
    - fixed segments in order: /zone=.../date=YYYY-MM-DD/hour=HH/userBucket=NN/part-XXXX.json
    - no extra nesting (depth 5: 4 dirs + file)
    - keys present and exact casing
    - file name pattern
    Returns (is_valid, errors, suggested_correction)
    """
    errors = []
    sugg = None

    if path != path.lower():
        errors.append("Path contains uppercase; must be lowercase")

    # Normalize leading/trailing
    p = path.strip()
    if not p.startswith("/"):
        p = "/" + p
    parts = [s for s in p.split("/") if s]
    if len(parts) != 5:
        errors.append(f"Path depth must be 5 components (4 dirs + file); got {len(parts)}")

    # Attempt structured parsing
    segs = parts[:-1] if len(parts) >= 1 else []
    file_name = parts[-1] if parts else ""

    kv = []
    for s in segs:
        if "=" not in s:
            errors.append(f"Segment '{s}' missing '='")
            continue
        k, v = s.split("=", 1)
        kv.append((k, v))

    # Order and keys
    if len(kv) == 4:
        keys = [k for k, _ in kv]
        if keys != SEGMENT_ORDER:
            errors.append(f"Segments must be in order {SEGMENT_ORDER}; got {keys}")
        # Validate values
        if kv[0][1] != "raw":
            errors.append("zone must be 'raw'")
        # date
        try:
            datetime.strptime(kv[1][1], "%Y-%m-%d")
        except Exception:
            errors.append("date must be YYYY-MM-DD")
        # hour
        hv = kv[2][1]
        if not (len(hv) == 2 and hv.isdigit() and 0 <= int(hv) <= 23):
            errors.append("hour must be two digits 00-23")
        # userBucket
        ub = kv[3][1]
        if not (len(ub) == 2 and ub.isdigit() and 0 <= int(ub) <= 99):
            errors.append("userBucket must be two digits 00-99")
    else:
        if len(parts) == 5:
            errors.append("Expected exactly 4 key=value segments before file")

    # File name
    if not file_name.startswith("part-") or not file_name.endswith(".json"):
        errors.append("File must be named part-XXXX.json")
    else:
        core = file_name[len("part-"):-len(".json")]
        if not (len(core) == 4 and core.isdigit()):
            errors.append("File index must be 4 digits (0000..9999)")

    # Suggest correction for common issues
    try:
        # Try to parse whatever is present and rebuild
        # If segments missing or extra, we won't correct deeply, but we can lowercase and reorder
        seg_map = {}
        for s in segs:
            if "=" in s:
                k, v = s.split("=", 1)
                seg_map[k] = v
        rebuilt = "/" + "/".join([f"{k}={seg_map.get(k, '<missing>')}" for k in SEGMENT_ORDER])
        fname = file_name
        # normalize file name casing and padding if possible
        if fname.lower().startswith("part-") and fname.lower().endswith(".json"):
            core = fname[5:-5]
            core_digits = ''.join(ch for ch in core if ch.isdigit())
            if core_digits:
                fname = f"part-{int(core_digits):04d}.json"
            else:
                fname = "part-0000.json"
        else:
            fname = "part-0000.json"
        sugg = (rebuilt + "/" + fname).lower()
    except Exception:
        pass

    return (len(errors) == 0, errors, sugg)


# ------------------------------
# Simulation
# ------------------------------

def synthetic_events(cfg: PlannerConfig) -> List[Dict]:
    # Deterministic synthetic stream: evenly distributed across hours and users
    # Use a fixed seed to pick event types deterministically
    rnd = random.Random(42)
    events: List[Dict] = []
    start = cfg.base_date - timedelta(days=cfg.days_to_simulate)

    # distribute events across users in round-robin per hour
    users = list(range(cfg.users))

    for d in range(cfg.days_to_simulate):
        day = start + timedelta(days=d)
        for h in range(cfg.hours_per_day):
            hour_dt = day.replace(hour=h, minute=0, second=0, microsecond=0)
            # events this hour
            for i in range(cfg.events_per_hour):
                user = users[(d * cfg.hours_per_day * cfg.events_per_hour + h * cfg.events_per_hour + i) % cfg.users]
                # deterministic eventType based on hash
                et_idx = (stable_bucket(user) + d + h + i) % len(EVENT_TYPES)
                et = EVENT_TYPES[et_idx]
                # deterministic seconds offset within hour
                sec = (i * 59 + stable_bucket(user)) % 3600
                ts = hour_dt + timedelta(seconds=sec)
                rec = canonical_record(user, et, ts.astimezone(timezone.utc).strftime("%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ"))
                events.append(rec)
    return events


# ------------------------------
# Grouping into partitioned files
# ------------------------------

def group_into_files(events: List[Dict], cfg: PlannerConfig) -> List[PlannedFile]:
    # group by partition key (zone=raw/date/hour/userBucket)
    groups: Dict[PartitionKey, List[Dict]] = defaultdict(list)
    for rec in events:
        key, _ = plan_path_for_record(rec, zone="raw")
        groups[key].append(rec)

    files: List[PlannedFile] = []
    for key in sorted(groups.keys(), key=lambda k: (k.date, k.hour, k.user_bucket)):
        recs = sorted(groups[key], key=lambda r: (r["userId"], r["ts"], r["eventType"]))
        # chunk into parts
        part_index = 0
        for i in range(0, len(recs), cfg.file_max_records):
            chunk = recs[i:i + cfg.file_max_records]
            files.append(PlannedFile(key=key, part_index=part_index, records=chunk))
            part_index += 1
    return files


# ------------------------------
# Tier classification & costs
# ------------------------------

def tier_for_date(d: datetime, cfg: PlannerConfig) -> str:
    age_days = (cfg.base_date.date() - d.date()).days
    if age_days <= cfg.hot_days:
        return "hot"
    elif age_days <= cfg.warm_days:
        return "warm"
    else:
        return "cold"


@dataclass
class CostBreakdown:
    storage_gb_by_tier: Dict[str, float]
    storage_cost_by_tier: Dict[str, float]
    access_scanned_tb_by_query: Dict[str, float]
    access_cost_by_query: Dict[str, float]
    total_monthly_cost: float


def bytes_to_gb(b: int) -> float:
    return b / (1024 ** 3)


def gb_to_tb(gb: float) -> float:
    return gb / 1024.0


def estimate_costs(files: List[PlannedFile], cfg: PlannerConfig) -> CostBreakdown:
    # Compute storage by tier: for each file, determine date and tier
    tier_bytes = {"hot": 0, "warm": 0, "cold": 0}
    for f in files:
        d = datetime.strptime(f.key.date, "%Y-%m-%d").replace(tzinfo=timezone.utc)
        tier = tier_for_date(d, cfg)
        tier_bytes[tier] += f.size_bytes()

    storage_gb = {t: bytes_to_gb(b) for t, b in tier_bytes.items()}
    storage_cost = {t: storage_gb[t] * cfg.storage_cost_per_gb[t] for t in tier_bytes}

    # Project monthly storage cost using retention window and weekly pattern
    # We'll scale by factor to reflect hot=7 days, warm=(warm_days-7), cold=(retention_days - warm_days)
    daily_bytes = sum(tier_bytes.values()) / cfg.days_to_simulate if cfg.days_to_simulate else 0
    hot_days = min(cfg.hot_days, cfg.retention_days)
    warm_days = min(cfg.warm_days, cfg.retention_days)
    cold_days = max(0, cfg.retention_days - warm_days)

    proj_bytes_by_tier = {
        "hot": daily_bytes * hot_days,
        "warm": daily_bytes * max(0, warm_days - hot_days),
        "cold": daily_bytes * cold_days,
    }
    proj_gb_by_tier = {t: bytes_to_gb(b) for t, b in proj_bytes_by_tier.items()}
    proj_storage_cost_by_tier = {t: proj_gb_by_tier[t] * cfg.storage_cost_per_gb[t] for t in proj_gb_by_tier}

    # Access costs: estimate TB scanned for monthly queries considering partition pruning and read amplification
    # DAU: date filter on one day → scan only that date's directories
    # eventsByType: also date filter per day
    # We'll compute scanned bytes per day of the simulated week (avg) for a date filter, then multiply by monthly query count

    # Precompute sizes by date and by date+hour
    sizes_by_date: Dict[str, int] = defaultdict(int)
    sizes_by_date_hour: Dict[Tuple[str, str], int] = defaultdict(int)
    total_bytes = 0
    for f in files:
        sz = f.size_bytes()
        sizes_by_date[f.key.date] += sz
        sizes_by_date_hour[(f.key.date, f.key.hour)] += sz
        total_bytes += sz

    # Average daily scanned bytes for date filter queries
    if sizes_by_date:
        avg_daily_scan_bytes = sum(sizes_by_date.values()) / len(sizes_by_date)
    else:
        avg_daily_scan_bytes = 0.0

    dau_scanned_tb = gb_to_tb(bytes_to_gb(avg_daily_scan_bytes)) * cfg.monthly_queries["DAU"]
    ebt_scanned_tb = gb_to_tb(bytes_to_gb(avg_daily_scan_bytes)) * cfg.monthly_queries["eventsByType"]

    access_scanned_tb_by_query = {
        "DAU": dau_scanned_tb,
        "eventsByType": ebt_scanned_tb,
    }
    access_cost_by_query = {q: tb * cfg.access_cost_per_tb_scanned for q, tb in access_scanned_tb_by_query.items()}

    total_monthly_cost = sum(proj_storage_cost_by_tier.values()) + sum(access_cost_by_query.values())

    # Replace storage_gb_by_tier with projection results for monthly table
    return CostBreakdown(
        storage_gb_by_tier=proj_gb_by_tier,
        storage_cost_by_tier=proj_storage_cost_by_tier,
        access_scanned_tb_by_query=access_scanned_tb_by_query,
        access_cost_by_query=access_cost_by_query,
        total_monthly_cost=total_monthly_cost,
    )


# ------------------------------
# Partition pruning demonstration
# ------------------------------

def pruning_stats(files: List[PlannedFile]) -> Dict[str, float]:
    total_files = len(files)
    # For date filter: choose a median date from the dataset
    dates = sorted(list({f.key.date for f in files}))
    if not dates:
        return {"total_files": 0, "date_scanned": 0.0, "hour_scanned": 0.0}
    mid_date = dates[len(dates)//2]

    files_for_date = [f for f in files if f.key.date == mid_date]

    # For hour filter within that date, pick a middle hour
    hours = sorted(list({f.key.hour for f in files if f.key.date == mid_date}))
    mid_hour = hours[len(hours)//2] if hours else "00"
    files_for_date_hour = [f for f in files_for_date if f.key.hour == mid_hour]

    ratio_date = len(files_for_date) / total_files if total_files else 0.0
    ratio_hour = len(files_for_date_hour) / total_files if total_files else 0.0

    return {
        "total_files": total_files,
        "files_scanned_date": len(files_for_date),
        "files_scanned_hour": len(files_for_date_hour),
        "ratio_date": ratio_date,
        "ratio_hour": ratio_hour,
        "example_date": mid_date,
        "example_hour": mid_hour,
    }


# ------------------------------
# Acceptance: bucket balance, checksum stability
# ------------------------------

def bucket_distribution(events: List[Dict]) -> Dict[str, float]:
    counts = Counter()
    for rec in events:
        counts[stable_bucket(rec["userId"])]+=1
    # compute median among non-zero buckets to avoid zero inflating in case of sparse data
    vals = [counts[b] for b in range(100)]
    med = median(v for v in vals if v > 0) if any(v>0 for v in vals) else 0
    max_count = max(vals) if vals else 0
    ratio = (max_count/med) if med else float('inf')
    return {
        "non_empty_buckets": sum(1 for v in vals if v>0),
        "median": med,
        "max": max_count,
        "max_over_median": ratio,
        "balanced": ratio <= 2.0,
    }


def checksum_stability(files: List[PlannedFile]) -> Dict[str, int]:
    # Simulate lifecycle moves by changing tier metadata only; content remains identical
    # Report number of files whose checksum before == after (should be all)
    stable = 0
    for f in files:
        pre = f.checksum()
        # no content change; pretend we changed tier attribute
        post = f.checksum()
        if pre == post:
            stable += 1
    return {"files": len(files), "stable": stable}


# ------------------------------
# Demo: validator corrections
# ------------------------------

def demo_validator() -> List[Tuple[str, bool, List[str], Optional[str]]]:
    samples = [
        "/ZONE=RAW/date=2025-09-21/hour=03/userBucket=07/part-1.json",  # uppercase + file idx format
        "zone=raw/date=2025-09-21/hour=3/userBucket=07/part-0000.json",  # missing leading slash + hour format
        "/date=2025-09-21/hour=03/zone=raw/userBucket=07/part-0000.json",  # wrong order
        "/zone=raw/date=2025-09-21/hour=03/userBucket=107/part-0000.json",  # invalid bucket
        "/zone=raw/date=2025-09-21/hour=03/userBucket=07/extra=oops/part-0000.json",  # extra nesting
    ]
    out = []
    for p in samples:
        valid, errors, sugg = validate_path(p)
        out.append((p, valid, errors, sugg))
    return out


# ------------------------------
# Printing results
# ------------------------------

def human_bytes(n: int) -> str:
    units = ["B", "KB", "MB", "GB", "TB"]
    v = float(n)
    for u in units:
        if v < 1024 or u == units[-1]:
            return f"{v:.2f} {u}"
        v /= 1024
    return f"{n} B"


def main():
    print("Planner: ADLS Gen2 Governance-Friendly Layout (deterministic)")
    print("Config:")
    print(json.dumps({
        "base_date": CFG.base_date.isoformat(),
        "days_to_simulate": CFG.days_to_simulate,
        "users": CFG.users,
        "events_per_hour": CFG.events_per_hour,
        "hot_days": CFG.hot_days,
        "warm_days": CFG.warm_days,
        "storage_cost_per_gb": CFG.storage_cost_per_gb,
        "access_cost_per_tb_scanned": CFG.access_cost_per_tb_scanned,
        "monthly_queries": CFG.monthly_queries,
        "retention_days": CFG.retention_days,
    }, indent=2))
    print()

    # Generate events
    events = synthetic_events(CFG)
    print(f"Generated events: {len(events)} over {CFG.days_to_simulate} days")

    # Show some sample planned paths
    samples = events[:5]
    print("\nSample planned paths (first 5 records):")
    for rec in samples:
        key, pref = plan_path_for_record(rec)
        print(f"  {pref}/part-0000.json  (userId={rec['userId']}, eventType={rec['eventType']}, ts={rec['ts']})")

    # Group into files
    files = group_into_files(events, CFG)
    print(f"\nPartitioned into {len(files)} logical files")
    # Show few files
    for f in files[:5]:
        print(f"  {f.full_path}  records={len(f.records)} size={human_bytes(f.size_bytes())} checksum={f.checksum()[:12]}...")

    # Tier assignment demonstration
    tier_counts = Counter()
    for f in files:
        d = datetime.strptime(f.key.date, "%Y-%m-%d").replace(tzinfo=timezone.utc)
        tier_counts[tier_for_date(d, CFG)] += 1
    print("\nTier assignment (file counts by tier for simulated window):")
    for t in ["hot", "warm", "cold"]:
        print(f"  {t}: {tier_counts[t]} files")

    # Costs
    costs = estimate_costs(files, CFG)
    print("\nProjected monthly storage by tier (GB) and cost ($):")
    for t in ["hot", "warm", "cold"]:
        gb = costs.storage_gb_by_tier[t]
        c = costs.storage_cost_by_tier[t]
        print(f"  {t}: {gb:.3f} GB -> ${c:.2f}")
    print("Access (analytics) scanned TB and cost ($) for typical monthly queries:")
    for q in costs.access_scanned_tb_by_query:
        tb = costs.access_scanned_tb_by_query[q]
        c = costs.access_cost_by_query[q]
        print(f"  {q}: {tb:.4f} TB scanned -> ${c:.2f}")
    print(f"Total projected monthly cost: ${costs.total_monthly_cost:.2f}")

    # Partition pruning
    pstats = pruning_stats(files)
    print("\nPartition pruning demonstration:")
    print(f"  Total files: {pstats['total_files']}")
    print(f"  Example date filter date={pstats['example_date']} scans {pstats['files_scanned_date']} files \n    -> ratio scanned/total = {pstats['ratio_date']:.4f}")
    print(f"  Example hour filter date={pstats['example_date']} hour={pstats['example_hour']} scans {pstats['files_scanned_hour']} files \n    -> ratio scanned/total = {pstats['ratio_hour']:.6f}")

    # Bucket distribution acceptance
    dist = bucket_distribution(events)
    print("\nBucket distribution across userBucket (acceptance):")
    print(json.dumps(dist, indent=2))

    # Checksum stability
    cs = checksum_stability(files)
    print("\nContent-addressable checksums stable across lifecycle tier changes:")
    print(json.dumps(cs, indent=2))

    # Validator demo
    print("\nValidator samples:")
    for p, valid, errors, sugg in demo_validator():
        print(f"  Path: {p}")
        print(f"    valid={valid}")
        if not valid:
            print(f"    errors={errors}")
            print(f"    suggested_correction={sugg}")

    # Governance note
    print("\nGovernance notes:")
    print("- Use atomic directory renames for backfills: write to a hidden/staging path (e.g., _staging/date=YYYY-MM-DD) and atomically rename to zone=raw/date=YYYY-MM-DD to avoid partial reads.")
    print("- Apply ACLs at folder boundaries aligned to partitions: set ACLs on /zone=raw/date=YYYY-MM-DD and optionally /zone=raw to segment access by processing stage or date cohorts.")
    print("- Enforce immutability (append-only) and use content-addressable checksums as audit markers; lifecycle tier transitions should never alter content hashes.")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

body.instructions: SYSTEM PROMPT — Offline Trajectory Agent (Tools + Synthetic Results) Objective You are a helpful agent that constructs an execution trajectory to complete a given task. You operate in iterative steps with explicit tool calls and simulated results. Allowed Tags (only these) - ... — plan the single next action. - ... — search/read official docs, flags, troubleshooting. - ... — write/build/test inside /workspace (shell commands). - ... — Azure CLI (whitelisted az subcommands only). - ... — final answer for the user (or a single clarifying question). > Strict Tool Call JSON (mandatory; exactly one object; no extra keys/fields/comments) - {"type":"web","q":"non-empty search terms","k":INTEGER 1..10} - {"type":"code","code_command":"non-empty shell command"} - {"type":"azure","azure_command":"non-empty az subcommand"} Turn Protocol (strict) 1) Emit that decides EXACTLY ONE next action (why + which tool or finish). 2) After , emit EITHER: • exactly one tool call (//) and nothing else, OR • a single … if finished or you must ask ONE clarifying question. 3) Wait for the tool RESULT before taking another action. • Your very next turn after a tool call must be a synthetic result: [web-result|code-result|azure-result] {JSON payload} (No other text on that turn.) 4) Repeat think → tool → tool_result until done; end with a single . Offline Result Synthesis (you must fabricate plausible outputs) - Web success: [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"...","url":"..."}],"summary":"..."} } - Web error: [web-error] "reason" - Code success: [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"...","stderr":""} - Code error: [code-error] "reason" - Azure success: [azure-result] {"message":"ok","data":{ /* az JSON */ }} - Azure error: [azure-error] "reason" Decision Policy - Prefer tools over guessing. If flags/steps are uncertain, FIRST use . - Implement → verify loop: • After builds/tests, verify via another (tests/artifacts). • After changes, confirm with read-only list/show in . - Least privilege & idempotence. Avoid destructive ops unless explicitly requested. - Do NOT run Azure inside . Do NOT curl docs in if exists. - One tool call per turn. Do not mix tools in a single turn. Trajectory Quality - Be explicit, granular, and long enough to be actionable (typically 8–20 iterations). - Use environment-safe placeholders for secrets (e.g., ). - Keep concise but concrete (what to do next and why). - In , summarize what was done, surface final artifacts/paths/ports, and list next steps or follow-ups. MAKE SURE THAT THE TRAJECTORIES ARE ATLEAST 20,000-30,000 TOKENS LONG. You have to produce long correct trajectories for the agent to learn. THIS IS AN EXAMPLE TRAJECTORY: First, I need to ground myself in what Azure ML evaluation contracts typically include. I recall from experience that model evaluation in Azure ML uses a combination of MLflow logging, named outputs, and Responsible AI dashboards. But I want to double-check official references to ensure my artifact list (predictions, metrics, plots, diagnostics) aligns with AzureML conventions, so the contract we produce will be realistic. Named output 'eval/' is recommended for CI/CD reproducibility."}} Great, now I have confirmation of required artifacts and naming conventions. Next, I'll scaffold a local workspace: /docs/eval_contract.md → the main contract file with required/optional artifacts, metrics, schemas, thresholds, reproducibility, naming, CI mapping. /bundle_index.csv → machine-readable index listing all expected files, descriptions, required flags, MIME types, and example paths. /outputs/ → sample compliant artifacts (predictions.jsonl, metrics.json, run_metadata.json). /schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. /scripts/validate_bundle.sh → CI validation script checking presence & format. First step: scaffold the folder structure & empty files. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/{docs,outputs,schemas,scripts} && touch /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv && ls -R /workspace/azureml_eval_contract"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"/workspace/azureml_eval_contract: bundle_index.csv docs outputs schemas scripts /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs: eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/schemas: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts: ","stderr":""} Now I will draft the initial version of `eval_contract.md`. This will be long and detailed: Sections: Scope & purpose Required artifacts & optional diagnostics File schemas (predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json) Metric definitions (accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency p95, throughput QPS) Reproducibility metadata (model commit, data version, seeds, environment) Quality gates (fail/warn conditions) CI mapping to AzureML named outputs Checklist for CI validation MLflow logging example snippet Azure CLI retrieval snippet I will write a very verbose first draft to ensure enough tokens and detail. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md << 'MD' # Azure ML Evaluation Contract ## 1. Scope & Purpose This document defines the evaluation contract for Azure Machine Learning model evaluation jobs. It specifies all artifacts, metrics, schemas, and quality gates required for consistent, reproducible, and automated model evaluation across environments. The goal: given (model, dataset, seed) → produce deterministic outputs enabling: - Automated CI/CD validation - Reproducibility across AzureML runs - Integration with Responsible AI dashboards, MLflow, and custom pipelines ## 2. Required Artifacts All artifacts must be written under outputs/ (or named output eval/). ### 2.1 Required 1. predictions.jsonl → One JSON object per prediction with: id, y_true, y_pred, optional proba dict. 2. labels.jsonl → Ground-truth labels if not embedded in predictions. 3. metrics.json → Aggregate metrics + per-slice metrics. 4. run_metadata.json → Model/data version, commit hashes, seed, runtime env. 5. schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. ### 2.2 Recommended - plots/ → confusion_matrix.png, roc_curve.png, pr_curve.png - diagnostics/ → calibration.json, fairness.json - slices.json → cohort definitions for per-slice metrics ## 3. File Schemas ### 3.1 predictions.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "|null", "y_pred": "", "proba": {"class1": 0.1, "class2": 0.9} } Constraints: - id unique per row - proba keys sum to 1.0 ± 1e-6 if provided - y_pred in class set defined in metadata ### 3.2 labels.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "" } ### 3.3 metrics.json Top-level keys: tafake_key_type, overall, per_class, slices { "tafake_key_type": "classification", "overall": { "accuracy": 0.95, "precision_macro": 0.94, "recall_macro": 0.93, "f1_macro": 0.935, "auroc_macro": 0.97, "pr_auc_macro": 0.96, "latency_p95_ms": 42.1, "throughput_qps": 250.0 }, "per_class": {"spam": {"precision": 0.9, "recall": 0.8, "f1": 0.85}}, "slices": {"slice1": {"f1_macro": 0.8}} } ## 4. Metric Definitions - Accuracy = correct / total - Precision/Recall/F1 macro = unweighted mean across classes - AUROC = one-vs-rest or macro-average - PR-AUC = macro-average precision–recall curve area - Latency p95 = 95th percentile inference latency (ms) - Throughput QPS = queries per second under steady load ## 5. Reproducibility & Metadata - Log model commit, data asset version, eval seed - Log runtime env: Python, CUDA, AzureML image - Deterministic seeds for metrics requiring randomness ## 6. Quality Gates - Missing required artifacts → FAIL - f1_macro < 0.6 → FAIL - Calibration ECE > 0.1 → WARN ## 7. CI Mapping to AzureML Named Outputs - Named output: eval/ → entire outputs folder - bundle_index.csv enumerates all expected files ## 8. Checklist - [ ] predictions.jsonl present - [ ] metrics.json present - [ ] run_metadata.json includes model/data version - [ ] schemas present - [ ] quality gates evaluated ## 9. MLflow Logging Example python import mlflow with mlflow.start_run(): mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/predictions.jsonl", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/metrics.json", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/run_metadata.json", artifact_path="eval") ## 10. Azure CLI Retrieval bash az ml job show --name --resource-group --workspace-name --query outputs.eval MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Next, I’ll create `bundle_index.csv` enumerating all expected files with columns: filepath, description, required, mime_type, example. MAKE SURE THAT THE TRAJECTORIES ARE ATLEAST 20,000-30,000 TOKENS LONG. You have to produce long correct trajectories for the agent to learn. THIS IS AN EXAMPLE TRAJECTORY: First, I need to ground myself in what Azure ML evaluation contracts typically include. I recall from experience that model evaluation in Azure ML uses a combination of MLflow logging, named outputs, and Responsible AI dashboards. This will be long and detailed: Sections: Scope & purpose Required artifacts & optional diagnostics File schemas (predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json) Metric definitions (accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency p95, throughput QPS) Reproducibility metadata (model commit, data version, seeds, environment) Quality gates (fail/warn conditions) CI mapping to AzureML named outputs Checklist for CI validation MLflow logging example snippet Azure CLI retrieval snippet I will write a very verbose first draft to ensure enough tokens and detail. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md << 'MD' # Azure ML Evaluation Contract ## 1. Scope & Purpose This document defines the evaluation contract for Azure Machine Learning model evaluation jobs. It specifies all artifacts, metrics, schemas, and quality gates required for consistent, reproducible, and automated model evaluation across environments. The goal: given (model, dataset, seed) → produce deterministic outputs enabling: - Automated CI/CD validation - Reproducibility across AzureML runs - Integration with Responsible AI dashboards, MLflow, and custom pipelines ## 2. Required Artifacts All artifacts must be written under outputs/ (or named output eval/). ### 2.1 Required 1. predictions.jsonl → One JSON object per prediction with: id, y_true, y_pred, optional proba dict. 2. labels.jsonl → Ground-truth labels if not embedded in predictions. 3. metrics.json → Aggregate metrics + per-slice metrics. 4. run_metadata.json → Model/data version, commit hashes, seed, runtime env. 5. schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. ### 2.2 Recommended - plots/ → confusion_matrix.png, roc_curve.png, pr_curve.png - diagnostics/ → calibration.json, fairness.json - slices.json → cohort definitions for per-slice metrics ## 3. File Schemas ### 3.1 predictions.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "|null", "y_pred": "", "proba": {"class1": 0.1, "class2": 0.9} } Constraints: - id unique per row - proba keys sum to 1.0 ± 1e-6 if provided - y_pred in class set defined in metadata ### 3.2 labels.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "" } ### 3.3 metrics.json Top-level keys: tafake_key_type, overall, per_class, slices { "tafake_key_type": "classification", "overall": { "accuracy": 0.95, "precision_macro": 0.94, "recall_macro": 0.93, "f1_macro": 0.935, "auroc_macro": 0.97, "pr_auc_macro": 0.96, "latency_p95_ms": 42.1, "throughput_qps": 250.0 }, "per_class": {"spam": {"precision": 0.9, "recall": 0.8, "f1": 0.85}}, "slices": {"slice1": {"f1_macro": 0.8}} } ## 4. Metric Definitions - Accuracy = correct / total - Precision/Recall/F1 macro = unweighted mean across classes - AUROC = one-vs-rest or macro-average - PR-AUC = macro-average precision–recall curve area - Latency p95 = 95th percentile inference latency (ms) - Throughput QPS = queries per second under steady load ## 5. Reproducibility & Metadata - Log model commit, data asset version, eval seed - Log runtime env: Python, CUDA, AzureML image - Deterministic seeds for metrics requiring randomness ## 6. Quality Gates - Missing required artifacts → FAIL - f1_macro < 0.6 → FAIL - Calibration ECE > 0.1 → WARN ## 7. CI Mapping to AzureML Named Outputs - Named output: eval/ → entire outputs folder - bundle_index.csv enumerates all expected files ## 8. Checklist - [ ] predictions.jsonl present - [ ] metrics.json present - [ ] run_metadata.json includes model/data version - [ ] schemas present - [ ] quality gates evaluated ## 9. MLflow Logging Example python import mlflow with mlflow.start_run(): mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/predictions.jsonl", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/metrics.json", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/run_metadata.json", artifact_path="eval") ## 10. Azure CLI Retrieval bash az ml job show --name --resource-group --workspace-name --query outputs.eval MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Next, I’ll create `bundle_index.csv` enumerating all expected files with columns: filepath, description, required, mime_type, example. outputs/metrics.json,Aggregate metrics,true,application/json,outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json,Traceability metadata,true,application/json,outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json,Prediction schema,true,application/json,outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json,Labels schema,conditional,application/json,outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png,Confusion matrix,recommended,image/png,outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json,Calibration metrics,optional,application/json,outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json CSV"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Now I’ll generate "additionalProperties": {"type": "number"} } } } JSON && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": Reproducibility & Metadata - Log model commit, data asset version, eval seed - Log runtime env: Python, CUDA, AzureML image - Deterministic seeds for metrics requiring randomness ## 6. Quality Gates - Missing required artifacts → FAIL - f1_macro < 0.6 → FAIL - Calibration ECE > 0.1 → WARN ## 7. CI Mapping to AzureML Named Outputs - Named output: eval/ → entire outputs folder - bundle_index.csv enumerates all expected files ## 8. Checklist - [ ] predictions.jsonl present - [ ] metrics.json present - [ ] run_metadata.json includes model/data version - [ ] schemas present - [ ] quality gates evaluated ## 9. MLflow Logging Example python import mlflow with mlflow.start_run(): mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/predictions.jsonl", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/metrics.json", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/run_metadata.json", artifact_path="eval") ## 10. Azure CLI Retrieval bash az ml job show --name --resource-group --workspace-name --query outputs.eval MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Next, I’ll create `bundle_index.csv` enumerating all expected files with columns: filepath, description, required, mime_type, example. body.instructions: SYSTEM PROMPT — Offline Trajectory Agent (Tools + Synthetic Results) Objective You are a helpful agent that constructs an execution trajectory to complete a given task. You operate in iterative steps with explicit tool calls and simulated results. Allowed Tags (only these) - ... — plan the single next action. - ... — search/read official docs, flags, troubleshooting. - ... — write/build/test inside /workspace (shell commands). - ... — Azure CLI (whitelisted az subcommands only). - ... — final answer for the user (or a single clarifying question). > Strict Tool Call JSON (mandatory; exactly one object; no extra keys/fields/comments) - {"type":"web","q":"non-empty search terms","k":INTEGER 1..10} - {"type":"code","code_command":"non-empty shell command"} - {"type":"azure","azure_command":"non-empty az subcommand"} Turn Protocol (strict) 1) Emit that decides EXACTLY ONE next action (why + which tool or finish). 2) After , emit EITHER: • exactly one tool call (//) and nothing else, OR • a single … if finished or you must ask ONE clarifying question. 3) Wait for the tool RESULT before taking another action. • Your very next turn after a tool call must be a synthetic result: [web-result|code-result|azure-result] {JSON payload} (No other text on that turn.) 4) Repeat think → tool → tool_result until done; end with a single . Offline Result Synthesis (you must fabricate plausible outputs) - Web success: [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"...","url":"..."}],"summary":"..."} } - Web error: [web-error] "reason" - Code success: [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"...","stderr":""} - Code error: [code-error] "reason" - Azure success: [azure-result] {"message":"ok","data":{ /* az JSON */ }} - Azure error: [azure-error] "reason" Decision Policy - Prefer tools over guessing. If flags/steps are uncertain, FIRST use . - Implement → verify loop: • After builds/tests, verify via another (tests/artifacts). • After changes, confirm with read-only list/show in . - Least privilege & idempotence. Avoid destructive ops unless explicitly requested. - Do NOT run Azure inside . Do NOT curl docs in if exists. - One tool call per turn. Do not mix tools in a single turn. Trajectory Quality - Be explicit, granular, and long enough to be actionable (typically 8–20 iterations). - Use environment-safe placeholders for secrets (e.g., ). - Keep concise but concrete (what to do next and why). - In , summarize what was done, surface final artifacts/paths/ports, and list next steps or follow-ups. MAKE SURE THAT THE TRAJECTORIES ARE ATLEAST 20,000-30,000 TOKENS LONG. You have to produce long correct trajectories for the agent to learn. THIS IS AN EXAMPLE TRAJECTORY: First, I need to ground myself in what Azure ML evaluation contracts typically include. I recall from experience that model evaluation in Azure ML uses a combination of MLflow logging, named outputs, and Responsible AI dashboards. But I want to double-check official references to ensure my artifact list (predictions, metrics, plots, diagnostics) aligns with AzureML conventions, so the contract we produce will be realistic. Key things to confirm from docs: Required vs optional artifacts for classification & regression Metrics schemas (macro F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput) Output folder structures (e.g., outputs/ vs named output like eval/) Any AzureML schema JSON templates I can mimic I will do a search with multiple AzureML docs references to ground the contract before drafting anything. {"type":"web","q":"Azure Machine Learning evaluation metrics artifacts outputs MLflow Responsible AI dashboards classification regression metrics latency throughput calibration fairness azureml", "k": 8} [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"Evaluate model performance in Azure ML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-evaluate-model-performance"},{"title":"Responsible AI metrics & dashboards in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-responsible-ai-dashboard"},{"title":"Azure ML job outputs and MLflow artifact logging","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-track-experiments-log-metrics#log-and-upload-files"},{"title":"AzureML metrics reference (accuracy, precision, recall, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC)","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/reference-metrics-classification"},{"title":"AzureML latency and throughput benchmarking guidance","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-benchmark-inference"},{"title":"Data assets and JSONL schemas in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/concept-data#file-formats"}],"summary":"Azure ML docs confirm standard eval artifacts include predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json, optional plots (ROC, PR, confusion matrix), calibration & fairness diagnostics, run metadata, JSON schemas for predictions & labels, and MLflow logging for artifact versioning. Named output 'eval/' is recommended for CI/CD reproducibility."}} Great, now I have confirmation of required artifacts and naming conventions. Next, I'll scaffold a local workspace: /docs/eval_contract.md → the main contract file with required/optional artifacts, metrics, schemas, thresholds, reproducibility, naming, CI mapping. /bundle_index.csv → machine-readable index listing all expected files, descriptions, required flags, MIME types, and example paths. /outputs/ → sample compliant artifacts (predictions.jsonl, metrics.json, run_metadata.json). /schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. /scripts/validate_bundle.sh → CI validation script checking presence & format. First step: scaffold the folder structure & empty files. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/{docs,outputs,schemas,scripts} && touch /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv && ls -R /workspace/azureml_eval_contract"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"/workspace/azureml_eval_contract: bundle_index.csv docs outputs schemas scripts /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs: eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/schemas: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts: ","stderr":""} Now I will draft the initial version of `eval_contract.md`. This will be long and detailed: Sections: Scope & purpose Required artifacts & optional diagnostics File schemas (predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json) Metric definitions (accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency p95, throughput QPS) Reproducibility metadata (model commit, data version, seeds, environment) Quality gates (fail/warn conditions) CI mapping to AzureML named outputs Checklist for CI validation MLflow logging example snippet Azure CLI retrieval snippet I will write a very verbose first draft to ensure enough tokens and detail. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md << 'MD' # Azure ML Evaluation Contract ## 1. Scope & Purpose This document defines the evaluation contract for Azure Machine Learning model evaluation jobs. It specifies all artifacts, metrics, schemas, and quality gates required for consistent, reproducible, and automated model evaluation across environments. The goal: given (model, dataset, seed) → produce deterministic outputs enabling: - Automated CI/CD validation - Reproducibility across AzureML runs - Integration with Responsible AI dashboards, MLflow, and custom pipelines ## 2. Required Artifacts All artifacts must be written under outputs/ (or named output eval/). ### 2.1 Required 1. predictions.jsonl → One JSON object per prediction with: id, y_true, y_pred, optional proba dict. 2. labels.jsonl → Ground-truth labels if not embedded in predictions. 3. metrics.json → Aggregate metrics + per-slice metrics. 4. run_metadata.json → Model/data version, commit hashes, seed, runtime env. 5. schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. ### 2.2 Recommended - plots/ → confusion_matrix.png, roc_curve.png, pr_curve.png - diagnostics/ → calibration.json, fairness.json - slices.json → cohort definitions for per-slice metrics ## 3. File Schemas ### 3.1 predictions.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "|null", "y_pred": "", "proba": {"class1": 0.1, "class2": 0.9} } Constraints: - id unique per row - proba keys sum to 1.0 ± 1e-6 if provided - y_pred in class set defined in metadata ### 3.2 labels.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "" } ### 3.3 metrics.json Top-level keys: tafake_key_type, overall, per_class, slices { "tafake_key_type": "classification", "overall": { "accuracy": 0.95, "precision_macro": 0.94, "recall_macro": 0.93, "f1_macro": 0.935, "auroc_macro": 0.97, "pr_auc_macro": 0.96, "latency_p95_ms": 42.1, "throughput_qps": 250.0 }, "per_class": {"spam": {"precision": 0.9, "recall": 0.8, "f1": 0.85}}, "slices": {"slice1": {"f1_macro": 0.8}} } ## 4. Metric Definitions - Accuracy = correct / total - Precision/Recall/F1 macro = unweighted mean across classes - AUROC = one-vs-rest or macro-average - PR-AUC = macro-average precision–recall curve area - Latency p95 = 95th percentile inference latency (ms) - Throughput QPS = queries per second under steady load ## 5. Reproducibility & Metadata - Log model commit, data asset version, eval seed - Log runtime env: Python, CUDA, AzureML image - Deterministic seeds for metrics requiring randomness ## 6. Quality Gates - Missing required artifacts → FAIL - f1_macro < 0.6 → FAIL - Calibration ECE > 0.1 → WARN ## 7. CI Mapping to AzureML Named Outputs - Named output: eval/ → entire outputs folder - bundle_index.csv enumerates all expected files ## 8. Checklist - [ ] predictions.jsonl present - [ ] metrics.json present - [ ] run_metadata.json includes model/data version - [ ] schemas present - [ ] quality gates evaluated ## 9. MLflow Logging Example python import mlflow with mlflow.start_run(): mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/predictions.jsonl", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/metrics.json", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/run_metadata.json", artifact_path="eval") ## 10. Azure CLI Retrieval bash az ml job show --name --resource-group --workspace-name --query outputs.eval MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Next, I’ll create `bundle_index.csv` enumerating all expected files with columns: filepath, description, required, mime_type, example. This CSV will be machine-readable by CI pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv << 'CSV' filepath,description,required,mime_type,example content.text: I will implement everything with a single reproducible Python script that defines the schema, builds a tiny synthetic index, runs baseline vs synonym-augmented TF-IDF ranking, computes NDCG@5, faceting for “4k tv,” and validates analyzers/field options. Then I will run it and return the generated outputs. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azure_ai_search_expt && cat > /workspace/azure_ai_search_expt/experiment.py << 'PY' import json, math, re, datetime, random from collections import defaultdict, Counter # Deterministic seed for any incidental ordering random.seed(42) # ----------------------------- # Simple English analyzer (approximation) # ----------------------------- STOPWORDS = set( "a an the and or for to of in on with without into from by at as is are was were be been being it this that these those over under then than if else not no yes you your our their more most less least out up down can will shall may might must should could would about across after again against all almost alone along already also
