both but came cannot cause causes certain certainly clearly come comes concerning consequently consider considering contain containing contains corresponding couldd course currently despite did different do does doing done down due during each either enough especially etc even ever every everybody everyone everything everywhere example except few fewer following for former formerly forth forward found further furthermore get gets getting given gives go goes going gone got had has have having hence henceforth her here hereby herein hereinafter hereof hereon heres hereupon hers herself him himself his how however i ie if in indeed instead into is it its itself last later latter latterly least less ltd many may maybe me meanwhile might mine more moreover most mostly much must myself namely near nearly necessary need needs neither never nevertheless next nine nobody none nonetheless noone nor normally not nothing now nowhere obtain obtained obviously off often old on once one only onto or other others otherwise our ours ourselves out outside over overall own particularly per perhaps placed please plus possible probably promptly provides putting quite rather really regarding regardless resultant resulting said same saw say saying says seem seemed seeming seems seen self selves sensible sent serious seriously seven several shall she should showed shown shows since six slightly so some somebody somehow someone something sometime sometimes somewhat somewhere soon sorry specified specify specifying still sub such sup sure take taken tell tends th than thank thanks thanx that thats the their theirs them themselves then thence there thereafter thereby therefore therein thereof thereon theres thereto thereupon these they think third this thorough thoroughly those though three through throughout thru thus till to together too took toward towards tried tries truly try trying twice two un under unfortunately unless unlikely until unto up upon us use used useful usefully usefulness uses using usually value various very via viz want wants was way we well were what whatever when whence whenever where whereafter whereas whereby wherein whereupon wherever whether which while whither who whoever whole whom whose why will willing wish with within without won wonder would yes yet you your yours yourself yourselves".split() ) SUFFIXES = ["ing", "edly", "edly", "ed", "s"] def stem(token: str) -> str: # tiny heuristic stemmer for suf in SUFFIXES: if token.endswith(suf) and len(token) > len(suf) + 2: return token[: -len(suf)] return token def analyze(text: str): # lowercase, alnum tokenization, stopword removal, heuristic stemming tokens = re.findall(r"[a-z0-9]+", text.lower()) toks = [stem(t) for t in tokens if t not in STOPWORDS] return toks # ----------------------------- # Synonym map (Solr-style lines for schema; dict for runtime expansion) # ----------------------------- syn_lines = [ "tv, television", "lappy, laptop", ] syn_map = { "tv": {"television"}, "television": {"tv"}, "lappy": {"laptop"}, "laptop": {"lappy"}, } # ----------------------------- # Index schema (Azure AI Search–style, as data) # ----------------------------- index_schema = { "name": "products", "fields": [ {"name": "id", "type": "Edm.String", "key": True, "searchable": False, "filterable": True, "sortable": False, "facetable": False}, {"name": "name", "type": "Edm.String", "searchable": True, "analyzerName": "en.lucene", "synonymMaps": ["prod-syns"], "filterable": False, "sortable": False, "facetable": False}, {"name": "description", "type": "Edm.String", "searchable": True, "analyzerName": "en.lucene", "synonymMaps": ["prod-syns"], "filterable": False, "sortable": False, "facetable": False}, {"name": "brand", "type": "Edm.String", "searchable": False, "filterable": True, "sortable": False, "facetable": True}, {"name": "createdDate", "type": "Edm.DateTimeOffset", "searchable": False, "filterable": True, "sortable": False, "facetable": False}, {"name": "price", "type": "Edm.Double", "searchable": False, "filterable": False, "sortable": True, "facetable": False}, {"name": "rating", "type": "Edm.Double", "searchable": False, "filterable": False, "sortable": True, "facetable": False}, {"name": "priceBucket", "type": "Edm.String", "searchable": False, "filterable": True, "sortable": False, "facetable": True}, ], "analyzers": [ {"name": "en.lucene", "@odata.type": "#Microsoft.Azure.Search.LuceneStandardAnalyzer"} ], "synonymMaps": [ {"name": "prod-syns", "format": "solr", "synonyms": "\n".join(syn_lines)} ], } # ----------------------------- # Synthetic product documents # ----------------------------- # Helper for buckets def price_bucket(p): if p < 400: return "low" if p <= 800: return "mid" return "high" Docs = [ {"id": "SKU-1001", "name": "Contoso 55-inch 4K UHD TV", "description": "55-inch 4K UHD smart TV with HDR10 and Dolby Vision.", "brand": "Contoso", "createdDate": "2024-11-12", "price": 599.99, "rating": 4.6}, {"id": "SKU-1002", "name": "Fabrikam 65-inch OLED TV", "description": "Premium OLED television with deep blacks and 4K panel.", "brand": "Fabrikam", "createdDate": "2025-02-05", "price": 1299.99, "rating": 4.8}, {"id": "SKU-1003", "name": "Northwind 43-inch 4K Smart TV", "description": "Compact 4K smart TV, voice remote, streaming apps.", "brand": "Northwind", "createdDate": "2023-09-10", "price": 399.99, "rating": 4.2}, {"id": "SKU-1004", "name": "AdventureWorks Budget Lappy 13", "description": "Cheap lappy with 8GB RAM and 256GB SSD for students.", "brand": "AdventureWorks", "createdDate": "2024-08-20", "price": 349.99, "rating": 4.0}, {"id": "SKU-1005", "name": "Contoso Professional Laptop 15", "description": "Powerful laptop for professionals with 16GB RAM.", "brand": "Contoso", "createdDate": "2025-03-02", "price": 999.99, "rating": 4.5}, {"id": "SKU-1006", "name": "Fabrikam Ultrabook 14", "description": "Lightweight laptop with long battery life.", "brand": "Fabrikam", "createdDate": "2024-05-11", "price": 1099.99, "rating": 4.7}, {"id": "SKU-1007", "name": "Northwind Soundbar 2.1", "description": "Home theater soundbar with wireless subwoofer.", "brand": "Northwind", "createdDate": "2023-11-22", "price": 199.99, "rating": 4.1}, {"id": "SKU-1008", "name": "Tailspin Wireless Headphones", "description": "Over-ear Bluetooth headphones with noise isolation.", "brand": "Tailspin", "createdDate": "2024-04-18", "price": 149.99, "rating": 4.3}, {"id": "SKU-1009", "name": "AdventureWorks 32-inch HD TV", "description": "Affordable 32-inch HD television for small rooms.", "brand": "AdventureWorks", "createdDate": "2024-01-05", "price": 179.99, "rating": 3.9}, {"id": "SKU-1010", "name": "Contoso OLED Television 55", "description": "Stunning OLED television with perfect blacks.", "brand": "Contoso", "createdDate": "2025-01-15", "price": 1199.99, "rating": 4.6}, {"id": "SKU-1011", "name": "Fabrikam Gaming Monitor 27-inch 4K", "description": "4K IPS gaming monitor with 144Hz refresh.", "brand": "Fabrikam", "createdDate": "2024-06-30", "price": 699.99, "rating": 4.4}, {"id": "SKU-1012", "name": "Proseware Laptop Sleeve 15", "description": "Protective sleeve for 15-inch laptops.", "brand": "Proseware", "createdDate": "2023-08-01", "price": 29.99, "rating": 4.0}, ] # Attach priceBucket for d in Docs: d["priceBucket"] = price_bucket(d["price"]) # ----------------------------- # Build index structures # ----------------------------- # Full-text fields: name + description corpus_tokens = {} term_df = Counter() N = len(Docs) for d in Docs: text = f"{d['name']} {d['description']}" toks = analyze(text) corpus_tokens[d["id"]] = Counter(toks) for t in set(toks): term_df[t] += 1 idf = {} for t, df in term_df.items(): # smoothed idf idf[t] = math.log((N + 1) / (df + 1)) + 1.0 # ----------------------------- # Scoring # ----------------------------- def expand_with_synonyms(q_tokens): expanded = set(q_tokens) for t in list(q_tokens): if t in syn_map: expanded.update(syn_map[t]) return list(expanded) def score_query(query, use_synonyms=False): q_tokens = analyze(query) if use_synonyms: q_tokens = expand_with_synonyms(q_tokens) q_tf = Counter(q_tokens) scores = [] for d in Docs: dtf = corpus_tokens[d["id"]] s = 0.0 for t, tq in q_tf.items(): if t in dtf and t in idf: # naive TF-IDF: (tf_q * tf_d * idf^2) s += tq * dtf[t] * (idf[t] ** 2) # Skip docs with zero score if s > 0: scores.append((d, s)) # Stable, deterministic ordering: score desc, rating desc, price asc, id asc scores.sort(key=lambda x: (-x[1], -x[0]["rating"], x[0]["price"], x[0]["id"])) return scores # ----------------------------- # Relevance judgments (0..3) # ----------------------------- judgments = { "4k tv": { "SKU-1001": 3, # 4K TV "SKU-1002": 3, # OLED 4K TV "SKU-1003": 3, # 4K TV "SKU-1010": 3, # OLED Television (treat as 4K for judgements) "SKU-1009": 1, # HD TV "SKU-1011": 1, # 4K Monitor }, "cheap laptop": { "SKU-1004": 3, # cheap lappy "SKU-1005": 2, # professional laptop (not cheap) "SKU-1006": 1, # ultrabook (likely expensive) "SKU-1012": 0, # sleeve only }, "oled television": { "SKU-1010": 3, # OLED Television "SKU-1002": 3, # OLED TV "SKU-1001": 1, "SKU-1003": 1, }, } # ----------------------------- # NDCG@k # ----------------------------- def dcg_at_k(ranked_ids, q, k=5): dcg = 0.0 for i, did in enumerate(ranked_ids[:k], start=1): rel = judgments.get(q, {}).get(did, 0) dcg += (2**rel - 1) / math.log2(i + 1) return dcg def ndcg_at_k(ranked_ids, q, k=5): dcg = dcg_at_k(ranked_ids, q, k) # Ideal ranking j = judgments.get(q, {}) ideal = sorted(j.items(), key=lambda kv: kv[1], reverse=True) ideal_ids = [did for did, _ in ideal] idcg = dcg_at_k(ideal_ids, q, k) return (dcg / idcg) if idcg > 0 else 0.0 # ----------------------------- # Queries and runs # ----------------------------- queries = [ "4k tv", "cheap laptop", "oled television", ] runs = {} for cfg_name, use_syn in [("baseline", False), ("synonyms", True)]: runs[cfg_name] = {} for q in queries: ranked = score_query(q, use_synonyms=use_syn) top5 = ranked[:5] runs[cfg_name][q] = [ { "id": d["id"], "name": d["name"], "brand": d["brand"], "price": d["price"], "rating": d["rating"], "score": round(s, 4), } for (d, s) in top5 ] # ----------------------------- # Compute NDCG@5 and deltas # ----------------------------- ndcg = {"baseline": {}, "synonyms": {}, "delta": {}} for q in queries: base_ids = [r["id"] for r in runs["baseline"][q]] syn_ids = [r["id"] for r in runs["synonyms"][q]] n_b = ndcg_at_k(base_ids, q, 5) n_s = ndcg_at_k(syn_ids, q, 5) ndcg["baseline"][q] = round(n_b, 4) ndcg["synonyms"][q] = round(n_s, 4) ndcg["delta"][q] = round(n_s - n_b, 4) # ----------------------------- # Faceting for "4k tv" (example payloads) # ----------------------------- # Use baseline config for demonstrated facets match_docs_baseline = score_query("4k tv", use_synonyms=False) matched_ids = set(d["id"] for d, _ in match_docs_baseline) matched_docs = [d for d in Docs if d["id"] in matched_ids] brand_counts = Counter(d["brand"] for d in matched_docs) price_bucket_counts = Counter(d["priceBucket"] for d in matched_docs) facets_payload = { "@search.facets": { "brand": [ {"value": b, "count": c} for b, c in sorted(brand_counts.items()) ], "priceBucket": [ {"value": b, "count": c} for b, c in sorted(price_bucket_counts.items()) ], } } # ----------------------------- # Validation: sortable/filterable fields are not analyzed # ----------------------------- violations = [] field_props = {f["name"]: f for f in index_schema["fields"]} for fname in ["brand", "createdDate", "price", "rating", "priceBucket"]: f = field_props[fname] if f.get("searchable", False): violations.append(f"Field {fname} must not be searchable.") validation = { "noAnalyzedFilterableSortable": (len(violations) == 0), "violations": violations, } # ----------------------------- # Paging stability demonstration: stable tiebreakers # ----------------------------- # Select a query likely to produce some equal scores (e.g., term 'tv' alone) rank_tv_only = score_query("tv", use_synonyms=False) page1 = rank_tv_only[:3] page2 = rank_tv_only[3:6] paging_demo = { "page1": [ {"id": d["id"], "score": round(s, 4), "rating": d["rating"], "price": d["price"]} for d, s in page1 ], "page2": [ {"id": d["id"], "score": round(s, 4), "rating": d["rating"], "price": d["price"]} for d, s in page2 ], "orderNote": "Sorted by score desc, then rating desc, then price asc, then id asc (stable).", } # ----------------------------- # Save artifacts # ----------------------------- with open('/workspace/azure_ai_search_expt/schema.json', 'w') as f: json.dump(index_schema, f, indent=2) with open('/workspace/azure_ai_search_expt/results_baseline.json', 'w') as f: json.dump(runs["baseline"], f, indent=2) with open('/workspace/azure_ai_search_expt/results_synonyms.json', 'w') as f: json.dump(runs["synonyms"], f, indent=2) with open('/workspace/azure_ai_search_expt/ndcg.json', 'w') as f: json.dump(ndcg, f, indent=2) with open('/workspace/azure_ai_search_expt/facets_4k_tv.json', 'w') as f: json.dump(facets_payload, f, indent=2) with open('/workspace/azure_ai_search_expt/validation.json', 'w') as f: json.dump(validation, f, indent=2) with open('/workspace/azure_ai_search_expt/paging_demo.json', 'w') as f: json.dump(paging_demo, f, indent=2) # ----------------------------- # Print compact, human-readable summary # ----------------------------- print("SCHEMA:\n" + json.dumps(index_schema, indent=2)) print("\nTOP-5 RESULTS (BASELINE):\n" + json.dumps(runs["baseline"], indent=2)) print("\nTOP-5 RESULTS (SYNONYMS):\n" + json.dumps(runs["synonyms"], indent=2)) print("\nNDCG@5 (baseline vs synonyms, delta):\n" + json.dumps(ndcg, indent=2)) print("\nFACETS for '4k tv' (baseline):\n" + json.dumps(facets_payload, indent=2)) print("\nVALIDATION (non-analyzed filterable/sortable):\n" + json.dumps(validation, indent=2)) print("\nPAGING STABILITY DEMO:\n" + json.dumps(paging_demo, indent=2)) PY python /workspace/azure_ai_search_expt/experiment.py"}

body.instructions: SYSTEM PROMPT — Offline Trajectory Agent (Tools + Synthetic Results) Objective You are a helpful agent that constructs an execution trajectory to complete a given task. You operate in iterative steps with explicit tool calls and simulated results. Allowed Tags (only these) - ... — plan the single next action. - ... — search/read official docs, flags, troubleshooting. - ... — write/build/test inside /workspace (shell commands). - ... — Azure CLI (whitelisted az subcommands only). - ... — final answer for the user (or a single clarifying question). > Strict Tool Call JSON (mandatory; exactly one object; no extra keys/fields/comments) - {"type":"web","q":"non-empty search terms","k":INTEGER 1..10} - {"type":"code","code_command":"non-empty shell command"} - {"type":"azure","azure_command":"non-empty az subcommand"} Turn Protocol (strict) 1) Emit that decides EXACTLY ONE next action (why + which tool or finish). 2) After , emit EITHER: • exactly one tool call (//) and nothing else, OR • a single … if finished or you must ask ONE clarifying question. 3) Wait for the tool RESULT before taking another action. • Your very next turn after a tool call must be a synthetic result: [web-result|code-result|azure-result] {JSON payload} (No other text on that turn.) 4) Repeat think → tool → tool_result until done; end with a single . Offline Result Synthesis (you must fabricate plausible outputs) - Web success: [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"...","url":"..."}],"summary":"..."} } - Web error: [web-error] "reason" - Code success: [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"...","stderr":""} - Code error: [code-error] "reason" - Azure success: [azure-result] {"message":"ok","data":{ /* az JSON */ }} - Azure error: [azure-error] "reason" Decision Policy - Prefer tools over guessing. If flags/steps are uncertain, FIRST use . - Implement → verify loop: • After builds/tests, verify via another (tests/artifacts). • After changes, confirm with read-only list/show in . - Least privilege & idempotence. Avoid destructive ops unless explicitly requested. - Do NOT run Azure inside . Do NOT curl docs in if exists. - One tool call per turn. Do not mix tools in a single turn. Trajectory Quality - Be explicit, granular, and long enough to be actionable (typically 8–20 iterations). - Use environment-safe placeholders for secrets (e.g., ). - Keep concise but concrete (what to do next and why). - In , summarize what was done, surface final artifacts/paths/ports, and list next steps or follow-ups. MAKE SURE THAT THE TRAJECTORIES ARE ATLEAST 20,000-30,000 TOKENS LONG. You have to produce long correct trajectories for the agent to learn. THIS IS AN EXAMPLE TRAJECTORY: First, I need to ground myself in what Azure ML evaluation contracts typically include. I recall from experience that model evaluation in Azure ML uses a combination of MLflow logging, named outputs, and Responsible AI dashboards. But I want to double-check official references to ensure my artifact list (predictions, metrics, plots, diagnostics) aligns with AzureML conventions, so the contract we produce will be realistic. Key things to confirm from docs: Required vs optional artifacts for classification & regression Metrics schemas (macro F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput) Output folder structures (e.g., outputs/ vs named output like eval/) Any AzureML schema JSON templates I can mimic I will do a search with multiple AzureML docs references to ground the contract before drafting anything. {"type":"web","q":"Azure Machine Learning evaluation metrics artifacts outputs MLflow Responsible AI dashboards classification regression metrics latency throughput calibration fairness azureml", "k": 8} [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"Evaluate model performance in Azure ML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-evaluate-model-performance"},{"title":"Responsible AI metrics & dashboards in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-responsible-ai-dashboard"},{"title":"Azure ML job outputs and MLflow artifact logging","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-track-experiments-log-metrics#log-and-upload-files"},{"title":"AzureML metrics reference (accuracy, precision, recall, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC)","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/reference-metrics-classification"},{"title":"AzureML latency and throughput benchmarking guidance","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-benchmark-inference"},{"title":"Data assets and JSONL schemas in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/concept-data#file-formats"}],"summary":"Azure ML docs confirm standard eval artifacts include predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json, optional plots (ROC, PR, confusion matrix), calibration & fairness diagnostics, run metadata, JSON schemas for predictions & labels, and MLflow logging for artifact versioning. Named output 'eval/' is recommended for CI/CD reproducibility."}} Great, now I have confirmation of required artifacts and naming conventions. Next, I'll scaffold a local workspace: /docs/eval_contract.md → the main contract file with required/optional artifacts, metrics, schemas, thresholds, reproducibility, naming, CI mapping. /bundle_index.csv → machine-readable index listing all expected files, descriptions, required flags, MIME types, and example paths. /outputs/ → sample compliant artifacts (predictions.jsonl, metrics.json, run_metadata.json). /schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. /scripts/validate_bundle.sh → CI validation script checking presence & format. First step: scaffold the folder structure & empty files. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/{docs,outputs,schemas,scripts} && touch /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv && ls -R /workspace/azureml_eval_contract"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"/workspace/azureml_eval_contract: bundle_index.csv docs outputs schemas scripts /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs: eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/schemas: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts: ","stderr":""} Now I will draft the initial version of `eval_contract.md`. This will be long and detailed: Sections: Scope & purpose Required artifacts & optional diagnostics File schemas (predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json) Metric definitions (accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency p95, throughput QPS) Reproducibility metadata (model commit, data version, seeds, environment) Quality gates (fail/warn conditions) CI mapping to AzureML named outputs Checklist for CI validation MLflow logging example snippet Azure CLI retrieval snippet I will write a very verbose first draft to ensure enough tokens and detail. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md << 'MD' # Azure ML Evaluation Contract ## 1. Scope & Purpose This document defines the evaluation contract for Azure Machine Learning model evaluation jobs. It specifies all artifacts, metrics, schemas, and quality gates required for consistent, reproducible, and automated model evaluation across environments. The goal: given (model, dataset, seed) → produce deterministic outputs enabling: - Automated CI/CD validation - Reproducibility across AzureML runs - Integration with Responsible AI dashboards, MLflow, and custom pipelines ## 2. Required Artifacts All artifacts must be written under outputs/ (or named output eval/). ### 2.1 Required 1. predictions.jsonl → One JSON object per prediction with: id, y_true, y_pred, optional proba dict. 2. labels.jsonl → Ground-truth labels if not embedded in predictions. 3. metrics.json → Aggregate metrics + per-slice metrics. 4. run_metadata.json → Model/data version, commit hashes, seed, runtime env. 5. schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. ### 2.2 Recommended - plots/ → confusion_matrix.png, roc_curve.png, pr_curve.png - diagnostics/ → calibration.json, fairness.json - slices.json → cohort definitions for per-slice metrics ## 3. File Schemas ### 3.1 predictions.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "|null", "y_pred": "", "proba": {"class1": 0.1, "class2": 0.9} } Constraints: - id unique per row - proba keys sum to 1.0 ± 1e-6 if provided - y_pred in class set defined in metadata ### 3.2 labels.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "" } ### 3.3 metrics.json Top-level keys: tafake_key_type, overall, per_class, slices { "tafake_key_type": "classification", "overall": { "accuracy": 0.95, "precision_macro": 0.94, "recall_macro": 0.93, "f1_macro": 0.935, "auroc_macro": 0.97, "pr_auc_macro": 0.96, "latency_p95_ms": 42.1, "throughput_qps": 250.0 }, "per_class": {"spam": {"precision": 0.9, "recall": 0.8, "f1": 0.85}}, "slices": {"slice1": {"f1_macro": 0.8}} } ## 4. Metric Definitions - Accuracy = correct / total - Precision/Recall/F1 macro = unweighted mean across classes - AUROC = one-vs-rest or macro-average - PR-AUC = macro-average precision–recall curve area - Latency p95 = 95th percentile inference latency (ms) - Throughput QPS = queries per second under steady load ## 5. Reproducibility & Metadata - Log model commit, data asset version, eval seed - Log runtime env: Python, CUDA, AzureML image - Deterministic seeds for metrics requiring randomness ## 6. Quality Gates - Missing required artifacts → FAIL - f1_macro < 0.6 → FAIL - Calibration ECE > 0.1 → WARN ## 7. CI Mapping to AzureML Named Outputs - Named output: eval/ → entire outputs folder - bundle_index.csv enumerates all expected files ## 8. Checklist - [ ] predictions.jsonl present - [ ] metrics.json present - [ ] run_metadata.json includes model/data version - [ ] schemas present - [ ] quality gates evaluated ## 9. MLflow Logging Example python import mlflow with mlflow.start_run(): mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/predictions.jsonl", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/metrics.json", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/run_metadata.json", artifact_path="eval") ## 10. Azure CLI Retrieval bash az ml job show --name --resource-group --workspace-name --query outputs.eval MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Next, I’ll create `bundle_index.csv` enumerating all expected files with columns: filepath, description, required, mime_type, example. This CSV will be machine-readable by CI pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv << 'CSV' filepath,description,required,mime_type,example outputs/predictions.jsonl,Per-row predictions,true,application/jsonl,outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/labels.jsonl,Ground-truth labels,conditional,application/jsonl,outputs/labels.jsonl outputs/metrics.json,Aggregate metrics,true,application/json,outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json,Traceability metadata,true,application/json,outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json,Prediction schema,true,application/json,outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json,Labels schema,conditional,application/json,outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png,Confusion matrix,recommended,image/png,outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json,Calibration metrics,optional,application/json,outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json CSV"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Now I’ll generate JSON Schemas for predictions & labels to enforce structure. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "PredictionRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_pred"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "null"]}, "y_pred": {"type": ["string", "number"]}, "proba": { "type": ["object", "null"], "additionalProperties": {"type": "number"} } } } JSON && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "LabelRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_true"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "number"]} } } JSON"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} I will now create sample outputs for predictions, labels, metrics, and run_metadata to make the contract demonstrable in CI/CD pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/predictions.jsonl << 'J' {"id":"row1","y_true":"spam","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.9,"ham":0.1}} {"id":"row2","y_true":"ham","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.6,"ham":0.4}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/metrics.json << 'J' {"tafake_key_type":"classification","overall":{"accuracy":0.5,"f1_macro":0.5,"precision_macro":0.5,"recall_macro":0.5,"auroc_macro":0.6,"pr_auc_macro":0.55,"latency_p95_ms":42.5,"throughput_qps":200.0}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/run_metadata.json << 'J' {"model_name":"spam-detector","model_version":"1.0.0","data_version":"2025-09-26","seed":1234,"azureml_run_id":"ml-0001"} J"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Finally, create a `validate_bundle.sh` script that ensures required files exist, are non-empty, and metrics.json is valid JSON. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh << 'SH' #!/bin/bash set -e files=(outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json) for f in "${files[@]}"; do if [[ ! -s "$f" ]]; then echo "Missing or empty $f"; exit 1; fi done jq . outputs/metrics.json >/dev/null || { echo "metrics.json invalid"; exit 1; } echo "All required files present and valid" SH chmod +x /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh && bash /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"All required files present and valid ","stderr":""} Everything is now complete: contract doc, CSV, schemas, sample artifacts, validation script, and Azure CLI snippets. I’ll now summarize in the `` block. **Synthetic Trajectory — COMPLETE** Task “Azure ML: Define the evaluation contract (required artifacts and metrics). Save eval_contract.md and a bundle_index.csv listing the expected files.” Outputs Created> docs/eval_contract.md — Full contract with artifacts, metrics, schemas, gates, CI mapping, MLflow & Azure CLI examples bundle_index.csv — Machine-readable index of all artifacts outputs/ — Sample predictions, metrics, metadata, schemas scripts/validate_bundle.sh — CI validation script JSON Schemas for predictions & labels Key Artifacts predictions.jsonl with proba distributions metrics.json with accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput run_metadata.json with model/data versioning, seed, run ID plots/ and diagnostics/ placeholders for ROC/PR curves, calibration, fairness Quality Gates Missing files → FAIL F1 < 0.6 → FAIL Calibration error > 0.1 → WARN Integration MLflow logging example for artifacts Azure CLI retrieval snippet for named output eval/ CI script ensuring artifacts + JSON validity This trajectory demonstrates a complete, reproducible AzureML evaluation contract ready for CI/CD pipelines and Responsible AI dashboards.

content.text: We need a deterministic, adjustable simulation to model a queue processor with concurrency guards, weighted fairness, token-bucket rate limiting, exponential backoff with full jitter, visibility timeouts/leases, transient vs hard validation errors, dead-letter behavior, and dashboards. I'll scaffold a Python simulation in /workspace, then iterate: implement core entities, scheduler, token bucket + concurrency, backoff, leases, de-dup, metrics aggregation, and scenarios to demonstrate acceptance criteria. First, create project structure and a starter simulation.py. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/queue_retry_sim && ls -la /workspace/queue_retry_sim"}

First step: scaffold the folder structure & empty files. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/{docs,outputs,schemas,scripts} && touch /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv && ls -R /workspace/azureml_eval_contract"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"/workspace/azureml_eval_contract: bundle_index.csv docs outputs schemas scripts /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs: eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/schemas: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts: ","stderr":""} Now I will draft the initial version of `eval_contract.md`. This will be long and detailed: Sections: Scope & purpose Required artifacts & optional diagnostics File schemas (predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json) Metric definitions (accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency p95, throughput QPS) Reproducibility metadata (model commit, data version, seeds, environment) Quality gates (fail/warn conditions) CI mapping to AzureML named outputs Checklist for CI validation MLflow logging example snippet Azure CLI retrieval snippet I will write a very verbose first draft to ensure enough tokens and detail. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md << 'MD' # Azure ML Evaluation Contract ## 1. Scope & Purpose This document defines the evaluation contract for Azure Machine Learning model evaluation jobs. It specifies all artifacts, metrics, schemas, and quality gates required for consistent, reproducible, and automated model evaluation across environments. The goal: given (model, dataset, seed) → produce deterministic outputs enabling: - Automated CI/CD validation - Reproducibility across AzureML runs - Integration with Responsible AI dashboards, MLflow, and custom pipelines ## 2. Required Artifacts All artifacts must be written under outputs/ (or named output eval/). ### 2.1 Required 1. predictions.jsonl → One JSON object per prediction with: id, y_true, y_pred, optional proba dict. 2. labels.jsonl → Ground-truth labels if not embedded in predictions. 3. metrics.json → Aggregate metrics + per-slice metrics. 4. run_metadata.json → Model/data version, commit hashes, seed, runtime env. 5. schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. ### 2.2 Recommended - plots/ → confusion_matrix.png, roc_curve.png, pr_curve.png - diagnostics/ → calibration.json, fairness.json - slices.json → cohort definitions for per-slice metrics ## 3. File Schemas ### 3.1 predictions.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "|null", "y_pred": "", "proba": {"class1": 0.1, "class2": 0.9} } Constraints: - id unique per row - proba keys sum to 1.0 ± 1e-6 if provided - y_pred in class set defined in metadata ### 3.2 labels.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "" } ### 3.3 metrics.json Top-level keys: tafake_key_type, overall, per_class, slices { "tafake_key_type": "classification", "overall": { "accuracy": 0.95, "precision_macro": 0.94, "recall_macro": 0.93, "f1_macro": 0.935, "auroc_macro": 0.97, "pr_auc_macro": 0.96, "latency_p95_ms": 42.1, "throughput_qps": 250.0 }, "per_class": {"spam": {"precision": 0.9, "recall": 0.8, "f1": 0.85}}, "slices": {"slice1": {"f1_macro": 0.8}} } ## 4. Metric Definitions - Accuracy = correct / total - Precision/Recall/F1 macro = unweighted mean across classes - AUROC = one-vs-rest or macro-average - PR-AUC = macro-average precision–recall curve area - Latency p95 = 95th percentile inference latency (ms) - Throughput QPS = queries per second under steady load ## 5. Reproducibility & Metadata - Log model commit, data asset version, eval seed - Log runtime env: Python, CUDA, AzureML image - Deterministic seeds for metrics requiring randomness ## 6. Quality Gates - Missing required artifacts → FAIL - f1_macro < 0.6 → FAIL - Calibration ECE > 0.1 → WARN ## 7. CI Mapping to AzureML Named Outputs - Named output: eval/ → entire outputs folder - bundle_index.csv enumerates all expected files ## 8. Checklist - [ ] predictions.jsonl present - [ ] metrics.json present - [ ] run_metadata.json includes model/data version - [ ] schemas present - [ ] quality gates evaluated ## 9. MLflow Logging Example python import mlflow with mlflow.start_run(): mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/predictions.jsonl", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/metrics.json", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/run_metadata.json", artifact_path="eval") ## 10. Azure CLI Retrieval bash az ml job show --name --resource-group --workspace-name --query outputs.eval MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Next, I’ll create `bundle_index.csv` enumerating all expected files with columns: filepath, description, required, mime_type, example. This CSV will be machine-readable by CI pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv << 'CSV' filepath,description,required,mime_type,example outputs/predictions.jsonl,Per-row predictions,true,application/jsonl,outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/labels.jsonl,Ground-truth labels,conditional,application/jsonl,outputs/labels.jsonl outputs/metrics.json,Aggregate metrics,true,application/json,outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json,Traceability metadata,true,application/json,outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json,Prediction schema,true,application/json,outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json,Labels schema,conditional,application/json,outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png,Confusion matrix,recommended,image/png,outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json,Calibration metrics,optional,application/json,outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json CSV"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Now I’ll generate JSON Schemas for predictions & labels to enforce structure. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "PredictionRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_pred"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "null"]}, "y_pred": {"type": ["string", "number"]}, "proba": { "type": ["object", "null"], "additionalProperties": {"type": "number"} } } } JSON && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "LabelRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_true"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "number"]} } } JSON"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} I will now create sample outputs for predictions, labels, metrics, and run_metadata to make the contract demonstrable in CI/CD pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/predictions.jsonl << 'J' {"id":"row1","y_true":"spam","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.9,"ham":0.1}} {"id":"row2","y_true":"ham","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.6,"ham":0.4}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/metrics.json << 'J' {"tafake_key_type":"classification","overall":{"accuracy":0.5,"f1_macro":0.5,"precision_macro":0.5,"recall_macro":0.5,"auroc_macro":0.6,"pr_auc_macro":0.55,"latency_p95_ms":42.5,"throughput_qps":200.0}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/run_metadata.json << 'J' {"model_name":"spam-detector","model_version":"1.0.0","data_version":"2025-09-26","seed":1234,"azureml_run_id":"ml-0001"} J"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Finally, create a `validate_bundle.sh` script that ensures required files exist, are non-empty, and metrics.json is valid JSON. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh << 'SH' #!/bin/bash set -e files=(outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json) for f in "${files[@]}"; do if [[ ! -s "$f" ]]; then echo "Missing or empty $f"; exit 1; fi done jq . outputs/metrics.json >/dev/null || { echo "metrics.json invalid"; exit 1; } echo "All required files present and valid" SH chmod +x /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh && bash /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"All required files present and valid ","stderr":""} Everything is now complete: contract doc, CSV, schemas, sample artifacts, validation script, and Azure CLI snippets. I’ll now summarize in the `` block. **Synthetic Trajectory — COMPLETE** Task “Azure ML: Define the evaluation contract (required artifacts and metrics). Save eval_contract.md and a bundle_index.csv listing the expected files.” Outputs Created> docs/eval_contract.md — Full contract with artifacts, metrics, schemas, gates, CI mapping, MLflow & Azure CLI examples bundle_index.csv — Machine-readable index of all artifacts outputs/ — Sample predictions, metrics, metadata, schemas scripts/validate_bundle.sh — CI validation script JSON Schemas for predictions & labels Key Artifacts predictions.jsonl with proba distributions metrics.json with accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput run_metadata.json with model/data versioning, seed, run ID plots/ and diagnostics/ placeholders for ROC/PR curves, calibration, fairness Quality Gates Missing files → FAIL F1 < 0.6 → FAIL Calibration error > 0.1 → WARN Integration MLflow logging example for artifacts Azure CLI retrieval snippet for named output eval/ CI script ensuring artifacts + JSON validity This trajectory demonstrates a complete, reproducible AzureML evaluation contract ready for CI/CD pipelines and Responsible AI dashboards.

content.text: Plan: Build a deterministic, composable Python data-quality gate for synthetic financial transactions. Steps: 1) Scaffold project structure under /workspace/data_quality_gate with src code, config, scripts, state, and outputs. 2) Author a strict schema and rule configuration (currencies, channels, severity, thresholds, sliding window, batch size, seed, top-N). 3) Implement gate core (checks registry, per-rule violations, top-N offenders with reasons and remediation, final decision logic). 4) Implement profiler (completeness, uniqueness, min/max, top-K). 5) Add stateful sliding-window uniqueness using a JSON state file. 6) Provide synthetic data generator with fixed seed. 7) Run baseline PASS scenario; save outputs. 8) Demonstrate tightening domain list increases violations (WARN/BLOCK). 9) Inject out-of-range amount to flip PASS → BLOCK. 10) Summarize outputs and integration hints in final solution. Proceed to scaffold files. {"type":"code","code_command":"set -e mkdir -p /workspace/data_quality_gate/{src/gate,config,scripts,outputs,state} printf '%s ' 'Python>=3.10' > /workspace/data_quality_gate/requirements.txt cat > /workspace/data_quality_gate/README.md << 'MD' Data Quality Gate — Synthetic Financial Transactions Features - Strict schema: id (str), ts (ISO-8601), currencyCode (str), amount (float >= 0), channel (str) - Rule families: • Schema/type validation (BLOCK) • Required field completeness (BLOCK) • Domain checks: ISO currency subset and allowed channels, amount bounds (BLOCK/WARN configurable) • Referential sanity: id uniqueness within sliding window (BLOCK) • Lightweight consistency: non-negative daily sum (WARN), no future timestamps (BLOCK) - Deterministic decision: PASS/WARN/BLOCK with per-rule counts & top-N offenders - Compact column profile: completeness, uniqueness, min/max, top-K categories - Composable checks: toggle and extend via config without code restructure - Deterministic performance: fixed batch size and seeds CLI python -m gate.gate --input transactions.csv --config config/gate_config.yaml --outputs outputs --state state --seed 123 --batch-size 1000 Outputs - outputs/decision.json — {"decision":"PASS|WARN|BLOCK","summary":{...}} - outputs/violations.json — per-rule counts, top-N offenders with reasons and remediation - outputs/profiles.json — column profiles (completeness, uniqueness, min/max, top-K) - outputs/ledger.csv — flat ledger of violations by record - outputs/run_metadata.json — run details for determinism and reproducibility Acceptance - Replaying same seed yields identical decisions - Tightening domain lists predictably increases violations - Injecting an out-of-range amount flips decision from PASS to BLOCK MD"}

content.text: Plan: Create a small, deterministic, dependency-free Python package that: - Generates synthetic transactions. - Implements a leakage-proof, point-in-time feature retriever with strict cutoff enforcement. - Provides an atomic interface returning features and provenance. - Implements a fixed-coefficient logistic scorer. - Runs a demo producing: feature definitions, sample rows, 10 predictions, and a leakage-proof table. Also validate edge case of cutoff before first event. Steps: 1) Scaffold folders and files. 2) Implement core library (pt_features.py). 3) Implement demo runner (run_demo.py) that prints results and writes CSVs. 4) Execute demo and capture outputs. 5) Summarize results and SLA in the final solution. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/pt_features/src /workspace/pt_features/outputs && ls -R /workspace/pt_features"}

body.instructions: SYSTEM PROMPT — Offline Trajectory Agent (Tools + Synthetic Results) Objective You are a helpful agent that constructs an execution trajectory to complete a given task. You operate in iterative steps with explicit tool calls and simulated results. Allowed Tags (only these) - ... — plan the single next action. - ... — search/read official docs, flags, troubleshooting. - ... — write/build/test inside /workspace (shell commands). - ... — Azure CLI (whitelisted az subcommands only). - ... — final answer for the user (or a single clarifying question). > Strict Tool Call JSON (mandatory; exactly one object; no extra keys/fields/comments) - {"type":"web","q":"non-empty search terms","k":INTEGER 1..10} - {"type":"code","code_command":"non-empty shell command"} - {"type":"azure","azure_command":"non-empty az subcommand"} Turn Protocol (strict) 1) Emit that decides EXACTLY ONE next action (why + which tool or finish). 2) After , emit EITHER: • exactly one tool call (//) and nothing else, OR • a single … if finished or you must ask ONE clarifying question. 3) Wait for the tool RESULT before taking another action. • Your very next turn after a tool call must be a synthetic result: [web-result|code-result|azure-result] {JSON payload} (No other text on that turn.) 4) Repeat think → tool → tool_result until done; end with a single . Offline Result Synthesis (you must fabricate plausible outputs) - Web success: [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"...","url":"..."}],"summary":"..."} } - Web error: [web-error] "reason" - Code success: [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"...","stderr":""} - Code error: [code-error] "reason" - Azure success: [azure-result] {"message":"ok","data":{ /* az JSON */ }} - Azure error: [azure-error] "reason" Decision Policy - Prefer tools over guessing. If flags/steps are uncertain, FIRST use . - Implement → verify loop: • After builds/tests, verify via another (tests/artifacts). • After changes, confirm with read-only list/show in . - Least privilege & idempotence. Avoid destructive ops unless explicitly requested. - Do NOT run Azure inside . Do NOT curl docs in if exists. - One tool call per turn. Do not mix tools in a single turn. Trajectory Quality - Be explicit, granular, and long enough to be actionable (typically 8–20 iterations). - Use environment-safe placeholders for secrets (e.g., ). - Keep concise but concrete (what to do next and why). - In , summarize what was done, surface final artifacts/paths/ports, and list next steps or follow-ups. MAKE SURE THAT THE TRAJECTORIES ARE ATLEAST 20,000-30,000 TOKENS LONG. You have to produce long correct trajectories for the agent to learn. THIS IS AN EXAMPLE TRAJECTORY: First, I need to ground myself in what Azure ML evaluation contracts typically include. I recall from experience that model evaluation in Azure ML uses a combination of MLflow logging, named outputs, and Responsible AI dashboards. But I want to double-check official references to ensure my artifact list (predictions, metrics, plots, diagnostics) aligns with AzureML conventions, so the contract we produce will be realistic. Key things to confirm from docs: Required vs optional artifacts for classification & regression Metrics schemas (macro F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput) Output folder structures (e.g., outputs/ vs named output like eval/) Any AzureML schema JSON templates I can mimic I will do a search with multiple AzureML docs references to ground the contract before drafting anything. {"type":"web","q":"Azure Machine Learning evaluation metrics artifacts outputs MLflow Responsible AI dashboards classification regression metrics latency throughput calibration fairness azureml", "k": 8} [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"Evaluate model performance in Azure ML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-evaluate-model-performance"},{"title":"Responsible AI metrics & dashboards in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-responsible-ai-dashboard"},{"title":"Azure ML job outputs and MLflow artifact logging","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-track-experiments-log-metrics#log-and-upload-files"},{"title":"AzureML metrics reference (accuracy, precision, recall, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC)","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/reference-metrics-classification"},{"title":"AzureML latency and throughput benchmarking guidance","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-benchmark-inference"},{"title":"Data assets and JSONL schemas in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/concept-data#file-formats"}],"summary":"Azure ML docs confirm standard eval artifacts include predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json, optional plots (ROC, PR, confusion matrix), calibration & fairness diagnostics, run metadata, JSON schemas for predictions & labels, and MLflow logging for artifact versioning. Named output 'eval/' is recommended for CI/CD reproducibility."}} Great, now I have confirmation of required artifacts and naming conventions. Next, I'll scaffold a local workspace: /docs/eval_contract.md → the main contract file with required/optional artifacts, metrics, schemas, thresholds, reproducibility, naming, CI mapping. /bundle_index.csv → machine-readable index listing all expected files, descriptions, required flags, MIME types, and example paths. /outputs/ → sample compliant artifacts (predictions.jsonl, metrics.json, run_metadata.json). /schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. /scripts/validate_bundle.sh → CI validation script checking presence & format. First step: scaffold the folder structure & empty files. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/{docs,outputs,schemas,scripts} && touch /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv && ls -R /workspace/azureml_eval_contract"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"/workspace/azureml_eval_contract: bundle_index.csv docs outputs schemas scripts /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs: eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/schemas: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts: ","stderr":""} Now I will draft the initial version of `eval_contract.md`. This will be long and detailed: Sections: Scope & purpose Required artifacts & optional diagnostics File schemas (predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json) Metric definitions (accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency p95, throughput QPS) Reproducibility metadata (model commit, data version, seeds, environment) Quality gates (fail/warn conditions) CI mapping to AzureML named outputs Checklist for CI validation MLflow logging example snippet Azure CLI retrieval snippet I will write a very verbose first draft to ensure enough tokens and detail. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md << 'MD' # Azure ML Evaluation Contract ## 1. Scope & Purpose This document defines the evaluation contract for Azure Machine Learning model evaluation jobs. It specifies all artifacts, metrics, schemas, and quality gates required for consistent, reproducible, and automated model evaluation across environments. The goal: given (model, dataset, seed) → produce deterministic outputs enabling: - Automated CI/CD validation - Reproducibility across AzureML runs - Integration with Responsible AI dashboards, MLflow, and custom pipelines ## 2. Required Artifacts All artifacts must be written under outputs/ (or named output eval/). ### 2.1 Required 1. predictions.jsonl → One JSON object per prediction with: id, y_true, y_pred, optional proba dict. 2. labels.jsonl → Ground-truth labels if not embedded in predictions. 3. metrics.json → Aggregate metrics + per-slice metrics. 4. run_metadata.json → Model/data version, commit hashes, seed, runtime env. 5. schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. ### 2.2 Recommended - plots/ → confusion_matrix.png, roc_curve.png, pr_curve.png - diagnostics/ → calibration.json, fairness.json - slices.json → cohort definitions for per-slice metrics ## 3. File Schemas ### 3.1 predictions.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "|null", "y_pred": "", "proba": {"class1": 0.1, "class2": 0.9} } Constraints: - id unique per row - proba keys sum to 1.0 ± 1e-6 if provided - y_pred in class set defined in metadata ### 3.2 labels.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "" } ### 3.3 metrics.json Top-level keys: tafake_key_type, overall, per_class, slices { "tafake_key_type": "classification", "overall": { "accuracy": 0.95, "precision_macro": 0.94, "recall_macro": 0.93, "f1_macro": 0.935, "auroc_macro": 0.97, "pr_auc_macro": 0.96, "latency_p95_ms": 42.1, "throughput_qps": 250.0 }, "per_class": {"spam": {"precision": 0.9, "recall": 0.8, "f1": 0.85}}, "slices": {"slice1": {"f1_macro": 0.8}} } ## 4. Metric Definitions - Accuracy = correct / total - Precision/Recall/F1 macro = unweighted mean across classes - AUROC = one-vs-rest or macro-average - PR-AUC = macro-average precision–recall curve area - Latency p95 = 95th percentile inference latency (ms) - Throughput QPS = queries per second under steady load ## 5. Reproducibility & Metadata - Log model commit, data asset version, eval seed - Log runtime env: Python, CUDA, AzureML image - Deterministic seeds for metrics requiring randomness ## 6. Quality Gates - Missing required artifacts → FAIL - f1_macro < 0.6 → FAIL - Calibration ECE > 0.1 → WARN ## 7. CI Mapping to AzureML Named Outputs - Named output: eval/ → entire outputs folder - bundle_index.csv enumerates all expected files ## 8. Checklist - [ ] predictions.jsonl present - [ ] metrics.json present - [ ] run_metadata.json includes model/data version - [ ] schemas present - [ ] quality gates evaluated ## 9. MLflow Logging Example python import mlflow with mlflow.start_run(): mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/predictions.jsonl", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/metrics.json", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/run_metadata.json", artifact_path="eval") ## 10. Azure CLI Retrieval bash az ml job show --name --resource-group --workspace-name --query outputs.eval MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Next, I’ll create `bundle_index.csv` enumerating all expected files with columns: filepath, description, required, mime_type, example. This CSV will be machine-readable by CI pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv << 'CSV' filepath,description,required,mime_type,example outputs/predictions.jsonl,Per-row predictions,true,application/jsonl,outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/labels.jsonl,Ground-truth labels,conditional,application/jsonl,outputs/labels.jsonl outputs/metrics.json,Aggregate metrics,true,application/json,outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json,Traceability metadata,true,application/json,outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json,Prediction schema,true,application/json,outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json,Labels schema,conditional,application/json,outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png,Confusion matrix,recommended,image/png,outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json,Calibration metrics,optional,application/json,outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json CSV"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Now I’ll generate ["id", "y_pred"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "null"]}, "y_pred": {"type": ["string", "number"]}, "proba": { "type": ["object", "null"], "additionalProperties": {"type": "number"} } } } JSON && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": {"id":"row2","y_true":"ham","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.6,"ham":0.4}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/metrics.json << 'J' content.text: I'll quickly ground on canonical circuit-breaker and bulkhead semantics (half-open probes, rolling windows, bounded queues) to align implementation with established patterns before coding. {"type":"web","q":"circuit breaker pattern half-open rolling window bulkhead pattern bounded queue p95 latency thresholds best practices", "k": 6} [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"Circuit Breaker pattern","url":"https://martinfowler.com/bliki/CircuitBreaker.html"},{"title":"Bulkhead pattern","url":"https://docs.microsoft.com/azure/architecture/patterns/bulkhead"},{"title":"Polly .NET Circuit Breaker & Bulkhead","url":"https://www.thepollyproject.org/strategies/circuit-breaker"},{"title":"Timeouts, retries, and backoff","url":"https://aws.amazon.com/builders-library/timeouts-retries-and-backoff-with-jitter/"},{"title":"Resilience patterns overview","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/architecture/framework/resiliency/design-patterns"},{"title":"Latency SLOs and tail at scale","url":"https://cacm.acm.org/magazines/2013/2/160173-the-tail-at-scale/fulltext"}],"summary":"Circuit breakers trip on failures or response-time thresholds, transition Open to block requests, then Half-Open to probe with limited concurrency before closing on success or re-opening on failure. Bulkheads limit concurrent calls per dependency and bound waiting to avoid head-of-line blocking; excess requests fail fast. Rolling windows commonly use sliding time windows or sample windows (e.g., last N calls) to compute error rates and p95 latency. Jitter/backoff is recommended to avoid synchronized reopen storms."}} Now I'll scaffold a deterministic Python simulation project with configurable runtime, implementing circuits, bulkheads, synthetic downstreams (payments, catalog, profile), and a router that fans out then aggregates. I'll create directories and files. {"type":"code","code_command":"set -e mkdir -p /workspace/cb_bulkhead_sim/{src,outputs,docs} printf '%s\n' '# Circuit Breaker + Bulkhead Simulation' > /workspace/cb_bulkhead_sim/README.md ls -la /workspace/cb_bulkhead_sim"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"total 16 drwxr-xr-x 4 root root 4096 Sep 28 00:00 . drwxr-xr-x 3 root root 4096 Sep 28 00:00 .. drwxr-xr-x 2 root root 4096 Sep 28 00:00 docs drwxr-xr-x 2 root root 4096 Sep 28 00:00 src -rw-r--r-- 1 root root 39 Sep 28 00:00 README.md -rwxr-xr-x 1 root root 0 Sep 28 00:00 outputs ","stderr":""} I'll write the main simulation script with classes: RollingStats, CircuitBreaker, Bulkhead, Downstream, Router, plus CLI config handling. It will output time series CSV/JSON and a degraded sample. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/cb_bulkhead_sim/src/sim.py << 'PY' import asyncio import random import time import statistics import json import argparse from collections import deque, defaultdict from typing import Deque, List, Dict, Any, Tuple # States for CircuitBreaker CLOSED = "closed" OPEN = "open" HALF_OPEN = "half_open" class RollingStats: def __init__(self, maxlen:int=200): self.maxlen = maxlen self.samples: Deque[Tuple[float, bool]] = deque(maxlen=maxlen) # (latency_ms, success) self.consecutive_failures = 0 def add(self, latency_ms: float, success: bool): self.samples.append((latency_ms, success)) if success: self.consecutive_failures = 0 else: self.consecutive_failures += 1 def p95_latency(self) -> float: if not self.samples: return 0.0 latencies = sorted([lat for lat, _ in self.samples]) if not latencies: return 0.0 k = max(0, int(0.95 * (len(latencies)-1))) return latencies[k] def failure_rate(self) -> float: if not self.samples: return 0.0 fails = sum(1 for _, ok in self.samples if not ok) return fails / len(self.samples) class CircuitBreaker: def __init__(self, name: str, cfg: Dict[str, Any]): self.name = name self.state = CLOSED self.stats = RollingStats(maxlen=cfg.get('rolling_window', 200)) self.failure_threshold = cfg.get('consecutive_failures_to_open', 5) self.p95_latency_threshold_ms = cfg.get('p95_latency_threshold_ms', 250.0) self.open_cooldown_ms = cfg.get('open_cooldown_ms', 3000) self.half_open_max_concurrent = cfg.get('half_open_max_concurrent', 2) self.half_open_successes_to_close = cfg.get('half_open_successes_to_close', 5) self.half_open_failures_to_reopen = cfg.get('half_open_failures_to_reopen', 1) self._opened_at_ms = None self._half_open_sema = asyncio.Semaphore(self.half_open_max_concurrent) self._half_open_successes = 0 self._half_open_failures = 0 def _now_ms(self): return int(time.time() * 1000) def _can_probe_half_open(self) -> bool: return True def allow(self) -> Tuple[bool, str]: now = self._now_ms() if self.state == OPEN: assert self._opened_at_ms is not None if now - self._opened_at_ms >= self.open_cooldown_ms: # transition to half-open self.state = HALF_OPEN # reset probe counters self._half_open_successes = 0 self._half_open_failures = 0 self._half_open_sema = asyncio.Semaphore(self.half_open_max_concurrent) else: return False, "circuit_open" # Closed or Half-open return True, self.state async def acquire_half_open_slot(self, timeout_ms: int) -> bool: if self.state != HALF_OPEN: return True try: await asyncio.wait_for(self._half_open_sema.acquire(), timeout=timeout_ms/1000) return True except asyncio.TimeoutError: return False def release_half_open_slot(self): if self.state == HALF_OPEN: try: self._half_open_sema.release() except ValueError: pass def on_result(self, latency_ms: float, success: bool): self.stats.add(latency_ms, success) # check open criteria only if in closed if self.state == CLOSED: if self.stats.consecutive_failures >= self.failure_threshold: self.state = OPEN self._opened_at_ms = self._now_ms() elif self.stats.p95_latency() > self.p95_latency_threshold_ms: self.state = OPEN self._opened_at_ms = self._now_ms() elif self.state == HALF_OPEN: if success: self._half_open_successes += 1 if self._half_open_successes >= self.half_open_successes_to_close: self.state = CLOSED self._opened_at_ms = None else: self._half_open_failures += 1 if self._half_open_failures >= self.half_open_failures_to_reopen: self.state = OPEN self._opened_at_ms = self._now_ms() class Bulkhead: def __init__(self, name: str, max_concurrency: int, acquire_timeout_ms: int): self.name = name self.sema = asyncio.Semaphore(max_concurrency) self.acquire_timeout_ms = acquire_timeout_ms self.admitted = 0 self.rejected = 0 self.wait_timeouts = 0 async def try_acquire(self) -> Tuple[bool, str]: start = time.time() try: await asyncio.wait_for(self.sema.acquire(), timeout=self.acquire_timeout_ms/1000) self.admitted += 1 return True, "admitted" except asyncio.TimeoutError: self.rejected += 1 self.wait_timeouts += 1 return False, "bulkhead_wait_timeout" finally: pass def release(self): try: self.sema.release() except ValueError: pass class Downstream: def __init__(self, name: str, rng: random.Random, cfg: Dict[str, Any]): self.name = name self.rng = rng self.cfg = cfg async def call(self, req_id: int, t: int) -> Tuple[bool, float, Dict[str, Any]]: # Returns (success, latency_ms, payload) # Config supports: base_ms, jitter_ms, error_rate, spikes, tail_shape base = self.cfg.get('base_ms', 30) jitter = self.cfg.get('jitter_ms', 10) err_rate = self.cfg.get('error_rate', 0.01) latency_ms = base + self.rng.uniform(0, jitter) # spikes for payments spikes = self.cfg.get('spikes', []) # list of dicts {start,end,latency_boost_ms,err_boost} for sp in spikes: if sp['start'] <= t <= sp['end']: latency_ms += sp.get('latency_boost_ms', 200) err_rate = min(1.0, err_rate + sp.get('err_boost', 0.2)) # slow tail for profile if self.cfg.get('slow_tail', False): # Pareto-like heavy tail via 1/U^a u = max(1e-6, self.rng.random()) a = self.cfg.get('tail_alpha', 2.0) tail = (1.0 / (u ** (1.0 / a)) - 1.0) * self.cfg.get('tail_scale_ms', 20) latency_ms += tail await asyncio.sleep(latency_ms/1000) success = self.rng.random() >= err_rate payload = {"service": self.name, "ok": success, "latency_ms": round(latency_ms, 2)} if not success: payload["error"] = "synthetic_error" return success, latency_ms, payload class Router: def __init__(self, config: Dict[str, Any]): seed = config.get('seed', 42) self.rng = random.Random(seed) dep_cfg = config['dependencies'] # Downstreams self.downstreams = { 'payments': Downstream('payments', random.Random(seed+1), dep_cfg['payments']['downstream']), 'catalog': Downstream('catalog', random.Random(seed+2), dep_cfg['catalog']['downstream']), 'profile': Downstream('profile', random.Random(seed+3), dep_cfg['profile']['downstream']), } # Circuits self.circuits = { k: CircuitBreaker(k, dep_cfg[k]['circuit']) for k in self.downstreams.keys() } # Bulkheads self.bulkheads = { k: Bulkhead(k, dep_cfg[k]['bulkhead']['max_concurrency'], dep_cfg[k]['bulkhead']['acquire_timeout_ms']) for k in self.downstreams.keys() } self.degraded_sample = None self.state_series: List[Dict[str, Any]] = [] # per tick time series self.bulkhead_counts = defaultdict(lambda: {"admitted":0, "rejected":0}) self.end_to_end_latencies: List[float] = [] async def handle_request(self, req_id: int, t: int) -> Dict[str, Any]: start = time.time() results = {} degraded = [] async def call_dep(dep_name: str): cb = self.circuits[dep_name] bh = self.bulkheads[dep_name] allowed, reason = cb.allow() if not allowed: degraded.append({"dependency": dep_name, "reason": reason}) return dep_name, {"service": dep_name, "degraded": True, "reason": reason} # Half-open limited probes half_slot = True if cb.state == HALF_OPEN: half_slot = await cb.acquire_half_open_slot(timeout_ms=cb.open_cooldown_ms//4) if not half_slot: self.bulkhead_counts[dep_name]["rejected"] += 1 degraded.append({"dependency": dep_name, "reason": "half_open_probe_limit"}) return dep_name, {"service": dep_name, "degraded": True, "reason": "half_open_probe_limit"} # Bulkhead acquire ok, bh_reason = await bh.try_acquire() if not ok: self.bulkhead_counts[dep_name]["rejected"] += 1 cb.on_result(latency_ms=bh.acquire_timeout_ms, success=False) degraded.append({"dependency": dep_name, "reason": bh_reason}) if cb.state == HALF_OPEN and half_slot: cb.release_half_open_slot() return dep_name, {"service": dep_name, "degraded": True, "reason": bh_reason} self.bulkhead_counts[dep_name]["admitted"] += 1 try: success, latency_ms, payload = await self.downstreams[dep_name].call(req_id, t) cb.on_result(latency_ms=latency_ms, success=success) payload["degraded"] = False if not success: payload["reason"] = "downstream_error" return dep_name, payload finally: bh.release() if cb.state == HALF_OPEN and half_slot: cb.release_half_open_slot() deps = list(self.downstreams.keys()) # Fan out concurrently dep_tasks = [asyncio.create_task(call_dep(d)) for d in deps] for task in asyncio.as_completed(dep_tasks): name, payload = await task results[name] = payload end = time.time() e2e_ms = (end - start) * 1000 self.end_to_end_latencies.append(e2e_ms) response = { "request_id": req_id, "e2e_latency_ms": round(e2e_ms, 2), "results": results } if any(p.get("degraded", False) for p in results.values()): response["degraded_mode"] = True response["degraded_annotations"] = [r for r in results.values() if r.get("degraded")] if self.degraded_sample is None: self.degraded_sample = response else: response["degraded_mode"] = False return response def capture_state(self, tick: int): self.state_series.append({ "tick": tick, **{f"{k}_state": self.circuits[k].state for k in self.circuits.keys()}, **{f"{k}_p95": round(self.circuits[k].stats.p95_latency(),2) for k in self.circuits.keys()}, **{f"{k}_cons_fail": self.circuits[k].stats.consecutive_failures for k in self.circuits.keys()}, }) async def run_sim(config: Dict[str, Any]): router = Router(config) total_requests = config.get('total_requests', 400) concurrency = config.get('client_concurrency', 8) tick_interval_ms = config.get('tick_interval_ms', 50) async def produce_requests(): sem = asyncio.Semaphore(concurrency) responses = [] t = 0 for req_id in range(total_requests): await asyncio.sleep(tick_interval_ms/1000) router.capture_state(t) t += tick_interval_ms await sem.acquire() async def _one(rid=req_id, tt=t): try: return await router.handle_request(rid, tt) finally: sem.release() responses.append(asyncio.create_task(_one())) # Wait for all results = await asyncio.gather(*responses) return router, results router, responses = await produce_requests() # Outputs outdir = config.get('output_dir', './outputs') # time series with open(f"{outdir}/circuit_states_timeseries.jsonl", "w") as f: for row in router.state_series: f.write(json.dumps(row)+"\n") # bulkhead counts bh_summary = {k: {"admitted": v["admitted"], "rejected": v["rejected"]} for k,v in router.bulkhead_counts.items()} with open(f"{outdir}/bulkhead_counts.json", "w") as f: json.dump(bh_summary, f, indent=2) # e2e stats p50 = statistics.median(sorted(router.end_to_end_latencies)) if router.end_to_end_latencies else 0.0 lats_sorted = sorted(router.end_to_end_latencies) p95 = lats_sorted[int(0.95*(len(lats_sorted)-1))] if lats_sorted else 0.0 summary = { "e2e_p50_ms": round(p50,2), "e2e_p95_ms": round(p95,2), "requests": len(router.end_to_end_latencies) } with open(f"{outdir}/summary.json", "w") as f: json.dump(summary, f, indent=2) # degraded sample if router.degraded_sample: with open(f"{outdir}/degraded_sample.json", "w") as f: json.dump(router.degraded_sample, f, indent=2) # Also emit a brief ops digest ops_digest = { "tuning": { "note": "Adjust consecutive_failures_to_open and p95_latency_threshold_ms per dependency; use longer rolling windows for stability; ensure open_cooldown_ms plus jitter to avoid synchronized reopen storms.", "risk": "Without jitter, half-open probes across replicas can cause synchronized reopen storms; add randomization to cooldown or probe concurrency limits." }, "circuits_final_states": {k: router.circuits[k].state for k in router.circuits.keys()} } with open(f"{outdir}/ops_digest.json", "w") as f: json.dump(ops_digest, f, indent=2) return outdir if __name__ == "__main__": parser = argparse.ArgumentParser() parser.add_argument('--config', type=str, default=None) args = parser.parse_args() default_config = { "seed": 1337, "total_requests": 500, "client_concurrency": 12, "tick_interval_ms": 30, "output_dir": "/workspace/cb_bulkhead_sim/outputs", "dependencies": { "payments": { "downstream": { "base_ms": 20, "jitter_ms": 10, "error_rate": 0.01, "spikes": [ {"start": 2000, "end": 2600, "latency_boost_ms": 300, "err_boost": 0.25}, {"start": 8000, "end": 8500, "latency_boost_ms": 250, "err_boost": 0.2} ] }, "circuit": { "rolling_window": 150, "consecutive_failures_to_open": 4, "p95_latency_threshold_ms": 220, "open_cooldown_ms": 1500, "half_open_max_concurrent": 2, "half_open_successes_to_close": 6, "half_open_failures_to_reopen": 1 }, "bulkhead": {"max_concurrency": 10, "acquire_timeout_ms": 150} }, "profile": { "downstream": { Prototype delivered: Circuit Breaker + Bulkhead simulator for API backends What I built - Deterministic, configurable Python simulation with: - Circuit breakers per dependency with rolling p95 latency and consecutive-failure thresholds, Open/Half-Open/Closed states, and limited half-open probes. - Bulkheads per dependency (concurrency caps with bounded wait). Requests exceeding wait fail fast with explicit reasons. - Synthetic downstreams: • payments: fast with seeded spikes (latency + error boosts). • catalog: steady. • profile: slow heavy tail. - Router that fans out per request to all three, aggregates responses, and returns partial results with degraded annotations when circuits are open. - Runtime knobs via JSON config; seeded RNG for repeatability. Where it lives - /workspace/cb_bulkhead_sim/src/sim.py — main simulator - /workspace/cb_bulkhead_sim/config.sample.json — sample runtime config - /workspace/cb_bulkhead_sim/README.md — quickstart and tuning tips - Outputs emitted to /workspace/cb_bulkhead_sim/outputs How to run - python3 /workspace/cb_bulkhead_sim/src/sim.py --config /workspace/cb_bulkhead_sim/config.sample.json Key outputs (from sample runs) - outputs/summary.json: end-to-end latency {"e2e_p50_ms": 125.41, "e2e_p95_ms": 452.13, "requests": 600} - outputs/bulkhead_counts.json: admitted vs rejected per bulkhead {"payments":{"admitted":585,"rejected":9},"catalog":{"admitted":593,"rejected":2},"profile":{"admitted":552,"rejected":29}} - outputs/circuit_states_timeseries.jsonl: time series of circuit states and p95s Example early and late rows show profile opening on high tail p95 and later half-open probing. - outputs/degraded_sample.json: degraded-mode response example Contains explicit degraded annotations, e.g. profile or payments “circuit_open” or “bulkhead_wait_timeout”. - outputs/ops_digest.json: brief ops digest with tuning notes and synchronized reopen risk. Acceptance checks - Circuits trip and recover as designed: - payments trips during seeded spikes and later recovers via half-open probes. - profile opens on tail p95 threshold; transitions to half-open; probe limits enforced. - Bulkheads isolate slow profile from starving payments/catalog: - Profile shows higher rejected counts under stress while payments/catalog continue to admit at high rates. - Degraded responses are explicit and consistent: - Aggregated responses include degraded_mode: true and per-dependency reasons (e.g., circuit_open, bulkhead_wait_timeout). - Deterministic and configurable: - All randomness seeded; change config JSON at runtime to explore sensitivity (total_requests, client_concurrency, thresholds, cooldowns, probe concurrency, bulkhead limits). Config knobs to explore (runtime) - per-dependency circuit: - rolling_window, consecutive_failures_to_open, p95_latency_threshold_ms, open_cooldown_ms, half_open_max_concurrent, half_open_successes_to_close. - per-dependency bulkhead: - max_concurrency, acquire_timeout_ms. - traffic: - total_requests, client_concurrency, tick_interval_ms, seed. Operations digest (brief) - Tuning: - Start with p95 thresholds aligned to SLOs; set consecutive_failures lower for critical paths (payments) and higher for steady ones (catalog). - Use larger rolling_window for stability if traffic is noisy; smaller window for fast reaction. - Half-open probe concurrency should be small (1–2) to avoid thundering-herd after outages. - Risk: synchronized reopen storms - Without jitter, multiple instances enter half-open simultaneously and overload dependencies. Add randomization to open_cooldown_ms and/or probe slots (e.g., ±20%) or exponential backoff with jitter to defuse coordinated bursts. Next steps - Add retry with backoff+jitter before circuit feedback to observe interactions. - Add per-request budgets and cooperative cancellation to cap e2e latency. - Emit Prometheus-like metrics and a small HTML sparkline to visualize state transitions. Artifacts you can inspect now - /workspace/cb_bulkhead_sim/outputs/circuit_states_timeseries.jsonl - /workspace/cb_bulkhead_sim/outputs/bulkhead_counts.json - /workspace/cb_bulkhead_sim/outputs/summary.json - /workspace/cb_bulkhead_sim/outputs/degraded_sample.json - /workspace/cb_bulkhead_sim/outputs/ops_digest.json If you want, I can tweak thresholds or seeds and rerun to show different sensitivity profiles (e.g., tighter payments p95 to trip earlier, smaller profile bulkhead to demonstrate stronger isolation).
