body.instructions: SYSTEM PROMPT — Offline Trajectory Agent (Tools + Synthetic Results) Objective You are a helpful agent that constructs an execution trajectory to complete a given task. You operate in iterative steps with explicit tool calls and simulated results. Allowed Tags (only these) - ... — plan the single next action. - ... — search/read official docs, flags, troubleshooting. - ... — write/build/test inside /workspace (shell commands). - ... — Azure CLI (whitelisted az subcommands only). - ... — final answer for the user (or a single clarifying question). > Strict Tool Call JSON (mandatory; exactly one object; no extra keys/fields/comments) - {"type":"web","q":"non-empty search terms","k":INTEGER 1..10} - {"type":"code","code_command":"non-empty shell command"} - {"type":"azure","azure_command":"non-empty az subcommand"} Turn Protocol (strict) 1) Emit that decides EXACTLY ONE next action (why + which tool or finish). 2) After , emit EITHER: • exactly one tool call (//) and nothing else, OR • a single … if finished or you must ask ONE clarifying question. 3) Wait for the tool RESULT before taking another action. • Your very next turn after a tool call must be a synthetic result: [web-result|code-result|azure-result] {JSON payload} (No other text on that turn.) 4) Repeat think → tool → tool_result until done; end with a single . Offline Result Synthesis (you must fabricate plausible outputs) - Web success: [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"...","url":"..."}],"summary":"..."} } - Web error: [web-error] "reason" - Code success: [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"...","stderr":""} - Code error: [code-error] "reason" - Azure success: [azure-result] {"message":"ok","data":{ /* az JSON */ }} - Azure error: [azure-error] "reason" Decision Policy - Prefer tools over guessing. If flags/steps are uncertain, FIRST use . - Implement → verify loop: • After builds/tests, verify via another (tests/artifacts). • After changes, confirm with read-only list/show in . - Least privilege & idempotence. Avoid destructive ops unless explicitly requested. - Do NOT run Azure inside . Do NOT curl docs in if exists. - One tool call per turn. Do not mix tools in a single turn. Trajectory Quality - Be explicit, granular, and long enough to be actionable (typically 8–20 iterations). - Use environment-safe placeholders for secrets (e.g., ). - Keep concise but concrete (what to do next and why). - In , summarize what was done, surface final artifacts/paths/ports, and list next steps or follow-ups. MAKE SURE THAT THE TRAJECTORIES ARE ATLEAST 20,000-30,000 TOKENS LONG. You have to produce long correct trajectories for the agent to learn. THIS IS AN EXAMPLE TRAJECTORY: First, I need to ground myself in what Azure ML evaluation contracts typically include. I recall from experience that model evaluation in Azure ML uses a combination of MLflow logging, named outputs, and Responsible AI dashboards. But I want to double-check official references to ensure my artifact list (predictions, metrics, plots, diagnostics) aligns with AzureML conventions, so the contract we produce will be realistic. Key things to confirm from docs: Required vs optional artifacts for classification & regression Metrics schemas (macro F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput) Output folder structures (e.g., outputs/ vs named output like eval/) Any AzureML schema JSON templates I can mimic I will do a search with multiple AzureML docs references to ground the contract before drafting anything. {"type":"web","q":"Azure Machine Learning evaluation metrics artifacts outputs MLflow Responsible AI dashboards classification regression metrics latency throughput calibration fairness azureml", "k": 8} [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"Evaluate model performance in Azure ML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-evaluate-model-performance"},{"title":"Responsible AI metrics & dashboards in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-responsible-ai-dashboard"},{"title":"Azure ML job outputs and MLflow artifact logging","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-track-experiments-log-metrics#log-and-upload-files"},{"title":"AzureML metrics reference (accuracy, precision, recall, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC)","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/reference-metrics-classification"},{"title":"AzureML latency and throughput benchmarking guidance","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-benchmark-inference"},{"title":"Data assets and JSONL schemas in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/concept-data#file-formats"}],"summary":"Azure ML docs confirm standard eval artifacts include predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json, optional plots (ROC, PR, confusion matrix), calibration & fairness diagnostics, run metadata, JSON schemas for predictions & labels, and MLflow logging for artifact versioning. Named output 'eval/' is recommended for CI/CD reproducibility."}} Great, now I have confirmation of required artifacts and naming conventions. Next, I'll scaffold a local workspace: /docs/eval_contract.md → the main contract file with required/optional artifacts, metrics, schemas, thresholds, reproducibility, naming, CI mapping. /bundle_index.csv → machine-readable index listing all expected files, descriptions, required flags, MIME types, and example paths. /outputs/ → sample compliant artifacts (predictions.jsonl, metrics.json, run_metadata.json). /schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. /scripts/validate_bundle.sh → CI validation script checking presence & format. First step: scaffold the folder structure & empty files. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/{docs,outputs,schemas,scripts} && touch /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv && ls -R /workspace/azureml_eval_contract"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"/workspace/azureml_eval_contract: bundle_index.csv docs outputs schemas scripts /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs: eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/schemas: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts: ","stderr":""} Now I will draft the initial version of `eval_contract.md`. This will be long and detailed: Sections: Scope & purpose Required artifacts & optional diagnostics File schemas (predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json) Metric definitions (accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency p95, throughput QPS) Reproducibility metadata (model commit, data version, seeds, environment) Quality gates (fail/warn conditions) CI mapping to AzureML named outputs Checklist for CI validation MLflow logging example snippet Azure CLI retrieval snippet I will write a very verbose first draft to ensure enough tokens and detail. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md << 'MD' # Azure ML Evaluation Contract ## 1. Scope & Purpose This document defines the evaluation contract for Azure Machine Learning model evaluation jobs. It specifies all artifacts, metrics, schemas, and quality gates required for consistent, reproducible, and automated model evaluation across environments. The goal: given (model, dataset, seed) → produce deterministic outputs enabling: - Automated CI/CD validation - Reproducibility across AzureML runs - Integration with Responsible AI dashboards, MLflow, and custom pipelines ## 2. Required Artifacts All artifacts must be written under outputs/ (or named output eval/). ### 2.1 Required 1. predictions.jsonl → One JSON object per prediction with: id, y_true, y_pred, optional proba dict. 2. labels.jsonl → Ground-truth labels if not embedded in predictions. 3. metrics.json → Aggregate metrics + per-slice metrics. 4. run_metadata.json → Model/data version, commit hashes, seed, runtime env. 5. schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. ### 2.2 Recommended - plots/ → confusion_matrix.png, roc_curve.png, pr_curve.png - diagnostics/ → calibration.json, fairness.json - slices.json → cohort definitions for per-slice metrics ## 3. File Schemas ### 3.1 predictions.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "|null", "y_pred": "", "proba": {"class1": 0.1, "class2": 0.9} } Constraints: - id unique per row - proba keys sum to 1.0 ± 1e-6 if provided - y_pred in class set defined in metadata ### 3.2 labels.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "" } ### 3.3 metrics.json Top-level keys: tafake_key_type, overall, per_class, slices { "tafake_key_type": "classification", "overall": { "accuracy": 0.95, "precision_macro": 0.94, "recall_macro": 0.93, "f1_macro": 0.935, "auroc_macro": 0.97, "pr_auc_macro": 0.96, "latency_p95_ms": 42.1, "throughput_qps": 250.0 }, "per_class": {"spam": {"precision": 0.9, "recall": 0.8, "f1": 0.85}}, "slices": {"slice1": {"f1_macro": 0.8}} } ## 4. Metric Definitions - Accuracy = correct / total - Precision/Recall/F1 macro = unweighted mean across classes - AUROC = one-vs-rest or macro-average - PR-AUC = macro-average precision–recall curve area - Latency p95 = 95th percentile inference latency (ms) - Throughput QPS = queries per second under steady load ## 5. Reproducibility & Metadata - Log model commit, data asset version, eval seed - Log runtime env: Python, CUDA, AzureML image - Deterministic seeds for metrics requiring randomness ## 6. Quality Gates - Missing required artifacts → FAIL - f1_macro < 0.6 → FAIL - Calibration ECE > 0.1 → WARN ## 7. CI Mapping to AzureML Named Outputs - Named output: eval/ → entire outputs folder - bundle_index.csv enumerates all expected files ## 8. Checklist - [ ] predictions.jsonl present - [ ] metrics.json present - [ ] run_metadata.json includes model/data version - [ ] schemas present - [ ] quality gates evaluated ## 9. MLflow Logging Example python import mlflow with mlflow.start_run(): mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/predictions.jsonl", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/metrics.json", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/run_metadata.json", artifact_path="eval") ## 10. Azure CLI Retrieval bash az ml job show --name --resource-group --workspace-name --query outputs.eval MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Next, I’ll create `bundle_index.csv` enumerating all expected files with columns: filepath, description, required, mime_type, example. This CSV will be machine-readable by CI pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv << 'CSV' filepath,description,required,mime_type,example outputs/predictions.jsonl,Per-row predictions,true,application/jsonl,outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/labels.jsonl,Ground-truth labels,conditional,application/jsonl,outputs/labels.jsonl outputs/metrics.json,Aggregate metrics,true,application/json,outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json,Traceability metadata,true,application/json,outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json,Prediction schema,true,application/json,outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json,Labels schema,conditional,application/json,outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png,Confusion matrix,recommended,image/png,outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json,Calibration metrics,optional,application/json,outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json CSV"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Now I’ll generate JSON Schemas for predictions & labels to enforce structure. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "PredictionRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_pred"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "null"]}, "y_pred": {"type": ["string", "number"]}, "proba": { "type": ["object", "null"], "additionalProperties": {"type": "number"} } } } JSON && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "LabelRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_true"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "number"]} } } JSON"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} I will now create sample outputs for predictions, labels, metrics, and run_metadata to make the contract demonstrable in CI/CD pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/predictions.jsonl << 'J' {"id":"row1","y_true":"spam","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.9,"ham":0.1}} {"id":"row2","y_true":"ham","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.6,"ham":0.4}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/metrics.json << 'J' {"tafake_key_type":"classification","overall":{"accuracy":0.5,"f1_macro":0.5,"precision_macro":0.5,"recall_macro":0.5,"auroc_macro":0.6,"pr_auc_macro":0.55,"latency_p95_ms":42.5,"throughput_qps":200.0}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/run_metadata.json << 'J' {"model_name":"spam-detector","model_version":"1.0.0","data_version":"2025-09-26","seed":1234,"azureml_run_id":"ml-0001"} J"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Finally, create a `validate_bundle.sh` script that ensures required files exist, are non-empty, and metrics.json is valid JSON. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh << 'SH' #!/bin/bash set -e files=(outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json) for f in "${files[@]}"; do if [[ ! -s "$f" ]]; then echo "Missing or empty $f"; exit 1; fi done jq . outputs/metrics.json >/dev/null || { echo "metrics.json invalid"; exit 1; } echo "All required files present and valid" SH chmod +x /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh && bash /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"All required files present and valid ","stderr":""} Everything is now complete: contract doc, CSV, schemas, sample artifacts, validation script, and Azure CLI snippets. I’ll now summarize in the `` block. **Synthetic Trajectory — COMPLETE** Task “Azure ML: Define the evaluation contract (required artifacts and metrics). Save eval_contract.md and a bundle_index.csv listing the expected files.” Outputs Created> docs/eval_contract.md — Full contract with artifacts, metrics, schemas, gates, CI mapping, MLflow & Azure CLI examples bundle_index.csv — Machine-readable index of all artifacts outputs/ — Sample predictions, metrics, metadata, schemas scripts/validate_bundle.sh — CI validation script JSON Schemas for predictions & labels Key Artifacts predictions.jsonl with proba distributions metrics.json with accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput run_metadata.json with model/data versioning, seed, run ID plots/ and diagnostics/ placeholders for ROC/PR curves, calibration, fairness Quality Gates Missing files → FAIL F1 < 0.6 → FAIL Calibration error > 0.1 → WARN Integration MLflow logging example for artifacts Azure CLI retrieval snippet for named output eval/ CI script ensuring artifacts + JSON validity This trajectory demonstrates a complete, reproducible AzureML evaluation contract ready for CI/CD pipelines and Responsible AI dashboards.

content.text: I'll implement a small deterministic state machine simulator in Python that models SAML/OIDC SSO success and failure paths, including CSRF protection via signed state/RelayState and end-to-end latency tracking. First, scaffold a workspace with code, docs, outputs, and tests, and write the implementation plus a CLI to run scenarios. {"type":"code","code_command":"set -euo pipefail mkdir -p /workspace/sso_flow/{src,docs,outputs,tests} cat > /workspace/sso_flow/src/signer.py << 'PY' from __future__ import annotations import hmac import hashlib from typing import Union class Signer: """ HMAC-SHA256 signer for deterministic signing/verification of small payloads. """ def __init__(self, secret: Union[str, bytes]): self.secret = secret.encode() if isinstance(secret, str) else secret def sign(self, data: Union[str, bytes]) -> str: b = data.encode() if isinstance(data, str) else data return hmac.new(self.secret, b, hashlib.sha256).hexdigest() def verify(self, data: Union[str, bytes], sig_hex: str) -> bool: expected = self.sign(data) # constant-time compare return hmac.compare_digest(expected, sig_hex) PY cat > /workspace/sso_flow/src/sso_flow.py << 'PY' from __future__ import annotations import json from dataclasses import dataclass, asdict from typing import List, Dict, Optional, Literal, Any, Tuple from hashlib import sha256 from time import monotonic from pathlib import Path from .signer import Signer State = Literal[ "UNAUTHENTICATED", "REDIRECT_TO_IDP", "RECEIVE_ASSERTION_OR_TOKEN", "VALIDATE_AND_CREATE_SESSION", "AUTHENTICATED", "DENIED", ] @dataclass class Event: step: str state_from: State state_to: State start_ts_ms: int end_ts_ms: int duration_ms: int notes: str @dataclass class FlowResult: protocol: Literal["oidc", "saml"] success: bool final_state: State session_id: Optional[str] session_created_at_ms: Optional[int] deny_reason: Optional[str] timeline: List[Event] total_latency_ms: int class DeterministicClock: """ Simple monotonic-like deterministic clock advancing by known deltas. """ def __init__(self, start_ms: int = 1_700_000_000_000): self.now_ms = start_ms def advance(self, delta_ms: int) -> Tuple[int, int]: start = self.now_ms self.now_ms += delta_ms return start, self.now_ms class SSOFlow: def __init__(self, secret: str = "super-secret-hmac-key"): self.signer = Signer(secret) def _deterministic_id(self, *parts: str) -> str: h = sha256() for p in parts: h.update(p.encode()) return h.hexdigest()[:16] def _issue_request(self, protocol: str, req_id: str, clock: DeterministicClock, csrf_state: str) -> Dict[str, Any]: # Redirect parameters include signed state/RelayState to prevent CSRF. # Deterministic timing for request creation + redirect issuance. start, end = clock.advance(5) # 5ms to assemble request if protocol == "oidc": payload = { "response_type": "code", "client_id": "rp-123", "redirect_uri": "https://rp.example.com/callback", "scope": "openid profile email", "state": csrf_state, "state_sig": self.signer.sign(csrf_state), "nonce": req_id, } else: payload = { "SAMLRequest": f"", "RelayState": csrf_state, "RelayStateSig": self.signer.sign(csrf_state), "AssertionConsumerServiceURL": "https://sp.example.com/acs", } return { "event_meta": (start, end, 5, "Issued redirect with signed CSRF parameter"), "payload": payload, } def _idp_issue_response(self, protocol: str, req_id: str, sub: str, clock: DeterministicClock, csrf_state: str, tamper_csrf: bool, invalid_token_sig: bool, expired: bool) -> Dict[str, Any]: # Deterministic network + IdP processing # advance 30ms network to IdP, 120ms IdP auth, 50ms callback network clock.advance(30) start_proc, end_proc = clock.advance(120) _ = (start_proc, end_proc) clock.advance(50) # Construct token/assertion if protocol == "oidc": # Minimal token structure (not a real JWT, but signed deterministically) payload = { "iss": "https://idp.example.com", "aud": "rp-123", "sub": sub, "iat": clock.now_ms - 1000, "exp": clock.now_ms - 1 if expired else clock.now_ms + 30_000, "nonce": req_id, } serialized = json.dumps(payload, sort_keys=True) sig = self.signer.sign(serialized) if invalid_token_sig: sig = "0" * len(sig) state = csrf_state if not tamper_csrf else (csrf_state + "-tampered") state_sig = self.signer.sign(state) if not tamper_csrf else self.signer.sign("WRONG") return { "payload": { "token_payload": payload, "token_sig": sig, "state": state, "state_sig": state_sig, } } else: assertion = { "Issuer": "https://idp.example.com", "Subject": sub, "Audience": "sp-123", "IssueInstant": clock.now_ms - 1000, "NotOnOrAfter": clock.now_ms - 1 if expired else clock.now_ms + 30_000, "InResponseTo": req_id, } serialized = json.dumps(assertion, sort_keys=True) sig = self.signer.sign(serialized) if invalid_token_sig: sig = "deadbeef" * 8 # wrong length but fine for our check relay_state = csrf_state if not tamper_csrf else (csrf_state + "-tampered") relay_sig = self.signer.sign(relay_state) if not tamper_csrf else self.signer.sign("WRONG") return { "payload": { "assertion": assertion, "assertion_sig": sig, "RelayState": relay_state, "RelayStateSig": relay_sig, } } def _validate_and_create_session(self, protocol: str, req_id: str, callback_payload: Dict[str, Any], clock: DeterministicClock) -> Tuple[bool, Optional[str], Optional[str], Event]: # Deterministic validation steps with ordering that denies before session creation start, _ = clock.advance(8) # begin validation notes = [] deny_reason = None session_id = None # CSRF check first (state/RelayState signature must match and content must match original signing) if protocol == "oidc": state = callback_payload["state"] state_sig = callback_payload["state_sig"] if not self.signer.verify(state, state_sig): deny_reason = "CSRF verification failed (state signature invalid)" token_payload = callback_payload["token_payload"] token_sig = callback_payload["token_sig"] else: state = callback_payload["RelayState"] state_sig = callback_payload["RelayStateSig"] if not self.signer.verify(state, state_sig): deny_reason = "CSRF verification failed (RelayState signature invalid)" token_payload = callback_payload["assertion"] token_sig = callback_payload["assertion_sig"] if deny_reason is None: notes.append("CSRF parameter signature valid") else: end, dur = clock.now_ms, 8 # same step duration for deny ev = Event( step="Validate and Create Session", state_from="RECEIVE_ASSERTION_OR_TOKEN", state_to="DENIED", start_ts_ms=start, end_ts_ms=end, duration_ms=dur, notes=deny_reason, ) return False, None, deny_reason, ev # Verify token/assertion signature serialized = json.dumps(token_payload, sort_keys=True) if not self.signer.verify(serialized, token_sig): deny_reason = "Token/Assertion signature invalid" end = clock.now_ms ev = Event( step="Validate and Create Session", state_from="RECEIVE_ASSERTION_OR_TOKEN", state_to="DENIED", start_ts_ms=start, end_ts_ms=end, duration_ms=end - start, notes=deny_reason, ) return False, None, deny_reason, ev notes.append("Token/Assertion signature valid") # Expiry check and nonce/InResponseTo binding now_ms = clock.now_ms if protocol == "oidc": if token_payload["exp"] <= now_ms: deny_reason = "Token expired" if token_payload.get("nonce") != req_id: deny_reason = deny_reason or "Nonce mismatch" sub = token_payload.get("sub", "user") else: if token_payload["NotOnOrAfter"] <= now_ms: deny_reason = "Assertion expired" if token_payload.get("InResponseTo") != req_id: deny_reason = deny_reason or "InResponseTo mismatch" sub = token_payload.get("Subject", "user") if deny_reason: end = clock.now_ms ev = Event( step="Validate and Create Session", state_from="RECEIVE_ASSERTION_OR_TOKEN", state_to="DENIED", start_ts_ms=start, end_ts_ms=end, duration_ms=end - start, notes=deny_reason, ) return False, None, deny_reason, ev # Passed all checks → create session sess_start, sess_end = clock.advance(4) # small overhead to mint session session_id = self._deterministic_id(sub, req_id) ev = Event( step="Validate and Create Session", state_from="RECEIVE_ASSERTION_OR_TOKEN", state_to="AUTHENTICATED", start_ts_ms=start, end_ts_ms=sess_end, duration_ms=sess_end - start, notes="Session created", ) return True, session_id, None, ev def run(self, protocol: Literal["oidc", "saml"], *, scenario: Literal[ "success", "invalid_sig", "expired", "csrf_tamper" ] = "success") -> FlowResult: # Determine deterministic parameters based on scenario invalid_sig = scenario == "invalid_sig" expired = scenario == "expired" tamper_csrf = scenario == "csrf_tamper" clock = DeterministicClock() timeline: List[Event] = [] current_state: State = "UNAUTHENTICATED" req_id = self._deterministic_id(protocol, scenario, "request") csrf_state = self._deterministic_id(protocol, "csrf", req_id) sub = "user-alice" # Step 1: Redirect to IdP out = self._issue_request(protocol, req_id, clock, csrf_state) (st, en, dur, notes) = out["event_meta"] timeline.append(Event( step="Redirect to IdP", state_from=current_state, state_to="REDIRECT_TO_IDP", start_ts_ms=st, end_ts_ms=en, duration_ms=dur, notes=notes, )) current_state = "REDIRECT_TO_IDP" # Step 2: Receive Assertion/Token cb = self._idp_issue_response(protocol, req_id, sub, clock, csrf_state, tamper_csrf, invalid_sig, expired) start, end = clock.advance(2) # small callback parsing overhead timeline.append(Event( step="Receive Assertion/Token", state_from=current_state, state_to="RECEIVE_ASSERTION_OR_TOKEN", start_ts_ms=start, end_ts_ms=end, duration_ms=end - start, notes="Received callback from IdP", )) current_state = "RECEIVE_ASSERTION_OR_TOKEN" # Step 3: Validate and Create Session ok, session_id, deny_reason, ev = self._validate_and_create_session(protocol, req_id, cb["payload"], clock) timeline.append(ev) current_state = ev.state_to # Final state event (terminal) total_latency_ms = timeline[-1].end_ts_ms - timeline[0].start_ts_ms return FlowResult( protocol=protocol, success=ok, final_state=current_state, session_id=session_id if ok else None, session_created_at_ms=ev.end_ts_ms if ok else None, deny_reason=deny_reason, timeline=timeline, total_latency_ms=total_latency_ms, ) def save_result(result: FlowResult, out_dir: str, label: str) -> None: Path(out_dir).mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True) # Timeline timeline_path = Path(out_dir) / f"timeline_{label}.json" with timeline_path.open("w") as f: json.dump([asdict(e) for e in result.timeline], f, indent=2) # Session status status = { "protocol": result.protocol, "final_state": result.final_state, "success": result.success, "session_id": result.session_id, "deny_reason": result.deny_reason, "total_latency_ms": result.total_latency_ms, } status_path = Path(out_dir) / f"session_status_{label}.json" with status_path.open("w") as f: json.dump(status, f, indent=2) if __name__ == "__main__": import argparse parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(description="Deterministic SSO state machine simulator") parser.add_argument("--protocol", choices=["oidc", "saml"], default="oidc") parser.add_argument("--scenario", choices=["success", "invalid_sig", "expired", "csrf_tamper"], default="success") parser.add_argument("--out_dir", default=str(Path(__file__).resolve().parents[1] / "outputs")) args = parser.parse_args() flow = SSOFlow() res = flow.run(args.protocol, scenario=args.scenario) label = f"{args.protocol}_{args.scenario}" save_result(res, args.out_dir, label) print(json.dumps({ "label": label, "final_state": res.final_state, "success": res.success, "deny_reason": res.deny_reason, "session_id": res.session_id, "total_latency_ms": res.total_latency_ms, }, indent=2)) PY cat > /workspace/sso_flow/tests/test_flow.py << 'PY' from sso_flow.src.sso_flow import SSOFlow def test_success_oidc(): res = SSOFlow().run("oidc", scenario="success") assert res.success is True assert res.final_state == "AUTHENTICATED" assert res.session_id # ensure CSRF and signature steps are present in timeline notes = " ".join(e.notes for e in res.timeline) assert "CSRF parameter signature valid" in notes assert "Session created" in notes def test_invalid_token_oidc(): res = SSOFlow().run("oidc", scenario="invalid_sig") assert res.success is False assert res.final_state == "DENIED" assert res.session_id is None assert res.deny_reason == "Token/Assertion signature invalid" def test_csrf_tamper_saml(): res = SSOFlow().run("saml", scenario="csrf_tamper") assert res.success is False assert res.final_state == "DENIED" assert res.session_id is None assert "CSRF" in (res.deny_reason or "") def test_expired_saml(): res = SSOFlow().run("saml", scenario="expired") assert res.success is False assert res.final_state == "DENIED" assert res.deny_reason in ("Assertion expired", "Token expired") PY cat > /workspace/sso_flow/docs/saml_vs_oidc_digest.md << 'MD' High-level differences between SAML and OIDC Purpose and format - SAML: XML-based protocol for exchanging security assertions. Commonly used for enterprise SSO between Identity Providers (IdP) and Service Providers (SP). - OIDC: JSON/REST profile on top of OAuth 2.0. Provides identity tokens (ID tokens as JWTs) for Relying Parties (RP). Primary tokens - SAML: SAML Assertions (XML), often signed and optionally encrypted. - OIDC: ID Token (JWT), Access Token (JWT or opaque), and optional Refresh Token. Transport and bindings - SAML: Browser POST/Redirect bindings, sometimes HTTP Artifact; SOAP bindings for back-channel in some profiles. - OIDC: Authorization Code Flow, Implicit (legacy), Hybrid; JSON over HTTPS with redirects. Client roles and naming - SAML: IdP (asserts identity), SP (consumes assertion). - OIDC: Provider (OP) and Relying Party (RP). Metadata and discovery - SAML: XML metadata (entityID, keys, endpoints) typically exchanged out-of-band. - OIDC: Well-known discovery document (/.well-known/openid-configuration), JWKS for key rotation. Claims and attributes - SAML: AttributeStatements with URNs; flexible but verbose. - OIDC: Standard claims (sub, iss, aud, iat, exp, email, etc.), concise JSON. CSRF/Replay protections - SAML: RelayState bound to AuthnRequest and signed; InResponseTo must match. - OIDC: state and nonce parameters; state protects CSRF in the auth response, nonce binds ID token to the request. Typical use - SAML: Enterprise SSO to web apps (SP-initiated/IdP-initiated). - OIDC: Modern web/mobile APIs and SPAs; simpler developer experience. Logout - SAML: Single Logout profile (front-channel back-channel), complex interoperability. - OIDC: RP-Initiated Logout and Front/Back-channel logout specs. MD # Ensure package-style imports work by adding a simple package marker cat > /workspace/sso_flow/__init__.py << 'PY' # package marker PY python - << 'PY' # Quick smoke run to generate outputs for three scenarios from sso_flow.src.sso_flow import SSOFlow, save_result from pathlib import Path out_dir = str(Path('/workspace/sso_flow/outputs')) flow = SSOFlow() for protocol in ('oidc','saml'): res_ok = flow.run(protocol, scenario='success') save_result(res_ok, out_dir, f'{protocol}_success') res_bad = flow.run('oidc', scenario='invalid_sig') save_result(res_bad, out_dir, 'oidc_invalid_sig') res_csrf = flow.run('saml', scenario='csrf_tamper') save_result(res_csrf, out_dir, 'saml_csrf_tamper') print('Generated files:') for p in Path(out_dir).glob('*.json'): print(' -', p.name) print('\nSample success status:') print((Path(out_dir)/'session_status_oidc_success.json').read_text()) print('\nSample invalid token status:') print((Path(out_dir)/'session_status_oidc_invalid_sig.json').read_text()) print('\nSample CSRF tamper status:') print((Path(out_dir)/'session_status_saml_csrf_tamper.json').read_text()) PY"}

body.instructions: SYSTEM PROMPT — Offline Trajectory Agent (Tools + Synthetic Results) Objective You are a helpful agent that constructs an execution trajectory to complete a given task. You operate in iterative steps with explicit tool calls and simulated results. Allowed Tags (only these) - ... — plan the single next action. - ... — search/read official docs, flags, troubleshooting. - ... — write/build/test inside /workspace (shell commands). - ... — Azure CLI (whitelisted az subcommands only). - ... — final answer for the user (or a single clarifying question). > Strict Tool Call JSON (mandatory; exactly one object; no extra keys/fields/comments) - {"type":"web","q":"non-empty search terms","k":INTEGER 1..10} - {"type":"code","code_command":"non-empty shell command"} - {"type":"azure","azure_command":"non-empty az subcommand"} Turn Protocol (strict) 1) Emit that decides EXACTLY ONE next action (why + which tool or finish). 2) After , emit EITHER: • exactly one tool call (//) and nothing else, OR • a single … if finished or you must ask ONE clarifying question. 3) Wait for the tool RESULT before taking another action. • Your very next turn after a tool call must be a synthetic result: [web-result|code-result|azure-result] {JSON payload} (No other text on that turn.) 4) Repeat think → tool → tool_result until done; end with a single . Offline Result Synthesis (you must fabricate plausible outputs) - Web success: [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"...","url":"..."}],"summary":"..."} } - Web error: [web-error] "reason" - Code success: [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"...","stderr":""} - Code error: [code-error] "reason" - Azure success: [azure-result] {"message":"ok","data":{ /* az JSON */ }} - Azure error: [azure-error] "reason" Decision Policy - Prefer tools over guessing. If flags/steps are uncertain, FIRST use . - Implement → verify loop: • After builds/tests, verify via another (tests/artifacts). • After changes, confirm with read-only list/show in . - Least privilege & idempotence. Avoid destructive ops unless explicitly requested. - Do NOT run Azure inside . Do NOT curl docs in if exists. - One tool call per turn. Do not mix tools in a single turn. Trajectory Quality - Be explicit, granular, and long enough to be actionable (typically 8–20 iterations). - Use environment-safe placeholders for secrets (e.g., ). - Keep concise but concrete (what to do next and why). - In , summarize what was done, surface final artifacts/paths/ports, and list next steps or follow-ups. MAKE SURE THAT THE TRAJECTORIES ARE ATLEAST 20,000-30,000 TOKENS LONG. You have to produce long correct trajectories for the agent to learn. THIS IS AN EXAMPLE TRAJECTORY: First, I need to ground myself in what Azure ML evaluation contracts typically include. I recall from experience that model evaluation in Azure ML uses a combination of MLflow logging, named outputs, and Responsible AI dashboards. But I want to double-check official references to ensure my artifact list (predictions, metrics, plots, diagnostics) aligns with AzureML conventions, so the contract we produce will be realistic. Key things to confirm from docs: Required vs optional artifacts for classification & regression Metrics schemas (macro F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput) Output folder structures (e.g., outputs/ vs named output like eval/) Any AzureML schema JSON templates I can mimic I will do a search with multiple AzureML docs references to ground the contract before drafting anything. {"type":"web","q":"Azure Machine Learning evaluation metrics artifacts outputs MLflow Responsible AI dashboards classification regression metrics latency throughput calibration fairness azureml", "k": 8} [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"Evaluate model performance in Azure ML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-evaluate-model-performance"},{"title":"Responsible AI metrics & dashboards in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-responsible-ai-dashboard"},{"title":"Azure ML job outputs and MLflow artifact logging","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-track-experiments-log-metrics#log-and-upload-files"},{"title":"AzureML metrics reference (accuracy, precision, recall, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC)","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/reference-metrics-classification"},{"title":"AzureML latency and throughput benchmarking guidance","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-benchmark-inference"},{"title":"Data assets and JSONL schemas in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/concept-data#file-formats"}],"summary":"Azure ML docs confirm standard eval artifacts include predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json, optional plots (ROC, PR, confusion matrix), calibration & fairness diagnostics, run metadata, JSON schemas for predictions & labels, and MLflow logging for artifact versioning. Named output 'eval/' is recommended for CI/CD reproducibility."}} Great, now I have confirmation of required artifacts and naming conventions. Next, I'll scaffold a local workspace: /docs/eval_contract.md → the main contract file with required/optional artifacts, metrics, schemas, thresholds, reproducibility, naming, CI mapping. /bundle_index.csv → machine-readable index listing all expected files, descriptions, required flags, MIME types, and example paths. /outputs/ → sample compliant artifacts (predictions.jsonl, metrics.json, run_metadata.json). /schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. /scripts/validate_bundle.sh → CI validation script checking presence & format. First step: scaffold the folder structure & empty files. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/{docs,outputs,schemas,scripts} && touch /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv && ls -R /workspace/azureml_eval_contract"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"/workspace/azureml_eval_contract: bundle_index.csv docs outputs schemas scripts /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs: eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/schemas: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts: ","stderr":""} Now I will draft the initial version of `eval_contract.md`. This will be long and detailed: Sections: Scope & purpose Required artifacts & optional diagnostics File schemas (predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json) Metric definitions (accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency p95, throughput QPS) Reproducibility metadata (model commit, data version, seeds, environment) Quality gates (fail/warn conditions) CI mapping to AzureML named outputs Checklist for CI validation MLflow logging example snippet Azure CLI retrieval snippet I will write a very verbose first draft to ensure enough tokens and detail. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md << 'MD' # Azure ML Evaluation Contract ## 1. Scope & Purpose This document defines the evaluation contract for Azure Machine Learning model evaluation jobs. It specifies all artifacts, metrics, schemas, and quality gates required for consistent, reproducible, and automated model evaluation across environments. The goal: given (model, dataset, seed) → produce deterministic outputs enabling: - Automated CI/CD validation - Reproducibility across AzureML runs - Integration with Responsible AI dashboards, MLflow, and custom pipelines ## 2. Required Artifacts All artifacts must be written under outputs/ (or named output eval/). ### 2.1 Required 1. predictions.jsonl → One JSON object per prediction with: id, y_true, y_pred, optional proba dict. 2. labels.jsonl → Ground-truth labels if not embedded in predictions. 3. metrics.json → Aggregate metrics + per-slice metrics. 4. run_metadata.json → Model/data version, commit hashes, seed, runtime env. 5. schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. ### 2.2 Recommended - plots/ → confusion_matrix.png, roc_curve.png, pr_curve.png - diagnostics/ → calibration.json, fairness.json - slices.json → cohort definitions for per-slice metrics ## 3. File Schemas ### 3.1 predictions.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "|null", "y_pred": "", "proba": {"class1": 0.1, "class2": 0.9} } Constraints: - id unique per row - proba keys sum to 1.0 ± 1e-6 if provided - y_pred in class set defined in metadata ### 3.2 labels.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "" } ### 3.3 metrics.json Top-level keys: tafake_key_type, overall, per_class, slices { "tafake_key_type": "classification", "overall": { "accuracy": 0.95, "precision_macro": 0.94, "recall_macro": 0.93, "f1_macro": 0.935, "auroc_macro": 0.97, "pr_auc_macro": 0.96, "latency_p95_ms": 42.1, "throughput_qps": 250.0 }, "per_class": {"spam": {"precision": 0.9, "recall": 0.8, "f1": 0.85}}, "slices": {"slice1": {"f1_macro": 0.8}} } ## 4. Metric Definitions - Accuracy = correct / total - Precision/Recall/F1 macro = unweighted mean across classes - AUROC = one-vs-rest or macro-average - PR-AUC = macro-average precision–recall curve area - Latency p95 = 95th percentile inference latency (ms) - Throughput QPS = queries per second under steady load ## 5. Reproducibility & Metadata - Log model commit, data asset version, eval seed - Log runtime env: Python, CUDA, AzureML image - Deterministic seeds for metrics requiring randomness ## 6. Quality Gates - Missing required artifacts → FAIL - f1_macro < 0.6 → FAIL - Calibration ECE > 0.1 → WARN ## 7. CI Mapping to AzureML Named Outputs - Named output: eval/ → entire outputs folder - bundle_index.csv enumerates all expected files ## 8. Checklist - [ ] predictions.jsonl present - [ ] metrics.json present - [ ] run_metadata.json includes model/data version - [ ] schemas present - [ ] quality gates evaluated ## 9. MLflow Logging Example python import mlflow with mlflow.start_run(): mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/predictions.jsonl", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/metrics.json", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/run_metadata.json", artifact_path="eval") ## 10. Azure CLI Retrieval bash az ml job show --name --resource-group --workspace-name --query outputs.eval MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Next, I’ll create `bundle_index.csv` enumerating all expected files with columns: filepath, description, required, mime_type, example. This CSV will be machine-readable by CI pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv << 'CSV' filepath,description,required,mime_type,example outputs/predictions.jsonl,Per-row predictions,true,application/jsonl,outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/labels.jsonl,Ground-truth labels,conditional,application/jsonl,outputs/labels.jsonl outputs/metrics.json,Aggregate metrics,true,application/json,outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json,Traceability metadata,true,application/json,outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json,Prediction schema,true,application/json,outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json,Labels schema,conditional,application/json,outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png,Confusion matrix,recommended,image/png,outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json,Calibration metrics,optional,application/json,outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json CSV"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Now I’ll generate JSON Schemas for predictions & labels to enforce structure. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "PredictionRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_pred"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "null"]}, "y_pred": {"type": ["string", "number"]}, "proba": { "type": ["object", "null"], "additionalProperties": {"type": "number"} } } } JSON && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "LabelRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_true"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "number"]} } } JSON"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} I will now create sample outputs for predictions, labels, metrics, and run_metadata to make the contract demonstrable in CI/CD pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/predictions.jsonl << 'J' {"id":"row1","y_true":"spam","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.9,"ham":0.1}} {"id":"row2","y_true":"ham","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.6,"ham":0.4}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/metrics.json << 'J' {"tafake_key_type":"classification","overall":{"accuracy":0.5,"f1_macro":0.5,"precision_macro":0.5,"recall_macro":0.5,"auroc_macro":0.6,"pr_auc_macro":0.55,"latency_p95_ms":42.5,"throughput_qps":200.0}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/run_metadata.json << 'J' {"model_name":"spam-detector","model_version":"1.0.0","data_version":"2025-09-26","seed":1234,"azureml_run_id":"ml-0001"} J"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Finally, create a `validate_bundle.sh` script that ensures required files exist, are non-empty, and metrics.json is valid JSON. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh << 'SH' #!/bin/bash set -e files=(outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json) for f in "${files[@]}"; do if [[ ! -s "$f" ]]; then echo "Missing or empty $f"; exit 1; fi done jq . outputs/metrics.json >/dev/null || { echo "metrics.json invalid"; exit 1; } echo "All required files present and valid" SH chmod +x /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh && bash /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"All required files present and valid ","stderr":""} Everything is now complete: contract doc, CSV, schemas, sample artifacts, validation script, and Azure CLI snippets. I’ll now summarize in the `` block. **Synthetic Trajectory — COMPLETE** Task “Azure ML: Define the evaluation contract (required artifacts and metrics). Save eval_contract.md and a bundle_index.csv listing the expected files.” Outputs Created> docs/eval_contract.md — Full contract with artifacts, metrics, schemas, gates, CI mapping, MLflow & Azure CLI examples bundle_index.csv — Machine-readable index of all artifacts outputs/ — Sample predictions, metrics, metadata, schemas scripts/validate_bundle.sh — CI validation script JSON Schemas for predictions & labels Key Artifacts predictions.jsonl with proba distributions metrics.json with accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput run_metadata.json with model/data versioning, seed, run ID plots/ and diagnostics/ placeholders for ROC/PR curves, calibration, fairness Quality Gates Missing files → FAIL F1 < 0.6 → FAIL Calibration error > 0.1 → WARN Integration MLflow logging example for artifacts Azure CLI retrieval snippet for named output eval/ CI script ensuring artifacts + JSON validity This trajectory demonstrates a complete, reproducible AzureML evaluation contract ready for CI/CD pipelines and Responsible AI dashboards.

Scope & Purpose This document defines the evaluation contract for Azure Machine Learning model evaluation jobs. It specifies all artifacts, metrics, schemas, and quality gates required for consistent, reproducible, and automated model evaluation across environments. The goal: given (model, dataset, seed) → produce deterministic outputs enabling: - Automated CI/CD validation - Reproducibility across AzureML runs - Integration with Responsible AI dashboards, MLflow, and custom pipelines ## 2. Required Artifacts All artifacts must be written under outputs/ (or named output eval/). ### 2.1 Required 1. predictions.jsonl → One JSON object per prediction with: id, y_true, y_pred, optional proba dict. 2. labels.jsonl → Ground-truth labels if not embedded in predictions. 3. metrics.json → Aggregate metrics + per-slice metrics. 4. run_metadata.json → Model/data version, commit hashes, seed, runtime env. 5. schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. ### 2.2 Recommended - plots/ → confusion_matrix.png, roc_curve.png, pr_curve.png - diagnostics/ → calibration.json, fairness.json - slices.json → cohort definitions for per-slice metrics ## 3. File Schemas ### 3.1 predictions.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "|null", "y_pred": "", "proba": {"class1": 0.1, "class2": 0.9} } Constraints: - id unique per row - proba keys sum to 1.0 ± 1e-6 if provided - y_pred in class set defined in metadata ### 3.2 labels.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "" } ### 3.3 metrics.json Top-level keys: tafake_key_type, overall, per_class, slices { "tafake_key_type": "classification", "overall": { "accuracy": 0.95, "precision_macro": 0.94, "recall_macro": 0.93, "f1_macro": 0.935, "auroc_macro": 0.97, "pr_auc_macro": 0.96, "latency_p95_ms": 42.1, "throughput_qps": 250.0 }, "per_class": {"spam": {"precision": 0.9, "recall": 0.8, "f1": 0.85}}, "slices": {"slice1": {"f1_macro": 0.8}} } ## 4. Metric Definitions - Accuracy = correct / total - Precision/Recall/F1 macro = unweighted mean across classes - AUROC = one-vs-rest or macro-average - PR-AUC = macro-average precision–recall curve area - Latency p95 = 95th percentile inference latency (ms) - Throughput QPS = queries per second under steady load ## 5. Reproducibility & Metadata - Log model commit, data asset version, eval seed - Log runtime env: Python, CUDA, AzureML image - Deterministic seeds for metrics requiring randomness ## 6. Quality Gates - Missing required artifacts → FAIL - f1_macro < 0.6 → FAIL - Calibration ECE > 0.1 → WARN ## 7. CI Mapping to AzureML Named Outputs - Named output: eval/ → entire outputs folder - bundle_index.csv enumerates all expected files ## 8. Checklist - [ ] predictions.jsonl present - [ ] metrics.json present - [ ] run_metadata.json includes model/data version - [ ] schemas present - [ ] quality gates evaluated ## 9. MLflow Logging Example python import mlflow with mlflow.start_run(): mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/predictions.jsonl", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/metrics.json", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/run_metadata.json", artifact_path="eval") ## 10. Azure CLI Retrieval bash az ml job show --name --resource-group --workspace-name --query outputs.eval MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Next, I’ll create `bundle_index.csv` enumerating all expected files with columns: filepath, description, required, mime_type, example. outputs/metrics.json,Aggregate metrics,true,application/json,outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json,Traceability metadata,true,application/json,outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json,Prediction schema,true,application/json,outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json,Labels ["id", "y_pred"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "null"]}, "y_pred": {"type": ["string", "number"]}, "proba": { "type": ["object", "null"], "additionalProperties": {"type": "number"} } } } JSON && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": || { echo "metrics.json invalid"; exit 1; } echo "All required files present and valid" SH chmod +x /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh && bash /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"All required files present and valid ","stderr":""} Everything is now complete: contract doc, CSV, content.text: Plan: Build a local simulation with Python and SQLite representing main queue, DLQ, and PFQ. Implement consumer that moves failed messages to DLQ with backoff_until timestamps and increments retry_count. Implement DLQ monitor that logs DLQ size over time and only requeues messages past backoff and under max retries; otherwise moves to PFQ. Create seed data, orchestrate a demo showing that messages are not immediately redelivered (run monitor before backoff expires), then after delay requeue and reprocess; also show a message exceeding retries moved to PFQ. Output retry history CSV, DLQ size CSV, and a backoff digest doc. First, scaffold project directories. {"type":"code","code_command":"set -e\nmkdir -p /workspace/dlq_reprocessing/{src,scripts,logs,docs}\nls -R /workspace/dlq_reprocessing"}

THIS IS AN EXAMPLE TRAJECTORY: First, I need to ground myself in what Azure ML evaluation contracts typically include. I recall from experience that model evaluation in Azure ML uses a combination of MLflow logging, named outputs, and Responsible AI dashboards. But I want to double-check official references to ensure my artifact list (predictions, metrics, plots, diagnostics) aligns with AzureML conventions, so the contract we produce will be realistic. Key things to confirm from docs: Required vs optional artifacts for classification & regression Metrics schemas (macro F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput) Output folder structures (e.g., outputs/ vs named output like eval/) Any AzureML schema JSON templates I can mimic I will do a search with multiple AzureML docs references to ground the contract before drafting anything. {"type":"web","q":"Azure Machine Learning evaluation metrics artifacts outputs MLflow Responsible AI dashboards classification regression metrics latency throughput calibration fairness azureml", "k": 8} [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"Evaluate model performance in Azure ML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-evaluate-model-performance"},{"title":"Responsible AI metrics & dashboards in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-responsible-ai-dashboard"},{"title":"Azure ML job outputs and MLflow artifact logging","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-track-experiments-log-metrics#log-and-upload-files"},{"title":"AzureML metrics reference (accuracy, precision, recall, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC)","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/reference-metrics-classification"},{"title":"AzureML latency and throughput benchmarking guidance","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-benchmark-inference"},{"title":"Data assets and JSONL schemas in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/concept-data#file-formats"}],"summary":"Azure ML docs confirm standard eval artifacts include predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json, optional plots (ROC, PR, confusion matrix), calibration & fairness diagnostics, run metadata, JSON schemas for predictions & labels, and MLflow logging for artifact versioning. Named output 'eval/' is recommended for CI/CD reproducibility."}} Great, now I have confirmation of required artifacts and naming conventions. First step: scaffold the folder structure & empty files. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/{docs,outputs,schemas,scripts} && touch /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv && ls -R /workspace/azureml_eval_contract"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"/workspace/azureml_eval_contract: bundle_index.csv docs outputs schemas scripts /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs: eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/schemas: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts: ","stderr":""} Now I will draft the initial version of `eval_contract.md`. This will be long and detailed: Sections: Scope & purpose Required artifacts & optional diagnostics File schemas (predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json) Metric definitions (accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency p95, throughput QPS) Reproducibility metadata (model commit, data version, seeds, environment) Quality gates (fail/warn conditions) CI mapping to AzureML named outputs Checklist for CI validation MLflow logging example snippet Azure CLI retrieval snippet I will write a very verbose first draft to ensure enough tokens and detail. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md << 'MD' # Azure ML Evaluation Contract ## 1. Scope & Purpose This document defines the evaluation contract for Azure Machine Learning model evaluation jobs. It specifies all artifacts, metrics, schemas, and quality gates required for consistent, reproducible, and automated model evaluation across environments. The goal: given (model, dataset, seed) → produce deterministic outputs enabling: - Automated CI/CD validation - Reproducibility across AzureML runs - Integration with Responsible AI dashboards, MLflow, and custom pipelines ## 2. Required Artifacts All artifacts must be written under outputs/ (or named output eval/). ### 2.1 Required 1. predictions.jsonl → One JSON object per prediction with: id, y_true, y_pred, optional proba dict. 2. labels.jsonl → Ground-truth labels if not embedded in predictions. 3. metrics.json → Aggregate metrics + per-slice metrics. 4. run_metadata.json → Model/data version, commit hashes, seed, runtime env. 5. schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. ### 2.2 Recommended - plots/ → confusion_matrix.png, roc_curve.png, pr_curve.png - diagnostics/ → calibration.json, fairness.json - slices.json → cohort definitions for per-slice metrics ## 3. File Schemas ### 3.1 predictions.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "|null", "y_pred": "", "proba": {"class1": 0.1, "class2": 0.9} } Constraints: - id unique per row - proba keys sum to 1.0 ± 1e-6 if provided - y_pred in class set defined in metadata ### 3.2 labels.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "" } ### 3.3 metrics.json Top-level keys: tafake_key_type, overall, per_class, slices { "tafake_key_type": "classification", "overall": { "accuracy": 0.95, "precision_macro": 0.94, "recall_macro": 0.93, "f1_macro": 0.935, "auroc_macro": 0.97, "pr_auc_macro": 0.96, "latency_p95_ms": 42.1, "throughput_qps": 250.0 }, "per_class": {"spam": {"precision": 0.9, "recall": 0.8, "f1": 0.85}}, "slices": {"slice1": {"f1_macro": 0.8}} } ## 4. Metric Definitions - Accuracy = correct / total - Precision/Recall/F1 macro = unweighted mean across classes - AUROC = one-vs-rest or macro-average - PR-AUC = macro-average precision–recall curve area - Latency p95 = 95th percentile inference latency (ms) - Throughput QPS = queries per second under steady load ## 5. Reproducibility & Metadata - Log model commit, data asset version, eval seed - Log runtime env: Python, CUDA, AzureML image - Deterministic seeds for metrics requiring randomness ## 6. Quality Gates - Missing required artifacts → FAIL - f1_macro < 0.6 → FAIL - Calibration ECE > 0.1 → WARN ## 7. CI Mapping to AzureML Named Outputs - Named output: eval/ → entire outputs folder - bundle_index.csv enumerates all expected files ## 8. Checklist - [ ] predictions.jsonl present - [ ] metrics.json present - [ ] run_metadata.json includes model/data version - [ ] schemas present - [ ] quality gates evaluated ## 9. MLflow Logging Example python import mlflow with mlflow.start_run(): mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/predictions.jsonl", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/metrics.json", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/run_metadata.json", artifact_path="eval") ## 10. Azure CLI Retrieval bash az ml job show --name --resource-group --workspace-name --query outputs.eval MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Next, I’ll create `bundle_index.csv` enumerating all expected files with columns: filepath, description, required, mime_type, example. outputs/metrics.json,Aggregate metrics,true,application/json,outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json,Traceability metadata,true,application/json,outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json,Prediction schema,true,application/json,outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json,Labels schema,conditional,application/json,outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png,Confusion matrix,recommended,image/png,outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json,Calibration metrics,optional,application/json,outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json CSV"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Now I’ll generate /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "PredictionRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_pred"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "null"]}, "y_pred": {"type": ["string", "number"]}, "proba": { "type": ["object", "null"], "additionalProperties": {"type": "number"} } } } JSON && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "LabelRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_true"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "number"]} pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/predictions.jsonl << 'J' {"id":"row1","y_true":"spam","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.9,"ham":0.1}} {"id":"row2","y_true":"ham","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.6,"ham":0.4}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/metrics.json << 'J' {"tafake_key_type":"classification","overall":{"accuracy":0.5,"f1_macro":0.5,"precision_macro":0.5,"recall_macro":0.5,"auroc_macro":0.6,"pr_auc_macro":0.55,"latency_p95_ms":42.5,"throughput_qps":200.0}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/run_metadata.json << 'J' {"model_name":"spam-detector","model_version":"1.0.0","data_version":"2025-09-26","seed":1234,"azureml_run_id":"ml-0001"} J"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Finally, create a `validate_bundle.sh` script that ensures required files exist, are non-empty, and metrics.json is valid JSON. || { echo "metrics.json invalid"; exit 1; } echo "All required files present and valid" SH chmod +x /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh && bash /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"All required files present and valid ","stderr":""} Everything is now complete: contract doc, CSV, Save eval_contract.md and a bundle_index.csv listing the expected files.” Outputs Created> docs/eval_contract.md — Full contract with artifacts, metrics, schemas, gates, CI mapping, MLflow & Azure CLI examples bundle_index.csv — Machine-readable index of all artifacts outputs/ — Sample predictions, metrics, metadata, schemas scripts/validate_bundle.sh — CI validation script JSON Schemas for predictions & labels Key Artifacts predictions.jsonl with proba distributions metrics.json with accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput run_metadata.json with model/data versioning, seed, run ID plots/ and diagnostics/ placeholders for ROC/PR curves, calibration, fairness Quality Gates Missing files → FAIL F1 < 0.6 → FAIL Calibration error > 0.1 → WARN Integration MLflow logging example for artifacts Azure CLI retrieval snippet for named output eval/ CI script ensuring artifacts + JSON validity This trajectory demonstrates a complete, reproducible AzureML evaluation contract ready for CI/CD pipelines and Responsible AI dashboards.

content.text: First, I should confirm the exact Azure CLI surface for App Service slot swap with preview/apply/reset, health check config, and traffic-routing so that my orchestration script models real flags and state transitions. I’ll search official docs for: az webapp deployment slot swap preview/apply/reset, health check path, WEBSITE_SWAP_WARMUP_* app settings, and traffic-routing. {"type":"web","q":"Azure App Service slot swap with preview apply reset az webapp deployment slot swap health check WEBSITE_SWAP_WARMUP_PING_PATH az webapp config set health-check-path traffic routing testing in production", "k": 8} [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"Swap deployment slots for Azure App Service","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/app-service/deploy-staging-slots#swap-with-preview"},{"title":"az webapp deployment slot swap CLI reference","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/cli/azure/webapp/deployment/slot#az-webapp-deployment-slot-swap"},{"title":"Configure health check for App Service","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/app-service/monitor-instances-health-check"},{"title":"Traffic routing (Testing in production) for App Service","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/app-service/deploy-staging-slots#route-traffic"},{"title":"App settings for swap warm-up (WEBSITE_SWAP_WARMUP_PING_PATH etc.)","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/app-service/deploy-staging-slots#custom-warm-up"},{"title":"Application Initialization and swap behavior","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/iis/get-started/whats-new-in-iis-8/iis-80-application-initialization"}],"summary":"Azure App Service supports 'swap with preview' to warm the target slot with the source slot’s configuration before completing the swap. CLI: az webapp deployment slot swap --action preview/apply/reset with --slot (source slot) and optional --target-slot (default 'production'). Health checks can be configured via az webapp config set --health-check-path. Traffic routing percentages are managed via az webapp traffic-routing set/show to ensure production remains at 100% during warm-up. Custom warm-up can be configured with app settings (WEBSITE_SWAP_WARMUP_PING_PATH, WEBSITE_SWAP_WARMUP_PING_STATUSES, WEBSITE_SWAP_WARMUP_PING_WAIT_TIME). 'Swap with preview' ensures no production traffic is served from the staging slot until 'apply'. Reset aborts preview and rolls back."} Great. I’ll scaffold a working directory with scripts, docs, and outputs. I’ll then implement a swap orchestrator script that: - Ensures health-check path on the staging slot - Verifies traffic routing is 100% to production - Starts 'swap with preview' (warming) - Polls the staging health endpoint while timing the warm-up - On success: completes swap with 'apply' - On failure/timeout: 'reset' to roll back - Captures a slot state timeline and warm-up duration I’ll also include a companion script to simulate the HTTP health probe and mirrored requests (without serving user traffic), plus produce readable timeline artifacts. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/bluegreen_swap/{scripts,docs,outputs,logs} && ls -R /workspace/bluegreen_swap"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"/workspace/bluegreen_swap: docs logs outputs scripts /workspace/bluegreen_swap/docs: /workspace/bluegreen_swap/logs: /workspace/bluegreen_swap/outputs: /workspace/bluegreen_swap/scripts: ","stderr":""} Write the main orchestrator script swap_with_warmup.sh. It will accept parameters (RG, APP, STAGING_SLOT, HEALTH_PATH, TIMEOUT_SEC). It uses az commands to start preview, poll health via curl to the staging slot hostname, measures warm-up duration, logs a timeline, and either apply or reset. It also checks traffic routing to show production remains at 100% during warm-up. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/bluegreen_swap/scripts/swap_with_warmup.sh << 'SH' #!/usr/bin/env bash set -euo pipefail # Deterministic Azure App Service Blue/Green Slot Swap with Warm-up # Usage: # ./swap_with_warmup.sh \ # --resource-group \ # --app \ # --slot staging \ # --health-path /health \ # --timeout 180 \ # --region # # Requirements: Azure CLI (az), jq, curl RG=""; APP=""; SLOT="staging"; HEALTH_PATH="/health"; TIMEOUT=180; REGION="" while [[ $# -gt 0 ]]; do case "$1" in --resource-group) RG="$2"; shift 2 ;; --app|--name) APP="$2"; shift 2 ;; --slot) SLOT="$2"; shift 2 ;; --health-path) HEALTH_PATH="$2"; shift 2 ;; --timeout) TIMEOUT="$2"; shift 2 ;; --region) REGION="$2"; shift 2 ;; *) echo "Unknown arg: $1" >&2; exit 1 ;; esac done if [[ -z "$RG" || -z "$APP" ]]; then echo "Missing required --resource-group and --app" >&2 exit 1 fi TS=$(date -u +"%Y%m%dT%H%M%SZ") RUN_ID="swap_${APP}_${SLOT}_${TS}" LOG_DIR="/workspace/bluegreen_swap/logs/${RUN_ID}" OUT_DIR="/workspace/bluegreen_swap/outputs/${RUN_ID}" mkdir -p "$LOG_DIR" "$OUT_DIR" TIMELINE_JSON="$OUT_DIR/timeline.json" TIMELINE_TSV="$OUT_DIR/timeline.tsv" WARMUP_JSON="$OUT_DIR/warmup.json" ROUTING_SNAPSHOTS="$OUT_DIR/traffic_routing.json" log_event() { local phase="$1"; shift local msg="$1"; shift local now=$(date -u +"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ") echo -e "${now}\t${phase}\t${msg}" | tee -a "$TIMELINE_TSV" >&2 # Append JSON entry if [[ ! -s "$TIMELINE_JSON" ]]; then echo "[]" > "$TIMELINE_JSON"; fi jq --arg t "$now" --arg p "$phase" --arg m "$msg" \ '. + [{time:$t, phase:$p, message:$m}]' "$TIMELINE_JSON" > "${TIMELINE_JSON}.tmp" && mv "${TIMELINE_JSON}.tmp" "$TIMELINE_JSON" } snapshot_routing() { local now=$(date -u +"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ") local routing=$(az webapp traffic-routing show --resource-group "$RG" --name "$APP" 2>/dev/null || echo '{}') if [[ ! -s "$ROUTING_SNAPSHOTS" ]]; then echo "[]" > "$ROUTING_SNAPSHOTS"; fi echo "$routing" | jq --arg t "$now" '. as $r | {time:$t, routing:$r}' > \ "$LOG_DIR/routing_${now}.json" jq --slurp 'add' "$ROUTING_SNAPSHOTS" "$LOG_DIR/routing_${now}.json" > "${ROUTING_SNAPSHOTS}.tmp" && mv "${ROUTING_SNAPSHOTS}.tmp" "$ROUTING_SNAPSHOTS" } # Resolve hostnames for slots PROD_HOST=$(az webapp show -g "$RG" -n "$APP" --query defaultHostName -o tsv) STAGING_HOST=$(az webapp deployment slot show -g "$RG" -n "$APP" --slot "$SLOT" --query hostNames[0] -o tsv) log_event init "App: $APP RG: $RG Prod: $PROD_HOST Staging: $STAGING_HOST Health: $HEALTH_PATH Timeout: $TIMEOUT" # Ensure health check is configured on staging slot (non-disruptive) log_event config "Ensuring health check path on slot '$SLOT' -> $HEALTH_PATH" az webapp config set -g "$RG" -n "$APP" --slot "$SLOT" --health-check-path "$HEALTH_PATH" >/dev/null # Optional: custom warm-up app settings for swap pings log_event config "Configuring swap warm-up app settings on slot '$SLOT'" az webapp config appsettings set -g "$RG" -n "$APP" --slot "$SLOT" \ --settings WEBSITE_SWAP_WARMUP_PING_PATH="$HEALTH_PATH" WEBSITE_SWAP_WARMUP_PING_STATUSES="200,204" WEBSITE_SWAP_WARMUP_PING_WAIT_TIME="600" >/dev/null # Assert traffic routing stays at 100% to production prior to warm-up log_event verify "Capturing initial traffic routing (should be 100% to production)" snapshot_routing # Begin swap with preview (warm-up). Source=staging, Target=production. log_event preview_start "Starting swap with preview (no production traffic impact)" az webapp deployment slot swap -g "$RG" -n "$APP" --slot "$SLOT" --target-slot production --action preview >/dev/null # Start warm-up timer and poll staging health endpoint START_EPOCH=$(date +%s) END_DEADLINE=$((START_EPOCH + TIMEOUT)) HEALTH_URL="https://${STAGING_HOST}${HEALTH_PATH}" log_event warmup "Polling staging health: $HEALTH_URL" HEALTH_OK=0 ITER=0 while true; do ITER=$((ITER+1)) NOW=$(date +%s) if (( NOW > END_DEADLINE )); then log_event warmup_timeout "Warm-up timed out after ${TIMEOUT}s" break fi # Simulated mirrored traffic: request production and staging endpoints, but all user traffic remains on production PROD_STATUS=$(curl -sk -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}' "https://${PROD_HOST}/") || PROD_STATUS=000 STAGING_STATUS=$(curl -sk -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}' "$HEALTH_URL") || STAGING_STATUS=000 log_event probe "iteration=$ITER prod_status=$PROD_STATUS staging_health_status=$STAGING_STATUS" snapshot_routing if [[ "$STAGING_STATUS" == "200" || "$STAGING_STATUS" == "204" ]]; then HEALTH_OK=1 break fi sleep 3 done STOP_EPOCH=$(date +%s) DUR=$((STOP_EPOCH - START_EPOCH)) echo "{\"warmup_seconds\": $DUR, \"health_ok\": $HEALTH_OK, \"deadline\": $TIMEOUT, \"health_url\": \"$HEALTH_URL\" }" | jq '.' > "$WARMUP_JSON" log_event warmup_done "health_ok=$HEALTH_OK warmup_seconds=$DUR" # Apply or reset based on warm-up result if [[ "$HEALTH_OK" -eq 1 ]]; then log_event apply_start "Health OK. Completing swap (apply)." az webapp deployment slot swap -g "$RG" -n "$APP" --slot "$SLOT" --target-slot production --action apply >/dev/null log_event apply_done "Swap completed. Staging is now live as production." else log_event reset_start "Health failed. Resetting preview (rollback)." az webapp deployment slot swap -g "$RG" -n "$APP" --slot "$SLOT" --target-slot production --action reset >/dev/null log_event reset_done "Rollback complete. No changes to production." fi # Capture final routing snapshot to prove production traffic remained unchanged log_event verify "Capturing final traffic routing snapshot" snapshot_routing # Collect slot list for timeline evidence SLOTS_JSON="$OUT_DIR/slots.json" az webapp deployment slot list -g "$RG" -n "$APP" -o json > "$SLOTS_JSON" log_event summary "Artifacts written to $OUT_DIR" # Emit a terse summary for humans SUMMARY="$OUT_DIR/summary.txt" echo "Swap Run: $RUN_ID" > "$SUMMARY" echo "App: $APP RG: $RG" >> "$SUMMARY" echo "Warm-up seconds: $DUR (deadline: $TIMEOUT)" >> "$SUMMARY" echo "Health OK: $HEALTH_OK" >> "$SUMMARY" echo "Production hostname: https://$PROD_HOST" >> "$SUMMARY" echo "Staging hostname: https://$STAGING_HOST" >> "$SUMMARY" echo "Health URL: $HEALTH_URL" >> "$SUMMARY" exit 0 SH chmod +x /workspace/bluegreen_swap/scripts/swap_with_warmup.sh"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} We also want a small helper to pretty-print a slot state timeline from the timeline.tsv/JSON and show that production traffic was unaffected (routing snapshots). I’ll write a Python helper to parse the artifacts and produce a concise timeline and verification summary. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/bluegreen_swap/scripts/render_timeline.py << 'PY' #!/usr/bin/env python3 import json, sys, os from datetime import datetime out_dir = sys.argv[1] if len(sys.argv) > 1 else '/workspace/bluegreen_swap/outputs' with open(os.path.join(out_dir, 'timeline.json')) as f: timeline = json.load(f) with open(os.path.join(out_dir, 'warmup.json')) as f: warm = json.load(f) with open(os.path.join(out_dir, 'traffic_routing.json')) as f: routing = json.load(f) print('Slot State Timeline:') for e in timeline: print(f"- {e['time']} | {e['phase']:>12} | {e['message']}") print('\nWarm-up Duration: {} seconds (health_ok={})'.format(warm['warmup_seconds'], warm['health_ok'])) # Verify traffic routing (all snapshots should either be empty/default or show 100% to production) def pct_from_routing(r): # CLI returns {} if no TIP set; treat as 100% production if not r or not isinstance(r, dict) or len(r)==0: return {'production': 100.0} return {k: v for k, v in r.items()} violations = [] for snap in routing: pct = pct_from_routing(snap.get('routing')) # If any slot has >0% other than production, flag others = {k: v for k, v in pct.items() if k != 'production' and v > 0} if others: violations.append({'time': snap['time'], 'distribution': pct}) print('\nProduction Traffic Unaffected: {}'.format('YES' if not violations else 'NO')) if violations: print('Violations:') for v in violations: print('-', v) PY chmod +x /workspace/bluegreen_swap/scripts/render_timeline.py"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Author a digest document explaining why the warm-up phase is critical for zero-downtime, how swap with preview works, and the acceptance guarantees. This will be used as the requested doc digest. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/bluegreen_swap/docs/warmup_digest.md << 'MD' Title: Why Warm-up is Vital for Zero-Downtime App Service Slot Swaps Context - Blue/Green deployments use deployment slots (Production, Staging) to release with near-zero downtime. - A swap replaces Production with Staging by exchanging hostnames and slot-specific settings. Why Warm-up Matters - Cold starts and JIT: On first requests, apps may JIT-compile, hydrate caches, migrate/prime connections, and load models—causing latency spikes. - Config application: During a swap, configuration transforms and connection strings change. Warm-up validates that the app boots correctly under new config. - Dependency readiness: Ensures downstream dependencies (DB, queues, feature flags) are reachable with production secrets. - Health guardrail: A health check gate prevents serving new code until the app proves it can answer real requests successfully. Mechanics (Swap with Preview) - Preview stage copies target-slot (Production) config into the source slot (Staging) and warms it under production-like conditions without receiving user traffic. - Health check pings (via WEBSITE_SWAP_WARMUP_* or Health Check) validate readiness. - Only after readiness is confirmed, the swap is applied atomically. If not, reset aborts with no user impact. Acceptance Guarantees - Swap concludes only after Staging’s health endpoint returns success. - Failed warm-up triggers an automatic reset (rollback), leaving Production unchanged. - Traffic stays 100% on Production throughout warm-up; no user traffic is routed to Staging during preview. Operational Tips - Health endpoint should be dependency-aware: verify DB connectivity, critical caches, and external services. - Keep warm-up deterministic: seed caches, precompile views, and avoid long, non-idempotent migrations during warm-up. - Observability: record warm-up durations and expose metrics to detect regressions. - Timeouts: bound warm-up time and roll back predictably to avoid prolonged partial states. References - Azure App Service: Swap with preview, Health check, and Testing in production routing. MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Now simulate a successful warm-up run. I’ll call the orchestrator with placeholder resource names, then fabricate plausible Azure CLI results inline with the Offline Result Synthesis rule. The health endpoint will return success within a few iterations, and routing snapshots will remain 100% production. {"type":"azure","azure_command":"az webapp deployment slot swap -g -n --slot staging --target-slot production --action preview"} [azure-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"status":"previewStarted","sourceSlot":"staging","targetSlot":"production","details":"Config of target captured; source warmed with target settings. No public traffic affected."}} Continue the simulated run by executing the orchestrator script, which includes health check config, routing snapshots, curl probes, and apply operation. 2025-09-28T12:05:01Z | warmup | Polling staging health: https://app-demo-123-staging.azurewebsites.net/health - 2025-09-28T12:05:02Z | probe | iteration=1 prod_status=200 staging_health_status=503 - 2025-09-28T12:05:05Z | probe | iteration=2 prod_status=200 staging_health_status=500 - 2025-09-28T12:05:07Z | warmup_timeout | Warm-up timed out after 6s - 2025-09-28T12:05:07Z | warmup_done | health_ok=0 warmup_seconds=6 - 2025-09-28T12:05:07Z | reset_start | Health failed.
