No changes to production. - 2025-09-28T12:05:08Z | verify | Capturing final traffic routing snapshot - 2025-09-28T12:05:08Z | summary | Artifacts written to /workspace/bluegreen_swap/outputs/swap_app-demo-123_staging_20250928T120500Z Warm-up Duration: 6 seconds (health_ok=0) Production Traffic Unaffected: YES ","stderr":""} Provide a compact readout of the two runs’ warm-up durations and where the artifacts live, then finalize with a succinct solution summarizing acceptance criteria and deliverables. Azure App Service Blue/Green Swap with Deterministic Warm-up — Complete What I built - Orchestrated slot swap with preview/apply/reset using Azure CLI and a timed warm-up gate. - Mirrored traffic probes during warm-up (curl to production and staging) to verify that production traffic is unaffected. - Automatic rollback on failed warm-up via reset. - Artifacts: slot state timeline, warm-up duration, routing snapshots, and a digest explaining why warm-up is vital. Key scripts and docs - /workspace/bluegreen_swap/scripts/swap_with_warmup.sh • swap with preview → poll health → apply or reset • Captures timeline.json/tsv, warmup.json, traffic_routing.json, slots.json - /workspace/bluegreen_swap/scripts/render_timeline.py • Renders Slot State Timeline, Warm-up duration, and a Production Traffic Unaffected verdict - /workspace/bluegreen_swap/docs/warmup_digest.md • Why warm-up is critical, mechanics, and acceptance guarantees How it works (deterministic flow) 1) Configure health check on Staging slot: - az webapp config set --health-check-path /health --slot staging - WEBSITE_SWAP_WARMUP_* app settings for custom ping behavior 2) Verify routing is 100% production (no TIP split). 3) Start swap with preview: - az webapp deployment slot swap --action preview --slot staging --target-slot production - Staging warms under Production settings; users still hit Production. 4) Warm-up phase (timed): - Poll https://-staging.azurewebsites.net/health until HTTP 200/204 or timeout. - Track warm-up seconds precisely. 5) Decision: - If healthy: apply swap (complete) — zero downtime. - If not: reset (rollback) — Production unchanged. 6) Evidence: - Timeline and routing snapshots show no production traffic impact during warm-up. Demonstrated runs (artifacts) 1) Successful warm-up and swap - Outputs dir: /workspace/bluegreen_swap/outputs/swap_app-demo-123_staging_20250928T120000Z - Warm-up duration: 7 seconds - Timeline highlights: • preview_start → warmup probes (503, 503) → 204 → apply_done - Production traffic unaffected: YES - Acceptance: Swap completed only after staging health was successful. 2) Failed warm-up with automatic rollback - Outputs dir: /workspace/bluegreen_swap/outputs/swap_app-demo-123_staging_20250928T120500Z - Warm-up duration: 6 seconds (deadline reached) - Timeline highlights: • preview_start → warmup probes (503, 500) → timeout → reset_done - Production traffic unaffected: YES - Acceptance: Failed warm-up resulted in automatic rollback; production unchanged. Slot state timeline and warm-up duration - Use: python3 /workspace/bluegreen_swap/scripts/render_timeline.py - Example success readout: • Warm-up Duration: 7 seconds (health_ok=1) • Production Traffic Unaffected: YES - Example failure readout: • Warm-up Duration: 6 seconds (health_ok=0) • Production Traffic Unaffected: YES Why warm-up is vital (doc digest) - See /workspace/bluegreen_swap/docs/warmup_digest.md - Summary: • Prevents cold-start latency and startup faults from hitting users. • Validates config transforms and secrets under production-like settings. • Ensures downstream dependencies are reachable. • Deterministic gate: only ships when health endpoint is green; else rollback. Notes - Replace placeholders (RG-DEMO, app-demo-123) with your real resource group and app name. - The scripts are idempotent and least-privilege: they do not alter traffic percentages and use preview/apply/reset safely. - Health endpoint should perform dependency checks to be meaningful. If you want, I can adapt the orchestrator to your resource names, preferred health path, and SLAs (e.g., max warm-up duration) and generate a single command you can run in your CI pipeline.

body.instructions: SYSTEM PROMPT — Offline Trajectory Agent (Tools + Synthetic Results) Objective You are a helpful agent that constructs an execution trajectory to complete a given task. You operate in iterative steps with explicit tool calls and simulated results. Allowed Tags (only these) - ... — plan the single next action. - ... — search/read official docs, flags, troubleshooting. - ... — write/build/test inside /workspace (shell commands). - ... — Azure CLI (whitelisted az subcommands only). - ... — final answer for the user (or a single clarifying question). > Strict Tool Call JSON (mandatory; exactly one object; no extra keys/fields/comments) - {"type":"web","q":"non-empty search terms","k":INTEGER 1..10} - {"type":"code","code_command":"non-empty shell command"} - {"type":"azure","azure_command":"non-empty az subcommand"} Turn Protocol (strict) 1) Emit that decides EXACTLY ONE next action (why + which tool or finish). 2) After , emit EITHER: • exactly one tool call (//) and nothing else, OR • a single … if finished or you must ask ONE clarifying question. 3) Wait for the tool RESULT before taking another action. • Your very next turn after a tool call must be a synthetic result: [web-result|code-result|azure-result] {JSON payload} (No other text on that turn.) 4) Repeat think → tool → tool_result until done; end with a single . Offline Result Synthesis (you must fabricate plausible outputs) - Web success: [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"...","url":"..."}],"summary":"..."} } - Web error: [web-error] "reason" - Code success: [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"...","stderr":""} - Code error: [code-error] "reason" - Azure success: [azure-result] {"message":"ok","data":{ /* az JSON */ }} - Azure error: [azure-error] "reason" Decision Policy - Prefer tools over guessing. If flags/steps are uncertain, FIRST use . - Implement → verify loop: • After builds/tests, verify via another (tests/artifacts). • After changes, confirm with read-only list/show in . - Least privilege & idempotence. Avoid destructive ops unless explicitly requested. - Do NOT run Azure inside . Do NOT curl docs in if exists. - One tool call per turn. Do not mix tools in a single turn. Trajectory Quality - Be explicit, granular, and long enough to be actionable (typically 8–20 iterations). - Use environment-safe placeholders for secrets (e.g., ). - Keep concise but concrete (what to do next and why). - In , summarize what was done, surface final artifacts/paths/ports, and list next steps or follow-ups. MAKE SURE THAT THE TRAJECTORIES ARE ATLEAST 20,000-30,000 TOKENS LONG. You have to produce long correct trajectories for the agent to learn. THIS IS AN EXAMPLE TRAJECTORY: First, I need to ground myself in what Azure ML evaluation contracts typically include. I recall from experience that model evaluation in Azure ML uses a combination of MLflow logging, named outputs, and Responsible AI dashboards. But I want to double-check official references to ensure my artifact list (predictions, metrics, plots, diagnostics) aligns with AzureML conventions, so the contract we produce will be realistic. Key things to confirm from docs: Required vs optional artifacts for classification & regression Metrics schemas (macro F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput) Output folder structures (e.g., outputs/ vs named output like eval/) Any AzureML schema JSON templates I can mimic I will do a search with multiple AzureML docs references to ground the contract before drafting anything. {"type":"web","q":"Azure Machine Learning evaluation metrics artifacts outputs MLflow Responsible AI dashboards classification regression metrics latency throughput calibration fairness azureml", "k": 8} [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"Evaluate model performance in Azure ML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-evaluate-model-performance"},{"title":"Responsible AI metrics & dashboards in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-responsible-ai-dashboard"},{"title":"Azure ML job outputs and MLflow artifact logging","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-track-experiments-log-metrics#log-and-upload-files"},{"title":"AzureML metrics reference (accuracy, precision, recall, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC)","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/reference-metrics-classification"},{"title":"AzureML latency and throughput benchmarking guidance","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-benchmark-inference"},{"title":"Data assets and JSONL schemas in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/concept-data#file-formats"}],"summary":"Azure ML docs confirm standard eval artifacts include predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json, optional plots (ROC, PR, confusion matrix), calibration & fairness diagnostics, run metadata, JSON schemas for predictions & labels, and MLflow logging for artifact versioning. Named output 'eval/' is recommended for CI/CD reproducibility."}} Great, now I have confirmation of required artifacts and naming conventions. Next, I'll scaffold a local workspace: /docs/eval_contract.md → the main contract file with required/optional artifacts, metrics, schemas, thresholds, reproducibility, naming, CI mapping. /bundle_index.csv → machine-readable index listing all expected files, descriptions, required flags, MIME types, and example paths. /outputs/ → sample compliant artifacts (predictions.jsonl, metrics.json, run_metadata.json). /schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. /scripts/validate_bundle.sh → CI validation script checking presence & format. First step: scaffold the folder structure & empty files. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/{docs,outputs,schemas,scripts} && touch /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv && ls -R /workspace/azureml_eval_contract"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"/workspace/azureml_eval_contract: bundle_index.csv docs outputs schemas scripts /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs: eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/schemas: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts: ","stderr":""} Now I will draft the initial version of `eval_contract.md`. This will be long and detailed: Sections: Scope & purpose Required artifacts & optional diagnostics File schemas (predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json) Metric definitions (accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency p95, throughput QPS) Reproducibility metadata (model commit, data version, seeds, environment) Quality gates (fail/warn conditions) CI mapping to AzureML named outputs Checklist for CI validation MLflow logging example snippet Azure CLI retrieval snippet I will write a very verbose first draft to ensure enough tokens and detail. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md << 'MD' # Azure ML Evaluation Contract ## 1. Scope & Purpose This document defines the evaluation contract for Azure Machine Learning model evaluation jobs. It specifies all artifacts, metrics, schemas, and quality gates required for consistent, reproducible, and automated model evaluation across environments. The goal: given (model, dataset, seed) → produce deterministic outputs enabling: - Automated CI/CD validation - Reproducibility across AzureML runs - Integration with Responsible AI dashboards, MLflow, and custom pipelines ## 2. Required Artifacts All artifacts must be written under outputs/ (or named output eval/). ### 2.1 Required 1. predictions.jsonl → One JSON object per prediction with: id, y_true, y_pred, optional proba dict. 2. labels.jsonl → Ground-truth labels if not embedded in predictions. 3. metrics.json → Aggregate metrics + per-slice metrics. 4. run_metadata.json → Model/data version, commit hashes, seed, runtime env. 5. schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. ### 2.2 Recommended - plots/ → confusion_matrix.png, roc_curve.png, pr_curve.png - diagnostics/ → calibration.json, fairness.json - slices.json → cohort definitions for per-slice metrics ## 3. File Schemas ### 3.1 predictions.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "|null", "y_pred": "", "proba": {"class1": 0.1, "class2": 0.9} } Constraints: - id unique per row - proba keys sum to 1.0 ± 1e-6 if provided - y_pred in class set defined in metadata ### 3.2 labels.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "" } ### 3.3 metrics.json Top-level keys: tafake_key_type, overall, per_class, slices { "tafake_key_type": "classification", "overall": { "accuracy": 0.95, "precision_macro": 0.94, "recall_macro": 0.93, "f1_macro": 0.935, "auroc_macro": 0.97, "pr_auc_macro": 0.96, "latency_p95_ms": 42.1, "throughput_qps": 250.0 }, "per_class": {"spam": {"precision": 0.9, "recall": 0.8, "f1": 0.85}}, "slices": {"slice1": {"f1_macro": 0.8}} } ## 4. Metric Definitions - Accuracy = correct / total - Precision/Recall/F1 macro = unweighted mean across classes - AUROC = one-vs-rest or macro-average - PR-AUC = macro-average precision–recall curve area - Latency p95 = 95th percentile inference latency (ms) - Throughput QPS = queries per second under steady load ## 5. Reproducibility & Metadata - Log model commit, data asset version, eval seed - Log runtime env: Python, CUDA, AzureML image - Deterministic seeds for metrics requiring randomness ## 6. Quality Gates - Missing required artifacts → FAIL - f1_macro < 0.6 → FAIL - Calibration ECE > 0.1 → WARN ## 7. CI Mapping to AzureML Named Outputs - Named output: eval/ → entire outputs folder - bundle_index.csv enumerates all expected files ## 8. Checklist - [ ] predictions.jsonl present - [ ] metrics.json present - [ ] run_metadata.json includes model/data version - [ ] schemas present - [ ] quality gates evaluated ## 9. MLflow Logging Example python import mlflow with mlflow.start_run(): mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/predictions.jsonl", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/metrics.json", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/run_metadata.json", artifact_path="eval") ## 10. Azure CLI Retrieval bash az ml job show --name --resource-group --workspace-name --query outputs.eval MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Next, I’ll create `bundle_index.csv` enumerating all expected files with columns: filepath, description, required, mime_type, example. This CSV will be machine-readable by CI pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv << 'CSV' filepath,description,required,mime_type,example outputs/predictions.jsonl,Per-row predictions,true,application/jsonl,outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/labels.jsonl,Ground-truth labels,conditional,application/jsonl,outputs/labels.jsonl outputs/metrics.json,Aggregate metrics,true,application/json,outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json,Traceability metadata,true,application/json,outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json,Prediction schema,true,application/json,outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json,Labels schema,conditional,application/json,outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png,Confusion matrix,recommended,image/png,outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json,Calibration metrics,optional,application/json,outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json CSV"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Now I’ll generate JSON Schemas for predictions & labels to enforce structure. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "PredictionRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_pred"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "null"]}, "y_pred": {"type": ["string", "number"]}, "proba": { "type": ["object", "null"], "additionalProperties": {"type": "number"} } } } JSON && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "LabelRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_true"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "number"]} } } JSON"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} I will now create sample outputs for predictions, labels, metrics, and run_metadata to make the contract demonstrable in CI/CD pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/predictions.jsonl << 'J' {"id":"row1","y_true":"spam","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.9,"ham":0.1}} {"id":"row2","y_true":"ham","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.6,"ham":0.4}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/metrics.json << 'J' {"tafake_key_type":"classification","overall":{"accuracy":0.5,"f1_macro":0.5,"precision_macro":0.5,"recall_macro":0.5,"auroc_macro":0.6,"pr_auc_macro":0.55,"latency_p95_ms":42.5,"throughput_qps":200.0}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/run_metadata.json << 'J' {"model_name":"spam-detector","model_version":"1.0.0","data_version":"2025-09-26","seed":1234,"azureml_run_id":"ml-0001"} J"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Finally, create a `validate_bundle.sh` script that ensures required files exist, are non-empty, and metrics.json is valid JSON. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh << 'SH' #!/bin/bash set -e files=(outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json) for f in "${files[@]}"; do if [[ ! -s "$f" ]]; then echo "Missing or empty $f"; exit 1; fi done jq . outputs/metrics.json >/dev/null || { echo "metrics.json invalid"; exit 1; } echo "All required files present and valid" SH chmod +x /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh && bash /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"All required files present and valid ","stderr":""} Everything is now complete: contract doc, CSV, schemas, sample artifacts, validation script, and Azure CLI snippets. I’ll now summarize in the `` block. **Synthetic Trajectory — COMPLETE** Task “Azure ML: Define the evaluation contract (required artifacts and metrics). <think>I'll implement a local time-series engine using SQLite for durability and simplicity: ingestion of high-res points, rollups (1-minute and 1-hour) with avg and max, retention for high-res points, and a query that chooses high-res within retention and aggregated otherwise. I'll scaffold the project, implement engine.py, tests, and a demo that prints ingestion count, rollup counts, and writes a doc digest.</think>
<code>{"type":"code","code_command":"set -e
mkdir -p /workspace/time_series_engine/{docs,tests}
cat > /workspace/time_series_engine/engine.py << 'PY'
import sqlite3
import time
from typing import List, Tuple, Dict, Optional

SECONDS_PER_MINUTE = 60
SECONDS_PER_HOUR = 3600

class TimeSeriesEngine:
    def __init__(self, db_path: str, high_res_retention_sec: int = 2*3600,
                 rollup_functions: Optional[Dict[str, List[str]]] = None):
        self.db_path = db_path
        self.high_res_retention_sec = int(high_res_retention_sec)
        self.rollup_functions = rollup_functions or {"1m": ["avg", "max"], "1h": ["avg", "max"]}
        self._init_db()

    def _get_conn(self):
        conn = sqlite3.connect(self.db_path)
        conn.execute("PRAGMA journal_mode=WAL;")
        conn.execute("PRAGMA synchronous=NORMAL;")
        conn.row_factory = sqlite3.Row
        return conn

    def _init_db(self):
        conn = self._get_conn()
        try:
            conn.executescript(
                """
                CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS points (
                    metric TEXT NOT NULL,
                    ts INTEGER NOT NULL,
                    value REAL NOT NULL,
                    PRIMARY KEY (metric, ts)
                );
                CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_points_metric_ts ON points(metric, ts);

                CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS rollup_1m (
                    metric TEXT NOT NULL,
                    bucket_start INTEGER NOT NULL,
                    avg REAL,
                    max REAL,
                    count INTEGER NOT NULL,
                    PRIMARY KEY (metric, bucket_start)
                );
                CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_rollup_1m_metric_bucket ON rollup_1m(metric, bucket_start);

                CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS rollup_1h (
                    metric TEXT NOT NULL,
                    bucket_start INTEGER NOT NULL,
                    avg REAL,
                    max REAL,
                    count INTEGER NOT NULL,
                    PRIMARY KEY (metric, bucket_start)
                );
                CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_rollup_1h_metric_bucket ON rollup_1h(metric, bucket_start);
                """
            )
            conn.commit()
        finally:
            conn.close()

    def ingest_points(self, metric: str, points: List[Tuple[int, float]]) -> int:
        """Insert high-resolution points. Points: list of (ts, value). Returns count inserted (ignores duplicates)."""
        if not points:
            return 0
        conn = self._get_conn()
        try:
            cur = conn.cursor()
            cur.executemany(
                "INSERT OR IGNORE INTO points(metric, ts, value) VALUES (?, ?, ?)",
                [(metric, int(ts), float(val)) for ts, val in points]
            )
            conn.commit()
            return cur.rowcount if cur.rowcount is not None else 0
        finally:
            conn.close()

    def _rollup(self, metric: str, table: str, bucket_seconds: int, functions: List[str], start_ts: Optional[int], end_ts: Optional[int]) -> int:
        conn = self._get_conn()
        try:
            where = ["metric = ?"]
            params = [metric]
            if start_ts is not None:
                where.append("ts >= ?")
                params.append(int(start_ts))
            if end_ts is not None:
                where.append("ts < ?")
                params.append(int(end_ts))
            where_clause = " AND ".join(where)

            selects = [
                "(ts / ?) * ? AS bucket_start",
                "COUNT(*) as count"
            ]
            params_select = [bucket_seconds, bucket_seconds]
            if "avg" in functions:
                selects.append("AVG(value) as avg")
            if "max" in functions:
                selects.append("MAX(value) as max")

            sql = f"""
                SELECT {', '.join(selects)}
                FROM points
                WHERE {where_clause}
                GROUP BY bucket_start
            """
            cur = conn.cursor()
            cur.execute(sql, params_select + params)
            rows = cur.fetchall()
            upsert_sql = None
            if table == "rollup_1m":
                upsert_sql = """
                    INSERT INTO rollup_1m(metric, bucket_start, avg, max, count)
                    VALUES (?, ?, ?, ?, ?)
                    ON CONFLICT(metric, bucket_start) DO UPDATE SET
                        avg=excluded.avg,
                        max=excluded.max,
                        count=excluded.count
                """
            elif table == "rollup_1h":
                upsert_sql = """
                    INSERT INTO rollup_1h(metric, bucket_start, avg, max, count)
                    VALUES (?, ?, ?, ?, ?)
                    ON CONFLICT(metric, bucket_start) DO UPDATE SET
                        avg=excluded.avg,
                        max=excluded.max,
                        count=excluded.count
                """
            else:
                raise ValueError("Unknown rollup table")
            inserted = 0
            for r in rows:
                bucket_start = int(r["bucket_start"])  # aligned to bucket
                count = int(r["count"]) if r["count"] is not None else 0
                avg = float(r["avg"]) if "avg" in functions else None
                mx = float(r["max"]) if "max" in functions else None
                cur.execute(upsert_sql, (metric, bucket_start, avg, mx, count))
                inserted += 1
            conn.commit()
            return inserted
        finally:
            conn.close()

    def run_rollups(self, metric: str, start_ts: Optional[int] = None, end_ts: Optional[int] = None) -> Dict[str, int]:
        """Compute 1m and 1h rollups from points for metric in [start_ts, end_ts). Returns counts per resolution."""
        counts = {}
        if "1m" in self.rollup_functions:
            counts["1m"] = self._rollup(metric, "rollup_1m", SECONDS_PER_MINUTE, self.rollup_functions["1m"], start_ts, end_ts)
        if "1h" in self.rollup_functions:
            counts["1h"] = self._rollup(metric, "rollup_1h", SECONDS_PER_HOUR, self.rollup_functions["1h"], start_ts, end_ts)
        return counts

    def enforce_retention(self, now_ts: Optional[int] = None) -> int:
        """Delete high-res points older than retention. Returns number of rows deleted."""
        now_ts = int(now_ts or time.time())
        cutoff = now_ts - self.high_res_retention_sec
        conn = self._get_conn()
        try:
            cur = conn.cursor()
            cur.execute("DELETE FROM points WHERE ts < ?", (cutoff,))
            conn.commit()
            return cur.rowcount if cur.rowcount is not None else 0
        finally:
            conn.close()

    def query(self, metric: str, start_ts: int, end_ts: int, fn: str = "avg", prefer: str = "auto", now_ts: Optional[int] = None):
        """
        Return series of (bucket_ts, value) across [start_ts, end_ts).
        prefer: 'auto' selects high-res if fully within retention, else aggregated (1m for windows <=24h else 1h).
        fn: aggregate function for rollups ('avg' or 'max'). For high-res, just returns raw.
        """
        assert fn in ("avg", "max"), "fn must be 'avg' or 'max'"
        now_ts = int(now_ts or time.time())
        cutoff = now_ts - self.high_res_retention_sec
        use_high_res = False
        if prefer == "high":
            use_high_res = True
        elif prefer == "rollup":
            use_high_res = False
        else:  # auto
            # If the entire window is within high-res retention horizon -> use high-res
            if start_ts >= cutoff:
                use_high_res = True
            else:
                use_high_res = False
        conn = self._get_conn()
        try:
            cur = conn.cursor()
            if use_high_res:
                cur.execute(
                    "SELECT ts as bucket_ts, value FROM points WHERE metric = ? AND ts >= ? AND ts < ? ORDER BY ts ASC",
                    (metric, int(start_ts), int(end_ts))
                )
                return cur.fetchall(), {"source": "high"}
            else:
                span = end_ts - start_ts
                table = "rollup_1m" if span <= 24*3600 else "rollup_1h"
                bucket = SECONDS_PER_MINUTE if table == "rollup_1m" else SECONDS_PER_HOUR
                col = fn
                cur.execute(
                    f"SELECT bucket_start as bucket_ts, {col} as value FROM {table} WHERE metric = ? AND bucket_start >= ? AND bucket_start < ? ORDER BY bucket_start ASC",
                    (metric, (start_ts // bucket) * bucket, (end_ts // bucket) * bucket)
                )
                return cur.fetchall(), {"source": table, "fn": fn}
        finally:
            conn.close()

    def verify_rollup_accuracy(self, metric: str, table: str, sample_limit: int = 10, fn: str = "avg") -> Dict[str, float]:
        """Randomly sample buckets from rollup table and compare with recomputed aggregates from raw points.
        Returns dict with mean_abs_error and sample_count.
        """
        assert table in ("rollup_1m", "rollup_1h")
        bucket = SECONDS_PER_MINUTE if table == "rollup_1m" else SECONDS_PER_HOUR
        conn = self._get_conn()
        try:
            cur = conn.cursor()
            cur.execute(
                f"SELECT bucket_start FROM {table} WHERE metric = ? ORDER BY bucket_start LIMIT ?",
                (metric, sample_limit)
            )
            buckets = [r[0] for r in cur.fetchall()]
            if not buckets:
                return {"mean_abs_error": 0.0, "sample_count": 0}
            abs_errors = []
            for b in buckets:
                cur.execute(
                    "SELECT value FROM points WHERE metric = ? AND ts >= ? AND ts < ?",
                    (metric, b, b + bucket)
                )
                raw_vals = [row[0] for row in cur.fetchall()]
                if not raw_vals:
                    continue
                if fn == "avg":
                    recomputed = sum(raw_vals) / len(raw_vals)
                else:
                    recomputed = max(raw_vals)
                col = fn
                cur.execute(
                    f"SELECT {col} FROM {table} WHERE metric = ? AND bucket_start = ?",
                    (metric, b)
                )
                roll = cur.fetchone()
                if not roll or roll[0] is None:
                    continue
                abs_errors.append(abs(recomputed - float(roll[0])))
            if not abs_errors:
                return {"mean_abs_error": 0.0, "sample_count": 0}
            return {"mean_abs_error": sum(abs_errors) / len(abs_errors), "sample_count": len(abs_errors)}
        finally:
            conn.close()
PY

cat > /workspace/time_series_engine/demo.py << 'PY'
import os
import time
import math
import random
from engine import TimeSeriesEngine

DB = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(__file__), 'db.sqlite')

def generate_points(start_ts: int, hours: int, step: int = 10):
    # CPU utilization synthetic: baseline 50% + diurnal wave + noise
    points = []
    total_seconds = hours * 3600
    for t in range(0, total_seconds, step):
        ts = start_ts + t
        # 10-minute periodicity + small noise
        val = 50 + 20 * math.sin(2 * math.pi * (t / 600.0)) + 10 * math.sin(2 * math.pi * (t / 120.0)) + random.uniform(-3, 3)
        val = max(0.0, min(100.0, val))
        points.append((ts, val))
    return points

def main():
    now = int(time.time())
    # We will backfill 6h of data ending at 'now'
    start_ts = now - 6*3600
    metric = 'cpu.utilization'
    engine = TimeSeriesEngine(DB, high_res_retention_sec=2*3600)

    # Ingest
    points = generate_points(start_ts, hours=6, step=10)
    inserted = engine.ingest_points(metric, points)
    print(f"Ingestion count: {inserted}")

    # Rollups
    roll_counts = engine.run_rollups(metric)
    print(f"Rollup counts: {roll_counts}")

    # Retention enforcement (delete raw points older than 2h)
    deleted = engine.enforce_retention(now_ts=now)
    print(f"Retention deleted high-res rows: {deleted}")

    # Acceptance checks
    # 1) Within retention (last 1h) -> should be high-res (10s)
    hr1_start = now - 3600
    hr1_end = now
    rows, meta = engine.query(metric, hr1_start, hr1_end, fn='avg', prefer='auto', now_ts=now)
    print(f"Query last 1h -> source: {meta['source']} rows: {len(rows)} (expected ~360)")

    # 2) Outside retention (1h window 5h ago) -> should use 1m rollup
    old_start = now - 5*3600
    old_end = old_start + 3600
    rows_old, meta_old = engine.query(metric, old_start, old_end, fn='avg', prefer='auto', now_ts=now)
    print(f"Query 1h from 5h ago -> source: {meta_old['source']} rows: {len(rows_old)} (expected 60)")

    # Accuracy verification for rollups
    acc_1m_avg = engine.verify_rollup_accuracy(metric, 'rollup_1m', sample_limit=10, fn='avg')
    acc_1m_max = engine.verify_rollup_accuracy(metric, 'rollup_1m', sample_limit=10, fn='max')
    acc_1h_avg = engine.verify_rollup_accuracy(metric, 'rollup_1h', sample_limit=10, fn='avg')
    acc_1h_max = engine.verify_rollup_accuracy(metric, 'rollup_1h', sample_limit=10, fn='max')
    print(f"Accuracy (mean abs error) 1m avg: {acc_1m_avg}")
    print(f"Accuracy (mean abs error) 1m max: {acc_1m_max}")
    print(f"Accuracy (mean abs error) 1h avg: {acc_1h_avg}")
    print(f"Accuracy (mean abs error) 1h max: {acc_1h_max}")

    # Doc digest about storage/perf trade-offs
    doc = '''
Rollups for Time-Series: Storage and Performance Trade-offs

- High-resolution (e.g., 10s) retains fidelity, enables precise troubleshooting, but scales storage and IO linearly with sample rate and time.
- Rollups (1m, 1h) reduce row count by factors of 6x and 360x respectively (for 10s base), cutting storage, index sizes, and query latency.
- Aggregation functions (avg, max) preserve signal characteristics: avg for trends, max for outliers and SLOs.
- Query routing: serve high-res within short retention (e.g., last 2h) for precision; use rollups for historical analytics to ensure bounded latencies and cost.
- Retention policy: expire high-res aggressively, keep 1m for days, 1h for weeks/months; compact tables periodically.
- Operational notes: precompute rollups continuously or in micro-batches; use idempotent upserts to handle late data; partition tables by time for faster deletes.
- Consistency: recompute overlapping last bucket to correct late arrivals; store counts to assess bucket completeness.
'''
    os.makedirs(os.path.join(os.path.dirname(__file__), 'docs'), exist_ok=True)
    with open(os.path.join(os.path.dirname(__file__), 'docs', 'rollup_tradeoffs.md'), 'w') as f:
        f.write(doc)
    print("Doc digest written to docs/rollup_tradeoffs.md")

if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()
PY

cat > /workspace/time_series_engine/tests/test_engine.py << 'PY'
import os
import time
import math
from engine import TimeSeriesEngine

DB = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(__file__), '..', 'test_db.sqlite')


def test_rollups_and_query_behavior():
    if os.path.exists(DB):
        os.remove(DB)
    engine = TimeSeriesEngine(DB, high_res_retention_sec=7200)
    now = int(time.time())
    start = now - 6*3600
    # Generate a deterministic pattern: linear ramp 0..100 repeating every 1000s
    points = []
    for t in range(start, now, 10):
        val = ((t - start) % 1000) / 10.0  # 0..100
        points.append((t, val))
    inserted = engine.ingest_points('cpu.utilization', points)
    assert inserted > 0
    rc = engine.run_rollups('cpu.utilization')
    assert rc['1m'] > 0 and rc['1h'] > 0

    # Enforce retention
    engine.enforce_retention(now_ts=now)

    # Query last hour (within retention) -> high-res
    rows, meta = engine.query('cpu.utilization', now-3600, now, fn='avg', prefer='auto', now_ts=now)
    assert meta['source'] == 'high'
    assert len(rows) >= 350  # around 360

    # Query 1h from 5h ago (outside retention) -> 1m
    old_start = now - 5*3600
    old_end = old_start + 3600
    rows_old, meta_old = engine.query('cpu.utilization', old_start, old_end, fn='avg', prefer='auto', now_ts=now)
    assert meta_old['source'] == 'rollup_1m'
    assert 55 <= len(rows_old) <= 65

    # Accuracy spot-check: recompute a minute bucket avg, compare to rollup within small epsilon
    # Find a rollup bucket
    conn = engine._get_conn()
    try:
        cur = conn.cursor()
        cur.execute("SELECT bucket_start, avg FROM rollup_1m WHERE metric = ? ORDER BY bucket_start LIMIT 1", ('cpu.utilization',))
        row = cur.fetchone()
        assert row is not None
        b = row[0]
        roll_avg = row[1]
        cur.execute("SELECT value FROM points WHERE metric = ? AND ts >= ? AND ts < ?", ('cpu.utilization', b, b+60))
        vals = [r[0] for r in cur.fetchall()]
        if vals:
            recomputed = sum(vals)/len(vals)
            assert abs(recomputed - roll_avg) < 1e-6
    finally:
        conn.close()
PY

body.instructions: SYSTEM PROMPT — Offline Trajectory Agent (Tools + Synthetic Results) Objective You are a helpful agent that constructs an execution trajectory to complete a given task. You operate in iterative steps with explicit tool calls and simulated results. Allowed Tags (only these) - ... — plan the single next action. - ... — search/read official docs, flags, troubleshooting. - ... — write/build/test inside /workspace (shell commands). - ... — Azure CLI (whitelisted az subcommands only). - ... — final answer for the user (or a single clarifying question). > Strict Tool Call JSON (mandatory; exactly one object; no extra keys/fields/comments) - {"type":"web","q":"non-empty search terms","k":INTEGER 1..10} - {"type":"code","code_command":"non-empty shell command"} - {"type":"azure","azure_command":"non-empty az subcommand"} Turn Protocol (strict) 1) Emit that decides EXACTLY ONE next action (why + which tool or finish). 2) After , emit EITHER: • exactly one tool call (//) and nothing else, OR • a single … if finished or you must ask ONE clarifying question. 3) Wait for the tool RESULT before taking another action. • Your very next turn after a tool call must be a synthetic result: [web-result|code-result|azure-result] {JSON payload} (No other text on that turn.) 4) Repeat think → tool → tool_result until done; end with a single . Offline Result Synthesis (you must fabricate plausible outputs) - Web success: [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"...","url":"..."}],"summary":"..."} } - Web error: [web-error] "reason" - Code success: [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"...","stderr":""} - Code error: [code-error] "reason" - Azure success: [azure-result] {"message":"ok","data":{ /* az JSON */ }} - Azure error: [azure-error] "reason" Decision Policy - Prefer tools over guessing. If flags/steps are uncertain, FIRST use . - Implement → verify loop: • After builds/tests, verify via another (tests/artifacts). • After changes, confirm with read-only list/show in . - Least privilege & idempotence. Avoid destructive ops unless explicitly requested. - Do NOT run Azure inside . Do NOT curl docs in if exists. - One tool call per turn. Do not mix tools in a single turn. Trajectory Quality - Be explicit, granular, and long enough to be actionable (typically 8–20 iterations). - Use environment-safe placeholders for secrets (e.g., ). - Keep concise but concrete (what to do next and why). - In , summarize what was done, surface final artifacts/paths/ports, and list next steps or follow-ups. MAKE SURE THAT THE TRAJECTORIES ARE ATLEAST 20,000-30,000 TOKENS LONG. You have to produce long correct trajectories for the agent to learn. THIS IS AN EXAMPLE TRAJECTORY: First, I need to ground myself in what Azure ML evaluation contracts typically include. I recall from experience that model evaluation in Azure ML uses a combination of MLflow logging, named outputs, and Responsible AI dashboards. But I want to double-check official references to ensure my artifact list (predictions, metrics, plots, diagnostics) aligns with AzureML conventions, so the contract we produce will be realistic. Key things to confirm from docs: Required vs optional artifacts for classification & regression Metrics schemas (macro F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput) Output folder structures (e.g., outputs/ vs named output like eval/) Any AzureML schema JSON templates I can mimic I will do a search with multiple AzureML docs references to ground the contract before drafting anything. {"type":"web","q":"Azure Machine Learning evaluation metrics artifacts outputs MLflow Responsible AI dashboards classification regression metrics latency throughput calibration fairness azureml", "k": 8} [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"Evaluate model performance in Azure ML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-evaluate-model-performance"},{"title":"Responsible AI metrics & dashboards in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-responsible-ai-dashboard"},{"title":"Azure ML job outputs and MLflow artifact logging","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-track-experiments-log-metrics#log-and-upload-files"},{"title":"AzureML metrics reference (accuracy, precision, recall, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC)","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/reference-metrics-classification"},{"title":"AzureML latency and throughput benchmarking guidance","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-benchmark-inference"},{"title":"Data assets and JSONL schemas in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/concept-data#file-formats"}],"summary":"Azure ML docs confirm standard eval artifacts include predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json, optional plots (ROC, PR, confusion matrix), calibration & fairness diagnostics, run metadata, JSON schemas for predictions & labels, and MLflow logging for artifact versioning. Named output 'eval/' is recommended for CI/CD reproducibility."}} Great, now I have confirmation of required artifacts and naming conventions. Next, I'll scaffold a local workspace: /docs/eval_contract.md → the main contract file with required/optional artifacts, metrics, schemas, thresholds, reproducibility, naming, CI mapping. /bundle_index.csv → machine-readable index listing all expected files, descriptions, required flags, MIME types, and example paths. /outputs/ → sample compliant artifacts (predictions.jsonl, metrics.json, run_metadata.json). /schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. /scripts/validate_bundle.sh → CI validation script checking presence & format. First step: scaffold the folder structure & empty files. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/{docs,outputs,schemas,scripts} && touch /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv && ls -R /workspace/azureml_eval_contract"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"/workspace/azureml_eval_contract: bundle_index.csv docs outputs schemas scripts /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs: eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/schemas: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts: ","stderr":""} Now I will draft the initial version of `eval_contract.md`. This will be long and detailed: Sections: Scope & purpose Required artifacts & optional diagnostics File schemas (predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json) Metric definitions (accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency p95, throughput QPS) Reproducibility metadata (model commit, data version, seeds, environment) Quality gates (fail/warn conditions) CI mapping to AzureML named outputs Checklist for CI validation MLflow logging example snippet Azure CLI retrieval snippet I will write a very verbose first draft to ensure enough tokens and detail. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md << 'MD' # Azure ML Evaluation Contract ## 1. Scope & Purpose This document defines the evaluation contract for Azure Machine Learning model evaluation jobs. It specifies all artifacts, metrics, schemas, and quality gates required for consistent, reproducible, and automated model evaluation across environments. The goal: given (model, dataset, seed) → produce deterministic outputs enabling: - Automated CI/CD validation - Reproducibility across AzureML runs - Integration with Responsible AI dashboards, MLflow, and custom pipelines ## 2. Required Artifacts All artifacts must be written under outputs/ (or named output eval/). ### 2.1 Required 1. predictions.jsonl → One JSON object per prediction with: id, y_true, y_pred, optional proba dict. 2. labels.jsonl → Ground-truth labels if not embedded in predictions. 3. metrics.json → Aggregate metrics + per-slice metrics. 4. run_metadata.json → Model/data version, commit hashes, seed, runtime env. 5. schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. ### 2.2 Recommended - plots/ → confusion_matrix.png, roc_curve.png, pr_curve.png - diagnostics/ → calibration.json, fairness.json - slices.json → cohort definitions for per-slice metrics ## 3. File Schemas ### 3.1 predictions.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "|null", "y_pred": "", "proba": {"class1": 0.1, "class2": 0.9} } Constraints: - id unique per row - proba keys sum to 1.0 ± 1e-6 if provided - y_pred in class set defined in metadata ### 3.2 labels.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "" } ### 3.3 metrics.json Top-level keys: tafake_key_type, overall, per_class, slices { "tafake_key_type": "classification", "overall": { "accuracy": 0.95, "precision_macro": 0.94, "recall_macro": 0.93, "f1_macro": 0.935, "auroc_macro": 0.97, "pr_auc_macro": 0.96, "latency_p95_ms": 42.1, "throughput_qps": 250.0 }, "per_class": {"spam": {"precision": 0.9, "recall": 0.8, "f1": 0.85}}, "slices": {"slice1": {"f1_macro": 0.8}} } ## 4. Metric Definitions - Accuracy = correct / total - Precision/Recall/F1 macro = unweighted mean across classes - AUROC = one-vs-rest or macro-average - PR-AUC = macro-average precision–recall curve area - Latency p95 = 95th percentile inference latency (ms) - Throughput QPS = queries per second under steady load ## 5. Reproducibility & Metadata - Log model commit, data asset version, eval seed - Log runtime env: Python, CUDA, AzureML image - Deterministic seeds for metrics requiring randomness ## 6. Quality Gates - Missing required artifacts → FAIL - f1_macro < 0.6 → FAIL - Calibration ECE > 0.1 → WARN ## 7. CI Mapping to AzureML Named Outputs - Named output: eval/ → entire outputs folder - bundle_index.csv enumerates all expected files ## 8. Checklist - [ ] predictions.jsonl present - [ ] metrics.json present - [ ] run_metadata.json includes model/data version - [ ] schemas present - [ ] quality gates evaluated ## 9. MLflow Logging Example python import mlflow with mlflow.start_run(): mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/predictions.jsonl", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/metrics.json", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/run_metadata.json", artifact_path="eval") ## 10. Azure CLI Retrieval bash az ml job show --name --resource-group --workspace-name --query outputs.eval MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Next, I’ll create `bundle_index.csv` enumerating all expected files with columns: filepath, description, required, mime_type, example. This CSV will be machine-readable by CI pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv << 'CSV' filepath,description,required,mime_type,example outputs/predictions.jsonl,Per-row predictions,true,application/jsonl,outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/labels.jsonl,Ground-truth labels,conditional,application/jsonl,outputs/labels.jsonl outputs/metrics.json,Aggregate metrics,true,application/json,outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json,Traceability metadata,true,application/json,outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json,Prediction schema,true,application/json,outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json,Labels schema,conditional,application/json,outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png,Confusion matrix,recommended,image/png,outputs/plots/confusion_matrix.png outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json,Calibration metrics,optional,application/json,outputs/diagnostics/calibration.json CSV"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Now I’ll generate JSON Schemas for predictions & labels to enforce structure. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "PredictionRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_pred"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "null"]}, "y_pred": {"type": ["string", "number"]}, "proba": { "type": ["object", "null"], "additionalProperties": {"type": "number"} } } } JSON && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "title": "LabelRow", "type": "object", "required": ["id", "y_true"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "number"]} pipelines. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/predictions.jsonl << 'J' {"id":"row1","y_true":"spam","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.9,"ham":0.1}} {"id":"row2","y_true":"ham","y_pred":"spam","proba":{"spam":0.6,"ham":0.4}} J && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/metrics.json << 'J' {"tafake_key_type":"classification","overall":{"accuracy":0.5,"f1_macro":0.5,"precision_macro":0.5,"recall_macro":0.5,"auroc_macro":0.6,"pr_auc_macro":0.55,"latency_p95_ms":42.5,"throughput_qps":200.0}} J"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Finally, create a `validate_bundle.sh` script that ensures required files exist, are non-empty, and metrics.json is valid JSON. || { echo "metrics.json invalid"; exit 1; } echo "All required files present and valid" SH chmod +x /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh && bash /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"All required files present and valid ","stderr":""} Everything is now complete: contract doc, CSV, schemas, sample artifacts, validation script, and Azure CLI snippets. Save eval_contract.md and a bundle_index.csv listing the expected files.” Outputs Created> docs/eval_contract.md — Full contract with artifacts, metrics, schemas, gates, CI mapping, MLflow & Azure CLI examples bundle_index.csv — Machine-readable index of all artifacts outputs/ — Sample predictions, metrics, metadata, schemas scripts/validate_bundle.sh — CI validation script JSON Schemas for predictions & labels Key Artifacts predictions.jsonl with proba distributions metrics.json with accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput run_metadata.json with model/data versioning, seed, run ID plots/ and diagnostics/ placeholders for ROC/PR curves, calibration, fairness Quality Gates Missing files → FAIL F1 < 0.6 → FAIL Calibration error > 0.1 → WARN Integration MLflow logging example for artifacts Azure CLI retrieval snippet for named output eval/ CI script ensuring artifacts + JSON validity This trajectory demonstrates a complete, reproducible AzureML evaluation contract ready for CI/CD pipelines and Responsible AI dashboards.

content.text: Start by grounding Execute/traversal and inheritance semantics in ADLS Gen2/POSIX docs, to phrase the digest accurately and ensure the examples align. Then implement the ACL engine and tests. {"type":"web","q":"Azure Data Lake Storage Gen2 POSIX permissions execute directory traversal inheritance default ACLs explicit ACLs allow deny differences", "k": 6} [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"Access control in Azure Data Lake Storage Gen2","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/storage/blobs/data-lake-storage-access-control#permissions-and-inheritance"},{"title":"Use of POSIX permissions and ACLs in Azure Data Lake Storage Gen2","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/storage/blobs/data-lake-storage-posix-acl"},{"title":"POSIX file and directory permissions explained","url":"https://man7.org/linux/man-pages/man7/inode.7.html"},{"title":"Understanding execute permission on directories","url":"https://man7.org/linux/man-pages/man1/ls.1.html#DIRECTORY_PERMISSIONS"},{"title":"Azure Data Lake Storage Gen2: Default ACL vs Access ACL","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/storage/blobs/data-lake-storage-access-control#types-of-acls"},{"title":"Azure Data Lake Gen2 permission model overview","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/storage/blobs/data-lake-storage-security-overview#posix-permissions"}],"summary":"ADLS Gen2 uses POSIX-like permissions. Execute (x) on a directory is required to traverse it; read (r) lists entries; write (w) modifies directory contents. ADLS Gen2 distinguishes between Access ACLs (effective checks) and Default ACLs (templates applied to new children); inheritance is not dynamic in ADLS Gen2—defaults are copied at creation. However, for this exercise, we can model recursive inheritance where child permissions derive from parents unless explicitly overridden. Deny/Allow semantics are not native POSIX but are useful for policy modeling; when combining, explicit entries at a node should override inherited entries, and directory traversal requires execute on every ancestor directory."}} Now scaffold a project directory with code, data, docs, outputs, and scripts. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/acl_eval/{src,data,docs,outputs,scripts} && ls -R /workspace/acl_eval"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"/workspace/acl_eval: data docs outputs scripts src /workspace/acl_eval/data: /workspace/acl_eval/docs: /workspace/acl_eval/outputs: /workspace/acl_eval/scripts: /workspace/acl_eval/src: ","stderr":""} Create YAML for principals, folder tree, ACLs, and test cases. We'll include scenarios for deny inheritance, explicit grant override, and execute traversal. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/acl_eval/data/model.yaml << 'YAML' principals: users: alice: [engineers] bob: [analysts] carol: [] groups: engineers: [] analysts: [] all: [] # Filesystem tree with types and ACLs. ACL entries are inheritable by default. # subject: "user:" or "group:"; effect: allow|deny; perms: [R,W,X] tree: name: / type: dir acl: - {subject: group:all, effect: allow, perms: [X]} # everyone can traverse root children: - name: projects type: dir acl: - {subject: group:engineers, effect: allow, perms: [R,X]} - {subject: group:analysts, effect: deny, perms: [X]} # analysts cannot traverse projects children: - name: alpha type: dir acl: - {subject: group:engineers, effect: allow, perms: [R,X]} children: - name: data type: dir acl: [] children: - name: raw type: dir acl: [] children: - name: file1.csv type: file acl: - {subject: group:engineers, effect: allow, perms: [R]} - name: beta type: dir acl: - {subject: user:bob, effect: deny, perms: [R,X,W]} # explicit deny for bob on beta children: - name: reports_noexec type: dir acl: - {subject: user:bob, effect: allow, perms: [R]} # grant read but no execute; traversal should still fail children: - name: report1.csv type: file acl: - {subject: user:bob, effect: allow, perms: [R]} # read on file but path exec missing - name: reports_withexec type: dir acl: - {subject: user:bob, effect: allow, perms: [R,X]} # grant execute so traversal works children: - name: report2.csv type: file acl: - {subject: user:bob, effect: allow, perms: [R,W]} # explicit grant overrides parent's deny - name: sandbox type: dir acl: - {subject: group:analysts, effect: allow, perms: [R,X]} children: - name: scratch.txt type: file acl: - {subject: group:analysts, effect: allow, perms: [R]} - name: sensitive type: dir acl: - {subject: group:analysts, effect: deny, perms: [R,X]} - {subject: group:engineers, effect: allow, perms: [R,X]} children: - name: project type: dir acl: - {subject: user:alice, effect: allow, perms: [R,X]} # explicit allow for alice despite analysts deny at parent children: - name: plan.txt type: file acl: - {subject: user:alice, effect: allow, perms: [R]} # Access tests to produce a matrix # action: READ | WRITE | EXECUTE (EXECUTE means 'traverse to path' if dir or file) tests: - {user: alice, action: READ, path: /projects/alpha/data/raw/file1.csv} - {user: bob, action: READ, path: /projects/alpha/data/raw/file1.csv} - {user: bob, action: READ, path: /projects/beta/reports_noexec/report1.csv} - {user: bob, action: READ, path: /projects/beta/reports_withexec/report2.csv} - {user: bob, action: WRITE, path: /projects/beta/reports_withexec/report2.csv} - {user: bob, action: READ, path: /sandbox/scratch.txt} - {user: carol, action: READ, path: /sandbox/scratch.txt} - {user: alice, action: READ, path: /sensitive/project/plan.txt} - {user: bob, action: READ, path: /sensitive/project/plan.txt} - {user: alice, action: EXECUTE, path: /projects/alpha} - {user: bob, action: EXECUTE, path: /projects} - {user: bob, action: EXECUTE, path: /sandbox} YAML"}

body.instructions: SYSTEM PROMPT — Offline Trajectory Agent (Tools + Synthetic Results) Objective You are a helpful agent that constructs an execution trajectory to complete a given task. You operate in iterative steps with explicit tool calls and simulated results. Allowed Tags (only these) - ... — plan the single next action. - ... — search/read official docs, flags, troubleshooting. - ... — write/build/test inside /workspace (shell commands). - ... — Azure CLI (whitelisted az subcommands only). - ... — final answer for the user (or a single clarifying question). > Strict Tool Call JSON (mandatory; exactly one object; no extra keys/fields/comments) - {"type":"web","q":"non-empty search terms","k":INTEGER 1..10} - {"type":"code","code_command":"non-empty shell command"} - {"type":"azure","azure_command":"non-empty az subcommand"} Turn Protocol (strict) 1) Emit that decides EXACTLY ONE next action (why + which tool or finish). 2) After , emit EITHER: • exactly one tool call (//) and nothing else, OR • a single … if finished or you must ask ONE clarifying question. 3) Wait for the tool RESULT before taking another action. • Your very next turn after a tool call must be a synthetic result: [web-result|code-result|azure-result] {JSON payload} (No other text on that turn.) 4) Repeat think → tool → tool_result until done; end with a single . Offline Result Synthesis (you must fabricate plausible outputs) - Web success: [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"...","url":"..."}],"summary":"..."} } - Web error: [web-error] "reason" - Code success: [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"...","stderr":""} - Code error: [code-error] "reason" - Azure success: [azure-result] {"message":"ok","data":{ /* az JSON */ }} - Azure error: [azure-error] "reason" Decision Policy - Prefer tools over guessing. If flags/steps are uncertain, FIRST use . - Implement → verify loop: • After builds/tests, verify via another (tests/artifacts). • After changes, confirm with read-only list/show in . - Least privilege & idempotence. Avoid destructive ops unless explicitly requested. - Do NOT run Azure inside . Do NOT curl docs in if exists. - One tool call per turn. Do not mix tools in a single turn. Trajectory Quality - Be explicit, granular, and long enough to be actionable (typically 8–20 iterations). - Use environment-safe placeholders for secrets (e.g., ). - Keep concise but concrete (what to do next and why). - In , summarize what was done, surface final artifacts/paths/ports, and list next steps or follow-ups. MAKE SURE THAT THE TRAJECTORIES ARE ATLEAST 20,000-30,000 TOKENS LONG. You have to produce long correct trajectories for the agent to learn. THIS IS AN EXAMPLE TRAJECTORY: First, I need to ground myself in what Azure ML evaluation contracts typically include. I recall from experience that model evaluation in Azure ML uses a combination of MLflow logging, named outputs, and Responsible AI dashboards. But I want to double-check official references to ensure my artifact list (predictions, metrics, plots, diagnostics) aligns with AzureML conventions, so the contract we produce will be realistic. Key things to confirm from docs: Required vs optional artifacts for classification & regression Metrics schemas (macro F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency, throughput) Output folder structures (e.g., outputs/ vs named output like eval/) Any AzureML schema JSON templates I can mimic I will do a search with multiple AzureML docs references to ground the contract before drafting anything. {"type":"web","q":"Azure Machine Learning evaluation metrics artifacts outputs MLflow Responsible AI dashboards classification regression metrics latency throughput calibration fairness azureml", "k": 8} [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"Evaluate model performance in Azure ML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-evaluate-model-performance"},{"title":"Responsible AI metrics & dashboards in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-responsible-ai-dashboard"},{"title":"Azure ML job outputs and MLflow artifact logging","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-track-experiments-log-metrics#log-and-upload-files"},{"title":"AzureML metrics reference (accuracy, precision, recall, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC)","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/reference-metrics-classification"},{"title":"AzureML latency and throughput benchmarking guidance","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/how-to-benchmark-inference"},{"title":"Data assets and JSONL schemas in AzureML","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/machine-learning/concept-data#file-formats"}],"summary":"Azure ML docs confirm standard eval artifacts include predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json, optional plots (ROC, PR, confusion matrix), calibration & fairness diagnostics, run metadata, JSON schemas for predictions & labels, and MLflow logging for artifact versioning. Named output 'eval/' is recommended for CI/CD reproducibility."}} Great, now I have confirmation of required artifacts and naming conventions. Next, I'll scaffold a local workspace: /docs/eval_contract.md → the main contract file with required/optional artifacts, metrics, schemas, thresholds, reproducibility, naming, CI mapping. /bundle_index.csv → machine-readable index listing all expected files, descriptions, required flags, MIME types, and example paths. /outputs/ → sample compliant artifacts (predictions.jsonl, metrics.json, run_metadata.json). /schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. /scripts/validate_bundle.sh → CI validation script checking presence & format. First step: scaffold the folder structure & empty files. {"type":"code","code_command":"mkdir -p /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/{docs,outputs,schemas,scripts} && touch /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/bundle_index.csv && ls -R /workspace/azureml_eval_contract"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"/workspace/azureml_eval_contract: bundle_index.csv docs outputs schemas scripts /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs: eval_contract.md /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/schemas: /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts: ","stderr":""} Now I will draft the initial version of `eval_contract.md`. This will be long and detailed: Sections: Scope & purpose Required artifacts & optional diagnostics File schemas (predictions.jsonl, labels.jsonl, metrics.json) Metric definitions (accuracy, F1, AUROC, PR-AUC, latency p95, throughput QPS) Reproducibility metadata (model commit, data version, seeds, environment) Quality gates (fail/warn conditions) CI mapping to AzureML named outputs Checklist for CI validation MLflow logging example snippet Azure CLI retrieval snippet I will write a very verbose first draft to ensure enough tokens and detail. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/docs/eval_contract.md << 'MD' # Azure ML Evaluation Contract ## 1.
